title: Helix Health - Private installer upgrade blocker triage (stuck at migrations)
preview: Helix Health reported a blocked Redwood Private upgrade where the installer progresses but hangs/fails at the database migrations step. Redwood and Helix walked through what “stuck at migrations” typically means (migrations job pending/crashlooping, database connectivity, schema lock, or resource constraints), aligned on a safe diagnostics bundle to collect, and agreed on a conservative rollback plan if forward-fix cannot be validated quickly. The conversation stayed focused on minimizing risk for a compliance-heavy environment and keeping Support/Engineering escalation paths clean (SUP-1845).

The conversation stayed focused on minimizing risk for a compliance-heavy environment and keeping Support/Engineering escalation paths clean (SUP-1845).

title: Containment warroom handoff and stability roadmap - Crestline Logistics
preview: Short bridge to hand back to ops: we reviewed the immediate containment steps taken by Crestline, walked through Redwood's preliminary RCA (likely KV cache thrash + hot-path batching misalignment), agreed on short-term mitigations (rate‑limit and fallback routing), and committed to a joint load test, runbook handoff, and a canary rollout plan. Security/scoping questions (audit logs, KMS) to be answered via follow-up doc.

title: 2026-01-17 Vision Labs Expedition and Squad Challenges
preview: Leadership reviewed the 2026 strategic 'beacon' to align squads around a small number of directional experiments, discussed headcount pacing and tight fiscal guardrails, walked through recent reliability incidents and mitigations, and opened the floor to squad-level challenges and lightning demos. Action items around experiment charters, hiring approvals, and a post-mortem schedule were agreed.

title: Controlled Failover Game Day Briefing (prod) - us-east to eu-west
preview: Pre-brief for the production controlled regional failover (us-east -> eu-west). Covered run-of-show, go/no-go gates, signal dashboards to watch, comms plan (internal + support + status page), and explicit rollback triggers. Confirmed limited blast radius (selected routes/tenants), dry-run output expectations, and who has authority to approve execution/rollback.

title: Regional failover automation upgrade - kickoff
preview: Kickoff for the Reliability Program workstream to upgrade regional failover automation across Hosted API and Dedicated. Team aligned on problem statement (manual/partial automation, noisy triggers, slow detection, hard to validate), in-scope components (signal pipeline, region health aggregation, controller + deterministic policy evaluation, guardrails/rollback, observability, runbooks, game days) and out-of-scope items (multi-cloud migration, serving runtime rewrite, SLA language). Open questions focused on trigger hierarchy (authoritative signals vs corroboration), anti-flap behavior, approval model (auto vs ack-required), and how to safely run controlled production failovers with limited blast radius.

title: Stoneharbor Finance — security & KMS hosting scout
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2026-01-08 15:30 UTC
Duration: 48 minutes (approx)
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Ava Reynolds (Stoneharbor Head of Infra), Samir Patel (Stoneharbor Security Architect), Liam Brooks (CTO)

Summary:
Quick security posture discovery focused on hosting options (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private), KMS / BYOK, SAML/SSO integration, RBAC and audit log retention. Customer is a regulated financial services company assessing whether Redwood can fit into their PCI/GLBA-adjacent environment with minimal scope creep.

Topics:
- SOC2/ISO artifacts and scope
- SSO/SAML integration with Okta
- RBAC model and least privilege
- Audit logs: retention, format, access controls
- Encryption: in-transit, at-rest, BYOK/HSM options
- Data residency and logs PII handling
- Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private tradeoffs for scope and ops

Action items (recorded here inline): see metadata above

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya Chen: Hi everyone, thanks for joining, I'm Maya from Redwood, I'm the AE on this account. We also have Ethan, our solutions engineer. How are you folks doing?
[00:14] Ava Reynolds: good thanks, we are, um, excited to hear. We looped in Samir and Liam because this will touch security and infra.
[00:20] Liam Brooks: hey all, quick note, we're mainly concerned about reducing PCI scope and not expanding it with logs or external tokens, so that's front and center.
[00:30] Ethan Park: great, that's helpful. We'll frame things around minimizing your compliance surface. quick agenda: SOC2/ISO artifacts, auth (SSO/SAML), RBAC, encryption/KMS, audit logs and retention, and hosting choices. sound good?
[00:45] Samir Patel: yep, please — and we want specifics: do you support BYOK? can we bring our HSM? how are audit logs stored and who can access them.
[00:58] Maya Chen: absolutely. to start high-level, Redwood supports three primary deployment modes: Hosted (our managed API), Dedicated (reserved capacity/backing GPU pools for you only), and Private (VPC or on-prem private control plane). each has different operational boundaries for compliance.
[01:20] Samir Patel: so Hosted means logs and metadata are in your managed environment right? that tends to bring our PCI scope up a ton.
[01:28] Ethan Park: correct, Hosted is our multi-tenant control plane with strong tenancy boundaries, encryption at rest and in transit, and SOC2 controls. That said, we typically see customers with strict compliance needs go Dedicated or Private.
[01:44] Ava Reynolds: for Private, can we run the control plane on our AWS accounts? we need VPC peering and to control KMS keys.
[01:52] Maya Chen: yes, Private can be a customer-managed control plane in your VPC. We support VPC peering or AWS PrivateLink patterns; you can bring your own KMS — BYOK — and configure keys in your account, or we can integrate with a customer HSM via standard KMIP if you choose an on-prem package.
[02:10] Samir Patel: KMIP, ok. How mature is that integration? any customers doing full HSM?
[02:16] Ethan Park: a few financial customers are on BYOK with AWS KMS CMKs and one has an HSM-backed flow. The integration for HSM is more of an on-prem/out-of-band setup, but we have docs and an onboarding checklist.
[02:30] Liam Brooks: can you describe what data stays in our account versus what Redwood will still see in Private mode?
[02:36] Maya Chen: short answer: in Private we minimize data in Redwood control plane; model artifacts and sometimes binaries are pushed but you control keys and network. logs can be forwarded into your SIEM, and we can disable or encrypt logs at the control plane level to reduce what's persisted centrally. We can also configure redaction rules for PII inside logs.
[02:58] Samir Patel: audit logs, format? do they include prompt text? we've got to guarantee prompt data doesn't leak into long-term logs without encryption or truncation.
[03:08] Ethan Park: good question. By default, request traces include metadata, duration, model used, and a hashed/pseudonymized prompt id. Full prompt capture is optional; many customers turn that off for sensitive workloads. If you need prompt storage for debugging, we recommend short-lived retention and encrypting with your CMK.
[03:29] Ava Reynolds: so you can encrypt the logs with our CMK and set retention of, say, 30 days, to keep us out of long-term scope?
[03:36] Maya Chen: yes, that's a common pattern. We offer configurable retention windows and can set up automated deletes. For Dedicated and Private, you can also set retention to zero on the control plane side and keep logs in your SIEM only.
[03:49] Samir Patel: SAML/SSO: we run Okta. what's the SSO setup look like and is there SCIM for provisioning?
[03:57] Ethan Park: we support SAML 2.0 and OIDC. Okta is widely used by our customers. SCIM provisioning is supported for user lifecycle and groups, and we map SCIM groups to RBAC roles. There are pre-built SSO templates in our console to speed setup.
[04:14] Liam Brooks: about RBAC, can we define custom roles and limit access to tokens and logs? we want strict separation: devs can call the API but not access logs or key material.
[04:25] Maya Chen: yes, our RBAC model has built-in roles (admin, developer, auditor) and supports custom roles and fine-grained permissions. You can separate 'invoke' permissions from 'view logs' and 'manage keys'. For Private we can integrate with your SAML groups so role membership aligns with Okta.
[04:43] Samir Patel: do audit logs capture RBAC changes, who rotated keys, who created service accounts?
[04:50] Ethan Park: yes, audit trails include admin actions: role changes, key rotations, service account creation, and who performed each action. Those events are signed and can be forwarded to your central logging. retention and export rules are configurable.
[05:05] Ava Reynolds: encryption in-transit and at-rest standard? TLS and AES256? any FIPS modules?
[05:12] Maya Chen: we use TLS 1.2+ and AES-256 for at-rest encryption. For customers requiring FIPS-validated modules, we offer options with HSM-backed key stores and can support FIPS endpoints in Private deployments, though that is part of an enterprise setup.
[05:28] Samir Patel: token handling — do you store API tokens or ephemeral only? if someone exfiltrates tokens, what's the mitigation?
[05:36] Ethan Park: service tokens are stored encrypted; we also support short-lived tokens via our session tokens and recommend rotating keys. You can require mTLS for service-to-service traffic in Private to reduce token risk. We also provide token revocation APIs.
[05:50] Liam Brooks: about logs again: we need to avoid prompt contents being replicated into other vendors for indexing. Is there a way to block telemetry out of the box?
[05:58] Maya Chen: yes, we can disable telemetry and analytics capture for a given deployment. For Hosted there are some aggregate telemetry pipelines for performance; those can be disabled for enterprise customers and eliminated entirely in Private.
[06:12] Samir Patel: SOC2 / ISO artifacts — can you provide the certs and the scope? which systems are in scope for SOC2?
[06:19] Maya Chen: we can share our SOC 2 Type II report and an ISO 27001 certificate redacted as needed. scope will list hosted control plane and the managed data stores. For Dedicated and Private, the customer's environment is often out of Redwood scope because it's in their VPC, though Redwood support services connecting to it may still be in scope depending on contract. We'll supply a scope matrix with examples.
[06:42] Ava Reynolds: important — we can't have default support access to full keys. remote support must be role-limited and require approval.
[06:50] Ethan Park: understood. support access is auditable and granular; we can implement break-glass flows with approvals and time-limited access. All support sessions are logged.
[06:58] Liam Brooks: curious about model fallback behavior — you mentioned earlier auto fallback to smaller/cheaper models — does that ever change data handling?
[07:07] Maya Chen: fallback to model variants is a routing policy; it doesn't change where data lands, but it can affect tokenization and prompt truncation. You can pin models to avoid automatic fallbacks if determinism matters.
[07:20] Samir Patel: about data retention for embeddings — we create embeddings of customer data, do you store those?
[07:27] Ethan Park: embeddings can be stored in your account or in Redwood-managed stores. For sensitive data, customers typically choose to store embeddings in their own encrypted stores or disable indexing. When we store embeddings on behalf of customers, they're encrypted with either Redwood CMK or the customer's CMK in Private modes.
[07:44] Ava Reynolds: what about deletion guarantees? if we ask for data purge, what's the SLAs and can you provide attestations?
[07:51] Maya Chen: we have an automated deletion pipeline; for Hosted we commit to deletion within configured windows and provide confirmation receipts. For Private, deletion is deterministic because the data sits in your account. We can provide deletion attestations as a paid compliance service.
[08:05] Samir Patel: compliance with data residency — some of our customers' PII must stay in EU region. Do you support region locking?
[08:12] Ethan Park: yes, region pinning is supported for models and storage. For Hosted we host regionally; Dedicated and Private give you full control over region. We can document region controls for SOC2 mapping.
[08:24] Ava Reynolds: to shift tone a bit, what's the operational burden for Dedicated vs Private? we want low ops but still strict controls.
[08:33] Maya Chen: Dedicated is a good middle ground: Redwood hosts the infra in a dedicated GPU pool isolated for your account. You get the predictability of capacity with lower operational overhead. Private requires more customer ops (VPC, routing, KMS) but gives max control and reduces Redwood's scope.
[08:51] Liam Brooks: curious about costs of BYOK and HSM integration — any rough delta?
[08:57] Ethan Park: HSM/BYOK adds integration overhead and sometimes licensing for connectors; there is typically a one-time onboarding professional services fee plus minor ongoing costs for HSM usage. We'll include a rough BOM in the pricing doc.
[09:12] Samir Patel: can we get sample audit logs to review schema? CSV or JSON?
[09:18] Maya Chen: yes, we'll attach a sample JSON schema and example events — admin actions, invoke events (metadata only unless prompt capture enabled), key rotations, and SSO events.
[09:30] Ava Reynolds: ok, next steps from our side: Samir will send the KMS policy and a list of must-have compliance controls; Liam will confirm the internal timeline, then we will book the SE bench test.
[09:40] Maya Chen: perfect. we'll send the SOC2/ISO assets and a short playbook for Private onboarding. Ethan will own the bench test plan.
[09:48] Samir Patel: a couple nitty-gritty Qs — do you support hardware attestation for worker nodes (TPM) in Private?
[09:55] Ethan Park: for on-prem we can integrate with TPM attestation and signed images. In cloud Private deployments we support verified boot and image signing for compute nodes; we can get into details in the SE bench session.
[10:08] Liam Brooks: one last thing — we may need PCI scoping guidance. do you have an architecture whitepaper for financial customers?
[10:15] Maya Chen: yes, we have a compliance architecture reference for finance; we'll pull that and highlight patterns to reduce PCI scope like not storing cardholder data in prompts and forwarding logs to your vault.
[10:27] Ava Reynolds: thanks, this is helpful.
[10:30] Maya Chen: great, any other questions?
[10:33] Samir Patel: nothing at the moment, looking forward to the artifacts.
[10:36] Liam Brooks: no, thank you.
[10:38] Maya Chen: ok we'll follow up with the items noted. thanks everyone, talk soon.

Notes on transcript quality: some disfluencies and a few mis-transcribed product names (e.g., "K N S" instead of KMS, or "consul" instead of console) — this is from auto-transcription.

References mentioned (will attach in follow-up):
- SOC2 Type II report (redacted)
- ISO 27001 certificate (redacted)
- Private deployment checklist and BOM
- Sample audit log JSON schema and examples
- Bench test plan template for 2k token latency

End of transcript.

title: Urgent: Fallback policy & long-tail error mitigation sync
preview: Customer reported a series of long-tail 5xx/timeout errors affecting checkout traffic during peak. Today alignment focused on containment (immediate fallback/rollback), investigation scope (logs, KV cache checks, prefix caching), and medium-term reliability mitigations (canary, circuit breaker, observability tweaks). SE and AE walked through a proposed rollback playbook, discussed tradeoffs between hosted failover vs Dedicated fallbacks, and agreed on short list of actions and timelines.
text: Customer reported a series of long-tail 5xx/timeout errors affecting checkout traffic during peak. Today alignment focused on containment (immediate fallback/rollback), investigation scope (logs, KV cache checks, prefix caching), and medium-term reliability mitigations (canary, circuit breaker, observability tweaks). SE and AE walked through a proposed rollback playbook, discussed tradeoffs between hosted failover vs Dedicated fallbacks, and agreed on short list of actions and timelines.

title: Incident taxonomy working session (v1) - tricky classifications + owner mapping
preview: Working session to finalize incident taxonomy v1 edges and the owner mapping for disputed categories. Team aligned on keeping top-level types small (target ~12) with subtypes where needed. Resolved key disputes: capacity shortage remains Fleet primary; routing/fallback misbehavior remains Smart Routing primary; quota/rate limit enforcement is Control Plane primary (Support consulted for customer-caused throttling); streaming disconnects default to Edge/Gateway unless clear client SDK regression. Agreed to add a simple rule for multi-cause incidents: pick the mitigation owner as primary type, track other systems as contributing factors in postmortem. Observability reiterated cardinality guardrails (allowlist + unknown bucket). Action items captured for updating the decision tree, owner mapping config, and incident-bot suggestions.

Observability reiterated cardinality guardrails (allowlist + unknown bucket). Action items captured for updating the decision tree, owner mapping config, and incident-bot suggestions.

title: Helios Defense — Private pilot check-in (signature verification issues)
preview: Helios reports offline install preflight is failing signature verification on the latest pilot bundle after transfer into the air-gapped network. Redwood triages live: likely bundle was modified during unzip/repack (hash mismatch) and there is also an installer version mismatch (older installer doesn’t support the detached signature layout + updated trust store). Agreed on a clean re-transfer method, validating checksums before/after copy, and upgrading the installer to the required build. Redwood to resend the bundle + provide exact commands and expected output, and Helios to share the failing logs and the install host’s system time configuration.

title: Hardware tuning profiles pack - weekly sync
preview:
Meeting: Hardware tuning profiles pack - weekly sync
Date: 2025-12-03
Start time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: 47 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees (Redwood): Talia Benson, Hiro Tanaka, Sofia Ivanova, Irene Choi, Rina Kobayashi, Claire Dubois, Ethan Park, Benji Okafor, Omar El-Sayed, Kira Volkov, Sean Gallagher, Vanessa Ortiz

Auto-summary (generated)
- H100 preset looks net-positive on throughput with a tail-latency caveat that improved after the fairness patch; need one more matrix run to confirm.
- L40S remains the main stability risk due to long-context OOM; KV-cache budgeting guardrails are close but need validation.
- B200 stays behind experimental flag; limited fleet access + driver/CUDA constraints; need stricter gating and region-level disable.
- Perf-canary gates are noisy; plan to adjust thresholds and use multi-run aggregation.

Topics
1) Milestone check: profile pack v0.3.0 content
2) H100 benchmark deltas (balanced vs throughput-first)
3) L40S OOMs + KV cache guardrails
4) B200 experimental profile status and fleet constraints
5) Override order / loader edge cases (SKU strings, MIG)
6) Perf-canary + rollout guardrails

[00:00] Talia Benson: Alright, we’ll get started. This is the hardware tuning profiles weekly. Goal today: quick status from Kernels, Scheduling, Infra, Bench. Then blockers and next steps. We’re aiming for v0.3 pack content freeze end of week-ish, depending on the last matrix run.

[00:22] Talia Benson: Hiro, can you start with H100 kernel side? What changed since last week.

[00:28] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah. So kernel autoselect heuristics by sequence length buckets are in good shape. We adjusted the cutoffs. Previously we were switching too early and it hurt prefill on the mid bucket—like 2k to 4k tokens. Now it’s more stable.

[00:45] Hiro Tanaka: Also, the “balanced” preset is basically stable, but we still have a couple knobs I’m not sure we should freeze in the profile. Specifically the block size toggle—um the one that picks the fused attention variant. It’s safe on H100 SXM, less sure on PCIe.

[01:03] Irene Choi: And we do have both in fleet. The hosted pools are mostly SXM, dedicated varies.

[01:08] Hiro Tanaka: Exactly. So I want to guard on memory bandwidth / device name.

[01:13] Ethan Park: Just to clarify, guardrails here means the profile includes “applies_if” rules, right? Like “sku_family=h100” plus “form_factor=sxm” or “hbm>=80GB”?

[01:25] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah, that’s the idea.

[01:27] Talia Benson: Okay, so for v0.3 we keep it simple: H100 with explicit differentiation if needed. Ethan, that’s compatible with the schema we landed?

[01:35] Ethan Park: Mostly. We can do it without nesting complex expressions. But we need to keep it readable. Also we have to be careful with string variants. Like “NVIDIA H100 PCIe” vs “H100-80GB” and sometimes the cloud provider adds junk.

[01:49] Irene Choi: Yeah. We’ve got a list. I’ll send the updated list after this.

[01:54] Talia Benson: Great. Sofia, scheduling side?

[01:58] Sofia Ivanova: On scheduler: the big change is we picked up Marcus’s fix for prefill/decode fairness. That was the tail regression we saw when continuous batching was set more aggressively. The patch basically prevents decode starvation when we have a few long prefills.

[02:17] Sofia Ivanova: On the benchmark runs from yesterday, p95 improved. It’s not totally gone in the worst-case streaming scenario, but it’s within the guardrail bands we discussed with SRE.

[02:28] Sean Gallagher: Which band? Like, what are we talking.

[02:31] Sofia Ivanova: For the streaming chat workload at concurrency 64, p95 went from +18% to +6% versus baseline, while tokens/sec stayed +11%. So still a trade, but it’s less scary.

[02:45] Sean Gallagher: Okay. +6% might be acceptable if we’re honest about expected behavior and we can rollback quickly.

[02:52] Talia Benson: Rina/Claire, can you anchor this with the matrix summary? Are those numbers representative or cherry picked.

[02:59] Rina Kobayashi: Um, it’s representative for the “streaming mix” bucket. We have three main mixes right now: non-streaming summarization, streaming chat, and long-context Q&A. The streaming one is the noisy one.

[03:12] Claire Dubois: Also, we still don’t have the “tool calling” pattern in the matrix. Applied ML asked for that because the stop-token and structured output changes the decode pacing. I can add it but we won’t have perfect realism for v0.3.

[03:25] Talia Benson: Understood. Let’s add a lightweight version, not derail.

[03:30] Omar El-Sayed: Can I jump in on “long-context Q&A” because that’s where L40S is exploding.

[03:35] Talia Benson: Yep, go ahead.

[03:38] Omar El-Sayed: So L40S OOMs aren’t just “too big KV”. It’s fragmentation plus some caching behavior. When we do long prompts with moderate concurrency, the KV cache budget expands and we don’t reclaim fast enough. I’ve got a guardrail patch that caps KV per request and shifts to a more conservative eviction.

[03:59] Rina Kobayashi: Is that the thing that was called “KV cash” in the logs? Sorry, I keep reading it like money.

[04:04] Omar El-Sayed: Yeah, the logs are… not great.

[04:08] Benji Okafor: Also the config key name is “kv_cache_budget_mb” and someone’s typo’d it in one of the YAML examples. We should fix docs before it becomes a support issue.

[04:17] Talia Benson: Please capture that as an action item; we can fold into docs PR.

[04:22] Omar El-Sayed: For L40S: with the cap, OOMs went away in my local repro, but p50 got slightly worse on short prompts due to more frequent evictions.

[04:33] Sofia Ivanova: That’s expected. The question is: does it hurt p95 a lot.

[04:38] Rina Kobayashi: We didn’t finish the full L40S matrix because the perf-canary runner died mid run. It’s flaky.

[04:45] Kira Volkov: That’s the same flake I’ve been tracking. The job sometimes pins CPU and the driver resets. It’s not strictly a runtime regression.

[04:54] Vanessa Ortiz: That might be the runner node type. Those L40S boxes have weird CPU steal. We can allocate a different host class.

[05:02] Talia Benson: Okay, but for the pack release we need stable validation. Can we prioritize making L40S runner stable this week.

[05:10] Vanessa Ortiz: Yes, I’ll sync with infra CI folks. We can reserve a pool.

[05:15] Talia Benson: Great.

[05:17] Talia Benson: B200 status. Irene?

[05:20] Irene Choi: Limited access. We have, like, two nodes in us-east and one in eu-west. Driver is the big constraint. Some nodes are on a different branch; CUDA minor version mismatch. So we can’t run the same build everywhere.

[05:36] Hiro Tanaka: Also kernel differences. Some of the H100 heuristics don’t port cleanly. We have to keep the B200 profile experimental and strict-check the driver/CUDA. If check fails, fallback to generic.

[05:50] Ethan Park: The loader supports “requires” with min runtime version and min driver. But we need to decide what we log and what we do when it doesn’t match.

[05:59] Benji Okafor: Current behavior is: if incompatible, log warning, select default profile. But in Private deployments, warnings get ignored. We added startup log matrix in the runtime to show runtime version vs profile version. People will still ignore it.

[06:14] Sean Gallagher: From oncall perspective: I want a big obvious metric: selected_profile, source, and whether we fell back due to compatibility.

[06:22] Kira Volkov: That’s in the metrics PR. It emits “profile_selected{profile=..., source=..., reason=...}” basically.

[06:31] Talia Benson: Okay. For B200, we stick to “off by default” and enable in one pool only. We also need a clear rollback toggle.

[06:41] Irene Choi: Already planning to disable by pool config. But we need override precedence: fleet default vs per-pool vs per-customer.

[06:49] Ethan Park: That’s the ADR. In short: default is in runtime, pack overlays, then fleet/pool overrides, then customer overrides, then per-route overrides. Env var remains highest priority for emergency.

[07:03] Sean Gallagher: Env var at highest priority makes me nervous, but I get why. For emergency we want it.

[07:08] Talia Benson: Let’s park ADR debate; we have to ship.

[07:12] Claire Dubois: Can I ask about “expected behavior” language. Last week we had a docs thread: we need to avoid sounding like we guarantee p95 improves.

[07:20] Talia Benson: Correct. We say “expected behavior under this workload envelope”. We explicitly call out tradeoffs and failure modes.

[07:28] Sofia Ivanova: I’ll add a line: throughput-first can increase tail latency, especially streaming and long-prefill mixes. Balanced mode is default for Hosted. Throughput-first maybe for Dedicated batchy workloads.

[07:42] Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also some kernels are better at long seq but worse at short seq. That’s why the bucketization exists.

[07:51] Rina Kobayashi: On H100 matrix: overall tokens/sec improved 8–15% depending on model family. p50 latency improved slightly on non-streaming. p95 on streaming is variable but less bad after fairness fix.

[08:04] Claire Dubois: Which models are in the matrix again? For notes.

[08:08] Rina Kobayashi: Three families: Llama-style 70B-ish, a smaller 8B-ish, and an embedding model. I’m not saying names because we keep swapping the exact ones, but it’s consistent.

[08:20] Talia Benson: Good. Put the exact model pins in the benchmark artifact, not in this call.

[08:25] Kira Volkov: Perf-canary gates: right now thresholds are too tight for p95 on streaming. We’re failing builds due to noise. I propose we run each scenario three times and gate on median. Also widen band for the known noisy buckets.

[08:41] Sean Gallagher: Please run that by SRE. We can’t widen too much and miss regressions.

[08:47] Kira Volkov: Yeah. We can keep strict bands on error rate, OOM, and queue depth. p95 can be median-of-3 with narrower band.

[08:57] Benji Okafor: On queue depth: the new backpressure settings can change queue behavior. It might look “worse” but it’s actually protecting latency. We need to define the metric expectation.

[09:08] Sofia Ivanova: Exactly. A slightly higher queue depth might be fine if tail latency is stable. But if queue grows without bound, that’s bad.

[09:17] Talia Benson: We need a consistent oncall triage list: which dashboards, which metric thresholds mean rollback.

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: For rollback criteria, I want: p95 latency +10% sustained 15 minutes on top routes OR error rate above X OR OOM events > 0 in that pool post-deploy. Plus a “customer ticket spike” clause.

[09:40] Talia Benson: Great. Capture in runbook.

[09:43] (crosstalk)

[09:44] Vanessa Ortiz: Also autoscaling. If we change batching, throughput changes, autoscaling may react weirdly. We should watch utilization and scale oscillations.

[09:54] Irene Choi: Yep. Some pools have different scaling policies. We’ll do staged rollout: one canary pool per region, then 10%, then 50%, then 100.

[10:06] Talia Benson: Okay, let’s get more detailed on H100: balanced vs throughput-first. Sofia, what knobs differ.

[10:13] Sofia Ivanova: Balanced: lower max batch tokens, more strict prefill/decode fairness, smaller queue limits. Throughput-first: higher max batch tokens, more aggressive continuous batching window, less strict fairness.

[10:28] Hiro Tanaka: And kernel side: throughput-first allows selecting kernels that are slightly worse for short sequences but better for long, to maximize aggregate tokens/sec.

[10:40] Claire Dubois: That aligns with workloads: summarization batch jobs vs chat.

[10:45] Talia Benson: So default: Balanced for Hosted. For Dedicated, we can let customers pick. For Private, default Balanced but document override.

[10:56] Benji Okafor: On Private override: Helm values wire in the profile pack and also allow overriding per deployment. We should include safe “known good” examples.

[11:07] Ethan Park: And validation: if someone sets nonsense values, config validation should fail fast.

[11:13] Sean Gallagher: Fail fast is good, but in production it might brick a rollout.

[11:18] Ethan Park: Right. We only fail fast for invalid types/out-of-range. For optional keys, we ignore unknown keys with a warning. That was the compromise.

[11:30] Talia Benson: Okay.

[11:32] Talia Benson: Let’s check blockers. Rina: what’s blocking the next benchmark report.

[11:37] Rina Kobayashi: Two things: L40S runner flake and missing one H100 rerun after the fairness fix merged. The H100 rerun should finish today if the queue cooperates.

[11:49] Kira Volkov: The H100 queue is fine. L40S is the one with, like, random driver resets.

[11:55] Vanessa Ortiz: I’ll get you a reserved node class by tomorrow.

[12:00] Rina Kobayashi: Great.

[12:02] Talia Benson: Irene: any infra blockers.

[12:05] Irene Choi: Only B200 access. We can’t do a full matrix. I can get NVIDIA to lend us more for a week, but not guaranteed.

[12:14] Hiro Tanaka: For v0.3, maybe it’s fine if B200 remains “experimental preview” with limited validation.

[12:22] Talia Benson: That’s consistent with leadership: experimental, gated, clear fallback. No broad enablement.

[12:30] Claire Dubois: Can we ensure docs say “B200 profile requires driver X / CUDA Y” and “not supported in all regions”.

[12:38] Ethan Park: Yes, and runtime logs show compatibility matrix.

[12:42] Benji Okafor: Plus a debug endpoint to dump effective runtime config. Oncall can ask a customer to run it in Private.

[12:50] Sean Gallagher: That’s huge. As long as it’s gated/secured.

[12:54] Benji Okafor: It’s internal-only by default; for Private we require an explicit enable flag and auth.

[13:01] Talia Benson: Cool.

[13:03] (brief pause)

[13:05] Talia Benson: Let’s talk about override precedence edge cases: MIG and multi-GPU nodes. Irene, did we decide per-device vs per-node.

[13:14] Irene Choi: Most of our serving nodes are single GPU, but some Dedicated has multi. MIG is rare but exists.

[13:22] Ethan Park: The current loader selects a profile per process based on detected GPU 0. If you have mixed GPUs, that’s a problem. But we don’t support mixed in one node anyway.

[13:33] Sean Gallagher: What about MIG slices. The device name can include “MIG 1g.10gb” etc.

[13:40] Irene Choi: Yep, that breaks naive string matching. I’ll include the variants we saw.

[13:46] Hiro Tanaka: Kernel presets on MIG are risky. We should treat MIG as “unknown” and choose conservative defaults.

[13:54] Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling also changes; smaller memory means smaller KV budgets. So yes, conservative.

[14:00] Talia Benson: Okay, we’ll add guardrail: if MIG detected, don’t apply tuned profile unless explicitly allowed.

[14:08] Ethan Park: That’s easy.

[14:10] Talia Benson: Now canary rollout plan. Sean, what do you need.

[14:15] Sean Gallagher: I need the runbook to be explicit: which toggle disables profiles for a pool, and where to look. Dashboard should have panels segmented by selected profile. And alert thresholds should be enumerated.

[14:30] Kira Volkov: Observability pack has the new panels. We can link them.

[14:34] Sean Gallagher: Also: who’s on point during rollout windows. I don’t want a Friday night surprise.

[14:40] Talia Benson: We’ll coordinate with release train owner but for now: staged in business hours. Irene, can we commit to that.

[14:47] Irene Choi: Yes.

[14:49] Talia Benson: Great.

[14:51] (transcription note: multiple speakers)

[14:52] Sofia Ivanova: One more thing: the throughput-first preset can cause token latency spikes if the request mix has lots of short prompts. We should keep it opt-in.

[15:02] Hiro Tanaka: Yep.

[15:04] Claire Dubois: That’s also about workload envelope. We should put a table: recommended for “batchy summarization, non-streaming”, not recommended for “interactive streaming chat with tight p95”.

[15:15] Talia Benson: Good, docs will include.

[15:18] Talia Benson: Anything else from Bench.

[15:21] Rina Kobayashi: I want to mention one thing: embedding workloads. The tuned profiles didn’t help much; maybe 1–2% variance. But they didn’t hurt. So we can say “no meaningful change expected.”

[15:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[15:35] Omar El-Sayed: On L40S: we should define safe-by-default memory behavior. Like KV budget is a function of free memory. But we can cap at X. Right now I used 65% of available memory, leaving headroom.

[15:48] Sean Gallagher: Headroom is good. OOMs are the worst.

[15:52] Sofia Ivanova: But if headroom is too big, throughput suffers.

[15:56] Omar El-Sayed: Yep. That’s the trade.

[15:59] Talia Benson: For v0.3: stability > max throughput on L40S.

[16:05] Hiro Tanaka: Agree.

[16:07] Talia Benson: Okay, quick pass on timeline. Today: H100 rerun finishing. Tomorrow: Irene sends SKU variants + reserved L40S runner plan. End of week: v0.3 content freeze pending L40S validation.

[16:23] Benji Okafor: And integration tests for override precedence are in review. I’ll push to merge by Friday.

[16:30] Ethan Park: Schema validation is already merged.

[16:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[16:35] (The transcript continues; mid-section includes detailed Q&A on perf-canary thresholds, startup logging, and B200 gating checks.)

[24:10] Kira Volkov: One thing: gating on p95 as an absolute number is hard because different runs are noisy. That’s why I’m pushing relative change vs baseline on the same runner.

[24:22] Sean Gallagher: Relative change is fine as long as baseline is recent. Otherwise it drifts.

[24:28] Claire Dubois: We can pin baseline artifacts per runtime release. The matrix runner can store last green baseline.

[24:36] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, the runner emits a summary JSON artifact. We can compare.

[24:44] Talia Benson: Okay.

[31:05] Irene Choi: For the EU-west B200 node: we already saw weird regressions last time. I’d prefer we disable by default there until we know the driver is consistent.

[31:15] Hiro Tanaka: Agreed.

[31:17] Benji Okafor: That can be a pool override that forces profile “generic-safe”.

[31:24] Sean Gallagher: Perfect.

[38:02] Claire Dubois: Quick doc note: please stop saying “guarantee”. Say “expected”. Also we should include “known failure modes”: long prompt distributions, fragmentation, MIG.

[38:16] Talia Benson: Yes.

[42:40] Talia Benson: Recap time. H100: we’re close, one rerun after fairness fix, likely lock balanced preset. L40S: must fix runner flake and validate KV budget guardrail across long-context bucket. B200: experimental flag, strict version checks, disable in EU-west by default. Perf canary: adjust to median-of-3, tighten non-latency metrics. Runbook: concrete rollback thresholds and toggles.

[43:10] Talia Benson: Did I miss anything.

[43:12] (crosstalk)

[43:13] Sofia Ivanova: No, that’s it.

[43:15] Hiro Tanaka: I’ll share the final bucket mapping options in Slack.

[43:18] Rina Kobayashi: I’ll publish the artifact once H100 rerun finishes.

[43:21] Irene Choi: I’ll send SKU strings and MIG variants.

[43:25] Sean Gallagher: Please add rollback criteria draft in the runbook doc by Friday.

[43:32] Talia Benson: Done. Thanks everyone.

End of transcript

[06:31] Talia Benson: Okay. For B200, we stick to “off by default” and enable in one pool only. We also need a clear rollback toggle.

[06:41] Irene Choi: Already planning to disable by pool config. But we need override precedence: fleet default vs per-pool vs per-customer.

[06:49] Ethan Park: That’s the ADR. In short: default is in runtime, pack overlays, then fleet/pool overrides, then customer overrides, then per-route overrides. Env var remains highest priority for emergency.

[07:03] Sean Gallagher: Env var at highest priority makes me nervous, but I get why. For emergency we want it.

[07:08] Talia Benson: Let’s park ADR debate; we have to ship.

[07:12] Claire Dubois: Can I ask about “expected behavior” language. Last week we had a docs thread: we need to avoid sounding like we guarantee p95 improves.

[07:20] Talia Benson: Correct. We say “expected behavior under this workload envelope”. We explicitly call out tradeoffs and failure modes.

[07:28] Sofia Ivanova: I’ll add a line: throughput-first can increase tail latency, especially streaming and long-prefill mixes. Balanced mode is default for Hosted. Throughput-first maybe for Dedicated batchy workloads.

[07:42] Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also some kernels are better at long seq but worse at short seq. That’s why the bucketization exists.

[07:51] Rina Kobayashi: On H100 matrix: overall tokens/sec improved 8–15% depending on model family. p50 latency improved slightly on non-streaming. p95 on streaming is variable but less bad after fairness fix.

[08:04] Claire Dubois: Which models are in the matrix again? For notes.

[08:08] Rina Kobayashi: Three families: Llama-style 70B-ish, a smaller 8B-ish, and an embedding model. I’m not saying names because we keep swapping the exact ones, but it’s consistent.

[08:20] Talia Benson: Good. Put the exact model pins in the benchmark artifact, not in this call.

[08:25] Kira Volkov: Perf-canary gates: right now thresholds are too tight for p95 on streaming. We’re failing builds due to noise. I propose we run each scenario three times and gate on median. Also widen band for the known noisy buckets.

[08:41] Sean Gallagher: Please run that by SRE. We can’t widen too much and miss regressions.

[08:47] Kira Volkov: Yeah. We can keep strict bands on error rate, OOM, and queue depth. p95 can be median-of-3 with narrower band.

[08:57] Benji Okafor: On queue depth: the new backpressure settings can change queue behavior. It might look “worse” but it’s actually protecting latency. We need to define the metric expectation.

[09:08] Sofia Ivanova: Exactly. A slightly higher queue depth might be fine if tail latency is stable. But if queue grows without bound, that’s bad.

[09:17] Talia Benson: We need a consistent oncall triage list: which dashboards, which metric thresholds mean rollback.

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: For rollback criteria, I want: p95 latency +10% sustained 15 minutes on top routes OR error rate above X OR OOM events > 0 in that pool post-deploy. Plus a “customer ticket spike” clause.

[09:40] Talia Benson: Great. Capture in runbook.

[09:43] (crosstalk)

[09:44] Vanessa Ortiz: Also autoscaling. If we change batching, throughput changes, autoscaling may react weirdly. We should watch utilization and scale oscillations.

[09:54] Irene Choi: Yep. Some pools have different scaling policies. We’ll do staged rollout: one canary pool per region, then 10%, then 50%, then 100.

[10:06] Talia Benson: Okay, let’s get more detailed on H100: balanced vs throughput-first. Sofia, what knobs differ.

[10:13] Sofia Ivanova: Balanced: lower max batch tokens, more strict prefill/decode fairness, smaller queue limits. Throughput-first: higher max batch tokens, more aggressive continuous batching window, less strict fairness.

[10:28] Hiro Tanaka: And kernel side: throughput-first allows selecting kernels that are slightly worse for short sequences but better for long, to maximize aggregate tokens/sec.

[10:40] Claire Dubois: That aligns with workloads: summarization batch jobs vs chat.

[10:45] Talia Benson: So default: Balanced for Hosted. For Dedicated, we can let customers pick. For Private, default Balanced but document override.

[10:56] Benji Okafor: On Private override: Helm values wire in the profile pack and also allow overriding per deployment. We should include safe “known good” examples.

[11:07] Ethan Park: And validation: if someone sets nonsense values, config validation should fail fast.

[11:13] Sean Gallagher: Fail fast is good, but in production it might brick a rollout.

[11:18] Ethan Park: Right. We only fail fast for invalid types/out-of-range. For optional keys, we ignore unknown keys with a warning. That was the compromise.

[11:30] Talia Benson: Okay.

[11:32] Talia Benson: Let’s check blockers. Rina: what’s blocking the next benchmark report.

[11:37] Rina Kobayashi: Two things: L40S runner flake and missing one H100 rerun after the fairness fix merged. The H100 rerun should finish today if the queue cooperates.

[11:49] Kira Volkov: The H100 queue is fine. L40S is the one with, like, random driver resets.

[11:55] Vanessa Ortiz: I’ll get you a reserved node class by tomorrow.

[12:00] Rina Kobayashi: Great.

[12:02] Talia Benson: Irene: any infra blockers.

[12:05] Irene Choi: Only B200 access. We can’t do a full matrix. I can get NVIDIA to lend us more for a week, but not guaranteed.

[12:14] Hiro Tanaka: For v0.3, maybe it’s fine if B200 remains “experimental preview” with limited validation.

[12:22] Talia Benson: That’s consistent with leadership: experimental, gated, clear fallback. No broad enablement.

[12:30] Claire Dubois: Can we ensure docs say “B200 profile requires driver X / CUDA Y” and “not supported in all regions”.

[12:38] Ethan Park: Yes, and runtime logs show compatibility matrix.

[12:42] Benji Okafor: Plus a debug endpoint to dump effective runtime config. Oncall can ask a customer to run it in Private.

[12:50] Sean Gallagher: That’s huge. As long as it’s gated/secured.

[12:54] Benji Okafor: It’s internal-only by default; for Private we require an explicit enable flag and auth.

[13:01] Talia Benson: Cool.

[13:03] (brief pause)

[13:05] Talia Benson: Let’s talk about override precedence edge cases: MIG and multi-GPU nodes. Irene, did we decide per-device vs per-node.

[13:14] Irene Choi: Most of our serving nodes are single GPU, but some Dedicated has multi. MIG is rare but exists.

[13:22] Ethan Park: The current loader selects a profile per process based on detected GPU 0. If you have mixed GPUs, that’s a problem. But we don’t support mixed in one node anyway.

[13:33] Sean Gallagher: What about MIG slices. The device name can include “MIG 1g.10gb” etc.

[13:40] Irene Choi: Yep, that breaks naive string matching. I’ll include the variants we saw.

[13:46] Hiro Tanaka: Kernel presets on MIG are risky. We should treat MIG as “unknown” and choose conservative defaults.

[13:54] Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling also changes; smaller memory means smaller KV budgets. So yes, conservative.

[14:00] Talia Benson: Okay, we’ll add guardrail: if MIG detected, don’t apply tuned profile unless explicitly allowed.

[14:08] Ethan Park: That’s easy.

[14:10] Talia Benson: Now canary rollout plan. Sean, what do you need.

[14:15] Sean Gallagher: I need the runbook to be explicit: which toggle disables profiles for a pool, and where to look. Dashboard should have panels segmented by selected profile. And alert thresholds should be enumerated.

[14:30] Kira Volkov: Observability pack has the new panels. We can link them.

[14:34] Sean Gallagher: Also: who’s on point during rollout windows. I don’t want a Friday night surprise.

[14:40] Talia Benson: We’ll coordinate with release train owner but for now: staged in business hours. Irene, can we commit to that.

[14:47] Irene Choi: Yes.

[14:49] Talia Benson: Great.

[14:51] (transcription note: multiple speakers)

[14:52] Sofia Ivanova: One more thing: the throughput-first preset can cause token latency spikes if the request mix has lots of short prompts. We should keep it opt-in.

[15:02] Hiro Tanaka: Yep.

[15:04] Claire Dubois: That’s also about workload envelope. We should put a table: recommended for “batchy summarization, non-streaming”, not recommended for “interactive streaming chat with tight p95”.

[15:15] Talia Benson: Good, docs will include.

[15:18] Talia Benson: Anything else from Bench.

[15:21] Rina Kobayashi: I want to mention one thing: embedding workloads. The tuned profiles didn’t help much; maybe 1–2% variance. But they didn’t hurt. So we can say “no meaningful change expected.”

[15:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[15:35] Omar El-Sayed: On L40S: we should define safe-by-default memory behavior. Like KV budget is a function of free memory. But we can cap at X. Right now I used 65% of available memory, leaving headroom.

[15:48] Sean Gallagher: Headroom is good. OOMs are the worst.

[15:52] Sofia Ivanova: But if headroom is too big, throughput suffers.

[15:56] Omar El-Sayed: Yep. That’s the trade.

[15:59] Talia Benson: For v0.3: stability > max throughput on L40S.

[16:05] Hiro Tanaka: Agree.

[16:07] Talia Benson: Okay, quick pass on timeline. Today: H100 rerun finishing. Tomorrow: Irene sends SKU variants + reserved L40S runner plan. End of week: v0.3 content freeze pending L40S validation.

[16:23] Benji Okafor: And integration tests for override precedence are in review. I’ll push to merge by Friday.

[16:30] Ethan Park: Schema validation is already merged.

[16:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[16:35] (The transcript continues; mid-section includes detailed Q&A on perf-canary thresholds, startup logging, and B200 gating checks.)

[24:10] Kira Volkov: One thing: gating on p95 as an absolute number is hard because different runs are noisy. That’s why I’m pushing relative change vs baseline on the same runner.

[24:22] Sean Gallagher: Relative change is fine as long as baseline is recent. Otherwise it drifts.

[24:28] Claire Dubois: We can pin baseline artifacts per runtime release. The matrix runner can store last green baseline.

[24:36] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, the runner emits a summary JSON artifact. We can compare.

[24:44] Talia Benson: Okay.

[31:05] Irene Choi: For the EU-west B200 node: we already saw weird regressions last time. I’d prefer we disable by default there until we know the driver is consistent.

[31:15] Hiro Tanaka: Agreed.

[31:17] Benji Okafor: That can be a pool override that forces profile “generic-safe”.

[31:24] Sean Gallagher: Perfect.

[38:02] Claire Dubois: Quick doc note: please stop saying “guarantee”. Say “expected”. Also we should include “known failure modes”: long prompt distributions, fragmentation, MIG.

[38:16] Talia Benson: Yes.

[42:40] Talia Benson: Recap time. H100: we’re close, one rerun after fairness fix, likely lock balanced preset. L40S: must fix runner flake and validate KV budget guardrail across long-context bucket. B200: experimental flag, strict version checks, disable in EU-west by default. Perf canary: adjust to median-of-3, tighten non-latency metrics. Runbook: concrete rollback thresholds and toggles.

[43:10] Talia Benson: Did I miss anything.

[43:12] (crosstalk)

[43:13] Sofia Ivanova: No, that’s it.

[43:15] Hiro Tanaka: I’ll share the final bucket mapping options in Slack.

[43:18] Rina Kobayashi: I’ll publish the artifact once H100 rerun finishes.

[43:21] Irene Choi: I’ll send SKU strings and MIG variants.

[43:25] Sean Gallagher: Please add rollback criteria draft in the runbook doc by Friday.

[43:32] Talia Benson: Done. Thanks everyone.

End of transcript

title: Zero-trust firewalling & audit playbook - Galileo Tech VPC private deployment review
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date/Time: 2025-12-02 15:00 PT
- Duration: ~65 minutes
- Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Lina Morales (Galileo Head of Infra), Tomás Rivera (Galileo Security Lead), Priya Desai (Galileo Cloud Architect)

Summary:
Quick technical deep-dive focused on private/VPC deployment security controls: zero-trust firewalling, egress restrictions, ingress patterns for private endpoints, KMS/HSM integration for envelope encryption, audit log collection and retention, SSO/SCIM for RBAC, and SOC 2 readiness checklist. Goal: agree on pilot scope and confirm integration points and required artifacts.

Topics covered:
- Private deployment architecture options (VPC-only vs air-gapped on-prem)
- Network isolation and zero-trust firewall strategy (egress strict allow list vs deny all + exception)
- Ingress patterns: private endpoints, load balancer vs service endpoints
- KMS integration and key management (envelope encryption, CMKs, HSM options)
- Audit logging: retention, SIEM integration, tamper-proofing
- RBAC/SSO: SAML/Okta, SCIM provisioning, admin audit trails
- SOC 2/ISO deliverables and timelines

Transcript:
[00:00] Maya: Hey everyone, thanks for joining — we can kick off in a minute, uh is this the right line for Priya?
[00:08] Priya: Yeah this is Priya, sorry had some calendar lag, on my side in SF office.
[00:11] Lina: Hi, Lina here, thanks. We pulled in Tomas because he owns the compliance questions.
[00:16] Maya: Perfect. Quick agenda: run through our recommended zero-trust firewall playbook for Redwood Private, talk KMS integration for keying, then audit logs and retention, and leave 15 for next steps. Sound good?
[00:25] Tomás: Sounds good. I want to be clear up front, Galileo needs full egress lockdown — we only allow a handful of vendor IPs and everything else blocked.
[00:33] Ethan: Totally understood. We’ve done pilots with that posture — we usually provide a compact IP range list and an alternative SNI-based allowlist for services that use ephemeral ranges.
[00:42] Priya: SNI-based allowlist is interesting — we currently proxy via a forced egress nat gateway + outbound proxy for TLS-inspection. Any caveats?
[00:51] Ethan: The caveat is intercepting TLS is intrusive; better to rely on mutual TLS or mTLS between your proxy and the Redwood Private nodes, or use private endpoints so there's no egress at all for model weights and inference traffic.
[01:03] Lina: We prefer private endpoints. Can Redwood expose a private NLB inside our VPC or do we need TGW/VPN peering?
[01:11] Maya: For the Private product we can do either: private NLB in customer VPC that talks to the Redwood control plane over an outbound-only, customer-initiated connection, or via Transit Gateway/peering if you want cross-account routing. We typically recommend private NLB + restricted outbound for control plane telemetry.
[01:26] Tomás: Control plane telemetry — explain what hops out.
[01:29] Ethan: Primarily health checks, metrics, and heartbeats to the Redwood control plane if you opt in. We also support a fully air-gapped mode where you run a local control plane; in that case you push us audit bundles instead of real-time telemetry.
[01:41] Priya: Air-gapped mode — what about model updates and kernel patches?
[01:44] Maya: That’s a manual pull model. We provide signed update artifacts that you can fetch via an isolated mechanism under change windows.
[01:50] Tomás: Ok. On KMS — we need customer-managed CMKs in our HSM, rotation controls, and audit trails for key usage. How does Redwood integrate?
[01:59] Ethan: We support envelope encryption with your CMK. The flow is: Redwood generates a data key, envelope-encrypts model artifacts and cache blobs, then stores the encrypted key. The CMK usage APIs are calls you can audit. For HSM-backed CMKs you supply the ARN and we call KMS-Fwd endpoints or use your on-prem HSM via the KMIP bridge.
[02:18] Lina: You mentioned KV cache — are cache entries encrypted with same keys? What about ephemeral / in-memory encryption?
[02:24] Ethan: Cache entries at rest are encrypted using envelope keys. In-memory cache is protected by process isolation and cannot be swapped to disk; for extra assurance we support memory locking and optional SEV attestation on supporting hardware.
[02:36] Priya: SEV attestation is interesting, what vendors do you support?
[02:40] Ethan: AMD SEV and Intel TDX in beta; can provide attestation flows and evidence for each host.
[02:46] Tomás: Audit logs: we need immutable, tamper-evident logs with at least three months retention, filtered by user/role, with SIEM push to our Splunk. Can Redwood do that?
[02:58] Maya: Yes — we stream structured audit events (who, what, where) in JSON via an HTTPS sink to your SIEM, or you can pull from object storage that we write to with write-once semantics. We also support signed log bundles for offline verification.
[03:12] Priya: Signed log bundles — how are they signed? And do you preserve chain-of-custody metadata?
[03:18] Ethan: Signed with a rotational Redwood signing key and optionally with a customer-wrapped signing key; each bundle includes sequence numbers and checksums. We can include SHA-256 hashes in the bundles and we publish a manifest so you can verify continuity.
[03:31] Lina: On SOC 2, we need a report and evidence of controls around key management and change control. Does Redwood have SOC 2 certs for Private as well?
[03:40] Maya: Redwood has SOC 2 Type II for the hosted product; for Private deployments we provide a SOC 2 mapping document and can provide artifacts from our internal control evidence. For true customer-specific attestation you may want to do an on-site assessment — we’re open to facilitating that.
[03:55] Tomás: Ok, and ISO 27001?
[03:58] Maya: We also have an ISO 27001 certificate for the control plane. For Private we document the shared responsibility matrix — things we control vs the customer.
[04:08] Priya: Shared responsibility — we need explicit language around firewalling, egress, and key custody.
[04:13] Maya: Agreed. In the doc we'll mark network perimeter and VPC controls as customer responsibilities, while Redwood handles host-level hardening, process isolation, and the signing of model artifacts.
[04:25] Tomás: What about RBAC and SSO? We use Okta, SCIM provisioning, and want least privilege.
[04:31] Ethan: Redwood supports SAML SSO with SCIM for user provisioning, role mapping, and fine-grained RBAC. Admin actions are logged in the audit feed and we support session duration limits and step-up MFA for sensitive ops.
[04:45] Priya: Can we map SCIM groups to Kubernetes namespaces or to model-level permissions?
[04:50] Ethan: Yes — you can map groups to roles like "model deployer", "ops reader", "admin". For namespace isolation we recommend separate VPC projects or separate clusters per trust boundary.
[05:01] Lina: Separate clusters — costy, but simpler for compliance. Any middle ground?
[05:05] Maya: A middle ground is tenant isolation with strict RBAC plus network policies — i.e., same cluster but node pools and network ACLs per tenant. We also support encryption keys per tenant to add a cryptographic separation.
[05:17] Tomás: About ingress — we can only accept incoming connections from internal IP ranges. Can we avoid public ingress entirely?
[05:24] Maya: Yes. We can put the service behind an internal NLB and have customer-side routing to it. If you need cross-region access, Transit Gateway or private peering can handle that without exposing public IPs.
[05:36] Priya: For egress allow lists, some cloud providers rotate service IP ranges often. How do you manage that for vendor dependencies (e.g., image registries)?
[05:44] Ethan: We publish minimal, stable IP ranges for Redwood control endpoints; for public image registries we recommend using a private registry mirror inside your VPC and pull during maintenance windows.
[05:54] Lina: Mirror makes sense. But we also need to run nightly canary tests that call out to a benchmarking endpoint on your side — will that be allowed?
[06:01] Maya: Yep — we can host a fixed endpoint for canary tests and give you a single FQDN/IP to whitelist.
[06:07] Tomás: I want to call out threat model: internal adversary, stolen credentials, compromised node — what mitigations are in place?
[06:15] Ethan: For stolen credentials we enforce short-lived credentials and integrate with your token service for ephemeral session tokens. For node compromise we have host attestation, mTLS between services, and the ability to evict nodes and rotate keys quickly.
[06:28] Priya: Do you support hardware attestation logs for forensic investigations?
[06:32] Ethan: Yes, attestation evidence can be collected and stored in the audit bundles.
[06:36] Lina: How about firewall rule drift — we need IaC that’s reviewable.
[06:41] Maya: We'll provide Terraform modules and CloudFormation snippets for the recommended NLB and security groups. Ethan will also provide example policies that you can run through your policy-as-code (OPA) checks.
[06:53] Tomás: Great. On the logging front, what fields do you emit for each request? We need user, role, request id, route, model version, token count, and outcome.
[07:03] Ethan: Our structured logs include request_id, user_id (or api_key id), role, model_id/version, tokens_in, tokens_out, latency_ms, and response_status. We also emit binary hashes for outputs if you need content fingerprinting.
[07:18] Priya: Fingerprinting — useful for data residency audits. Speaking of residency, we need to guarantee that inference input data and embeddings don't leave the EU region. Can Redwood pin to region?
[07:29] Maya: Yes — in Redwood Private you control where the compute runs; we can restrict deployment to specific regions and ensure no cross-region replication for cache or logs unless explicitly configured. We’ll capture that requirement in the pilot SOW.
[07:42] Tomás: For key rotation, do you support automatic re-encryption of stored artifacts? That’s a must for us.
[07:49] Ethan: We provide a rewrap operation: you can rotate the CMK and call a key rewrap process that re-encrypts envelope keys. We also support background rewrap jobs that are rate-limited to avoid IO storms.
[07:59] Lina: What about emergency key revocation — if a CMK is compromised, how fast can we invalidate data keys?
[08:06] Ethan: If a CMK is revoked the data keys that are encrypted with it become unreadable. We recommend multiple layers: have split- custody CMKs and keep the encrypted payloads immutable while you coordinate a full data restore plan. We can also destroy cache shards quickly to prevent further inferencing until keys are rotated.
[08:23] Priya: Does Redwood store any unencrypted plaintext anywhere (logs, metrics)?
[08:27] Maya: No plaintext request bodies are persisted. Transient memory contains inputs for inference while processing but not written to disk; logs and metrics are redacted by default and can be configured to exclude PII.
[08:39] Tomás: Is there a way to prove deletion of cached artifacts?
[08:43] Ethan: We have a deletion API that returns signed deletion receipts and we maintain immutable deletion manifests so you can prove a key/artefact was purged at a given timestamp.
[08:55] Lina: Can you walk through a typical egress rule set you'd propose for our setup?
[08:59] Ethan: Sure. Minimal example: Deny all outbound by default; allow outbound to Redwood control plane CIDR on ports 443/5222 (if using XMPP-style heartbeat), allow outbound to your private registry mirror, allow outbound to your SIEM endpoint, allow DNS to your internal resolvers. No direct public S3 access; object storage writes via VPC endpoint only.
[09:20] Priya: How do you handle DNS for private endpoints?
[09:23] Ethan: We can give you a private hosted zone delegation that resolves the private NLB FQDNs to internal IPs, or you can use conditional forwarding from your resolvers.
[09:33] Tomás: About pen tests — do you allow third-party assessments and how do you handle coordinate windows?
[09:38] Maya: Absolutely. We have a pen-test coordination process and a brief intake form. For Private we usually do it within your network, under your rules; Redwood provides a contact window and we can sign an NDA and red-team rules.
[09:50] Lina: Are there any known single points of failure in the private architecture?
[09:54] Ethan: Single points are generally in customer-managed infra (e.g., single NAT gateway). We recommend multiple AZ NLBs and redundant peering links. For storage we recommend cross-AZ replication within region but not cross-region unless you want that.
[10:08] Priya: On the console/ops side — we need token-level billing and tracing but in a way that doesn't leak request content — is that possible?
[10:16] Maya: Yes, we emit token-level cost metrics and per-route traces without embedding the prompt by default — trace records reference request_id and metadata only. Full recording is opt-in and encrypted.
[10:28] Tomás: Ok, can Redwood support a compliance log schema we can ingest into Splunk?
[10:34] Ethan: We have a Splunk HEC sink and a generic syslog/HTTPS JSON sink — we’ll provide the schema.
[10:40] Priya: A couple of low-level questions: do you require host OS versions or kernel modules for your runtime?
[10:44] Ethan: We publish a minimal host profile: Linux kernel >=5.10 with specific driver requirements for GPU hosts (NVIDIA driver + CUDA), and we provide an AMI-like image for cloud deployments. For on-prem we provide an installer that checks host prerequisites.
[10:58] Lina: Regarding encryption of ephemeral disks — do you support LUKS/dm-crypt or rely on cloud provider encryption?
[11:04] Ethan: We can support both. For cloud we rely on provider-managed encryption by default, but we also support dm-crypt/LUKS on instance boot for additional assurance.
[11:12] Tomás: How granular are the admin audit trails? We need to show who promoted a model and when, and any changes to key policy.
[11:18] Maya: Admin audit trails record user, role, action, target resource, old/new values where feasible, and request provenance. All of that is timestamped and included in the audit stream.
[11:29] Priya: About pipeline access — our CI system will need to push model artifacts. Can we use short-lived deploy tokens?
[11:35] Ethan: Yes, deploy tokens are short-lived and scoped. You can also do ephemeral sessions with your internal identity broker.
[11:41] Lina: Are there rate limits that might affect batch deployments?
[11:45] Ethan: For Private, you can configure throughput limits per cluster; for large batch imports we suggest a bootstrap window or backpressure-friendly ingestion.
[11:54] Tomás: We need a clear escalation path for security incidents. Who do we call?
[11:59] Maya: We'll provide a SOC contact and an on-call incident manager. For enterprise deals we'll agree on SLAs and an incident runbook.
[12:06] Priya: Can Redwood integrate with our ticketing system for incidents?
[12:09] Ethan: Yes, webhook or ticket creation via API integration is supported.
[12:13] Lina: For the pilot, we want to run a 6-week exercise with strict egress allow list, Splunk ingestion, and KMS CMK in our account. We also want to run a pen-test mid-pilot. Is that feasible?
[12:24] Maya: Absolutely — typical pilot is 4–8 weeks. We'll scope SOW to include those requirements.
[12:29] Tomás: Ok I have a question about immutable storage for audit logs. If logs are stored in S3, do you support object lock/legal hold?
[12:36] Ethan: Yes, we can write to an S3 bucket with Object Lock enabled and write-once read-many semantics; you control the retention mode and legal holds.
[12:46] Priya: One more on RBAC — do you offer separation of duties out-of-the-box? Eg, a user can't both approve deployments and rotate keys.
[12:54] Ethan: Yes, we have discrete roles and a policy engine that can block conflicting permissions. You can also add approval workflows for sensitive operations.
[13:03] Lina: Let's talk about network posture remediation. If we detect a node behaving oddly, can it be quarantined network-wise automatically?
[13:10] Ethan: Yes, we emit signals to your network controller and can automatically update security groups or route tables to isolate nodes; we support integration with NW automation tooling.
[13:20] Maya: Quick interjection — we should confirm the specific ports/protocols we'd expect from the pilot so your firewall team can pre-approve. Ethan will send a minimal rule set.
[13:30] Tomás: Good. Regarding the control plane key material — where is it stored?
[13:34] Ethan: Control plane secrets are stored in a KMS-backed secret store; for Private we can wire that to your KMS so you control the master key.
[13:42] Priya: Can you attest that control plane logs are separated per-customer?
[13:46] Maya: Yes, multi-tenant hosted control plane is logically separated; for Private you can run an isolated control plane entirely in your environment.
[13:55] Tomás: For governance, we need an evidence package for our auditors: architecture diagram, key rotation procedures, audit schema, and test logs. Can Redwood package that?
[14:03] Maya: Yes — we’ll assemble a compliance bundle tailored to the pilot, including architecture diagrams, procedure docs, and sample signed log bundles.
[14:11] Priya: Any limitations around SIEM throughput we should know about?
[14:15] Ethan: We’ve pushed to high-throughput SIEMs in other deployments; if you need sustained high-volume we recommend batching events and using compressed manifests to reduce cost.
[14:27] Tomás: Are there any known caveats with Splunk HEC tokens and retries? We need at-least-once semantics but dedupe on ingest.
[14:36] Ethan: We can emit idempotency keys per event so your SIEM can dedupe, and we do retries with backoff.
[14:45] Lina: Ok, let's recap next steps before we run out of time.
[14:49] Maya: Recap: Redwood will share SOC2/ISO mapping, firewall/IaC snippets, and a minimal IP/port list. Ethan will prepare sample firewall rules and the Splunk schema. Galileo will provide egress whitelist and retention requirements. We’ll aim to have the pilot SOW draft by next week.
[15:05] Tomás: I’ll add retention numbers — think 90–180 days for audit logs and 13 months for billing traces.
[15:13] Priya: We'll also enumerate the internal approvers for the pen-test.
[15:17] Maya: Great. Anything else?
[15:20] Lina: One last ask — can Redwood provide a runbook for key compromise scenarios?
[15:24] Maya: Yes, we'll include a key compromise runbook with recovery steps and timelines.
[15:29] Tomás: Thanks, that helps.
[15:32] Maya: Thanks everyone — we'll follow up with the artifacts.
[15:36] Priya: Appreciate it.
[15:38] Lina: Thanks all.

End of meeting (approx 65 min).

References shared during call:
- Redwood: private-deployment-whitepaper.pdf (link provided post-call)
- Redwood: SOC2-summary.pdf (link)
- Redwood: terraform-modules/example-nlb-security-groups.tf (link)

Notes about transcript quality: automated transcription with occasional speaker mislabels and minor transcription errors (networking acronyms sometimes garbled).
text:
Meeting header:
- Date/Time: 2025-12-02 15:00 PT
- Duration: ~65 minutes
- Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Lina Morales (Galileo Head of Infra), Tomás Rivera (Galileo Security Lead), Priya Desai (Galileo Cloud Architect)

Summary:
Quick technical deep-dive focused on private/VPC deployment security controls: zero-trust firewalling, egress restrictions, ingress patterns for private endpoints, KMS/HSM integration for envelope encryption, audit log collection and retention, SSO/SCIM for RBAC, and SOC 2 readiness checklist. Goal: agree on pilot scope and confirm integration points and required artifacts.

Topics covered:
- Private deployment architecture options (VPC-only vs air-gapped on-prem)
- Network isolation and zero-trust firewall strategy (egress strict allow list vs deny all + exception)
- Ingress patterns: private endpoints, load balancer vs service endpoints
- KMS integration and key management (envelope encryption, CMKs, HSM options)
- Audit logging: retention, SIEM integration, tamper-proofing
- RBAC/SSO: SAML/Okta, SCIM provisioning, admin audit trails
- SOC 2/ISO deliverables and timelines

Transcript:
[00:00] Maya: Hey everyone, thanks for joining — we can kick off in a minute, uh is this the right line for Priya?
[00:08] Priya: Yeah this is Priya, sorry had some calendar lag, on my side in SF office.
[00:11] Lina: Hi, Lina here, thanks. We pulled in Tomas because he owns the compliance questions.
[00:16] Maya: Perfect. Quick agenda: run through our recommended zero-trust firewall playbook for Redwood Private, talk KMS integration for keying, then audit logs and retention, and leave 15 for next steps. Sound good?
[00:25] Tomás: Sounds good. I want to be clear up front, Galileo needs full egress lockdown — we only allow a handful of vendor IPs and everything else blocked.
[00:33] Ethan: Totally understood. We’ve done pilots with that posture — we usually provide a compact IP range list and an alternative SNI-based allowlist for services that use ephemeral ranges.
[00:42] Priya: SNI-based allowlist is interesting — we currently proxy via a forced egress nat gateway + outbound proxy for TLS-inspection. Any caveats?
[00:51] Ethan: The caveat is intercepting TLS is intrusive; better to rely on mutual TLS or mTLS between your proxy and the Redwood Private nodes, or use private endpoints so there's no egress at all for model weights and inference traffic.
[01:03] Lina: We prefer private endpoints. Can Redwood expose a private NLB inside our VPC or do we need TGW/VPN peering?
[01:11] Maya: For the Private product we can do either: private NLB in customer VPC that talks to the Redwood control plane over an outbound-only, customer-initiated connection, or via Transit Gateway/peering if you want cross-account routing. We typically recommend private NLB + restricted outbound for control plane telemetry.
[01:26] Tomás: Control plane telemetry — explain what hops out.
[01:29] Ethan: Primarily health checks, metrics, and heartbeats to the Redwood control plane if you opt in. We also support a fully air-gapped mode where you run a local control plane; in that case you push us audit bundles instead of real-time telemetry.
[01:41] Priya: Air-gapped mode — what about model updates and kernel patches?
[01:44] Maya: That’s a manual pull model. We provide signed update artifacts that you can fetch via an isolated mechanism under change windows.
[01:50] Tomás: Ok. On KMS — we need customer-managed CMKs in our HSM, rotation controls, and audit trails for key usage. How does Redwood integrate?
[01:59] Ethan: We support envelope encryption with your CMK. The flow is: Redwood generates a data key, envelope-encrypts model artifacts and cache blobs, then stores the encrypted key. The CMK usage APIs are calls you can audit. For HSM-backed CMKs you supply the ARN and we call KMS-Fwd endpoints or use your on-prem HSM via the KMIP bridge.
[02:18] Lina: You mentioned KV cache — are cache entries encrypted with same keys? What about ephemeral / in-memory encryption?
[02:24] Ethan: Cache entries at rest are encrypted using envelope keys. In-memory cache is protected by process isolation and cannot be swapped to disk; for extra assurance we support memory locking and optional SEV attestation on supporting hardware.
[02:36] Priya: SEV attestation is interesting, what vendors do you support?
[02:40] Ethan: AMD SEV and Intel TDX in beta; can provide attestation flows and evidence for each host.
[02:46] Tomás: Audit logs: we need immutable, tamper-evident logs with at least three months retention, filtered by user/role, with SIEM push to our Splunk. Can Redwood do that?
[02:58] Maya: Yes — we stream structured audit events (who, what, where) in JSON via an HTTPS sink to your SIEM, or you can pull from object storage that we write to with write-once semantics. We also support signed log bundles for offline verification.
[03:12] Priya: Signed log bundles — how are they signed? And do you preserve chain-of-custody metadata?
[03:18] Ethan: Signed with a rotational Redwood signing key and optionally with a customer-wrapped signing key; each bundle includes sequence numbers and checksums. We can include SHA-256 hashes in the bundles and we publish a manifest so you can verify continuity.
[03:31] Lina: On SOC 2, we need a report and evidence of controls around key management and change control. Does Redwood have SOC 2 certs for Private as well?
[03:40] Maya: Redwood has SOC 2 Type II for the hosted product; for Private deployments we provide a SOC 2 mapping document and can provide artifacts from our internal control evidence. For true customer-specific attestation you may want to do an on-site assessment — we’re open to facilitating that.
[03:55] Tomás: Ok, and ISO 27001?
[03:58] Maya: We also have an ISO 27001 certificate for the control plane. For Private we document the shared responsibility matrix — things we control vs the customer.
[04:08] Priya: Shared responsibility — we need explicit language around firewalling, egress, and key custody.
[04:13] Maya: Agreed. In the doc we'll mark network perimeter and VPC controls as customer responsibilities, while Redwood handles host-level hardening, process isolation, and the signing of model artifacts.
[04:25] Tomás: What about RBAC and SSO? We use Okta, SCIM provisioning, and want least privilege.
[04:31] Ethan: Redwood supports SAML SSO with SCIM for user provisioning, role mapping, and fine-grained RBAC. Admin actions are logged in the audit feed and we support session duration limits and step-up MFA for sensitive ops.
[04:45] Priya: Can we map SCIM groups to Kubernetes namespaces or to model-level permissions?
[04:50] Ethan: Yes — you can map groups to roles like "model deployer", "ops reader", "admin". For namespace isolation we recommend separate VPC projects or separate clusters per trust boundary.
[05:01] Lina: Separate clusters — costy, but simpler for compliance. Any middle ground?
[05:05] Maya: A middle ground is tenant isolation with strict RBAC plus network policies — i.e., same cluster but node pools and network ACLs per tenant. We also support encryption keys per tenant to add a cryptographic separation.
[05:17] Tomás: About ingress — we can only accept incoming connections from internal IP ranges. Can we avoid public ingress entirely?
[05:24] Maya: Yes. We can put the service behind an internal NLB and have customer-side routing to it. If you need cross-region access, Transit Gateway or private peering can handle that without exposing public IPs.
[05:36] Priya: For egress allow lists, some cloud providers rotate service IP ranges often. How do you manage that for vendor dependencies (e.g., image registries)?
[05:44] Ethan: We publish minimal, stable IP ranges for Redwood control endpoints; for public image registries we recommend using a private registry mirror inside your VPC and pull during maintenance windows.
[05:54] Lina: Mirror makes sense. But we also need to run nightly canary tests that call out to a benchmarking endpoint on your side — will that be allowed?
[06:01] Maya: Yep — we can host a fixed endpoint for canary tests and give you a single FQDN/IP to whitelist.
[06:07] Tomás: I want to call out threat model: internal adversary, stolen credentials, compromised node — what mitigations are in place?
[06:15] Ethan: For stolen credentials we enforce short-lived credentials and integrate with your token service for ephemeral session tokens. For node compromise we have host attestation, mTLS between services, and the ability to evict nodes and rotate keys quickly.
[06:28] Priya: Do you support hardware attestation logs for forensic investigations?
[06:32] Ethan: Yes, attestation evidence can be collected and stored in the audit bundles.
[06:36] Lina: How about firewall rule drift — we need IaC that’s reviewable.
[06:41] Maya: We'll provide Terraform modules and CloudFormation snippets for the recommended NLB and security groups. Ethan will also provide example policies that you can run through your policy-as-code (OPA) checks.
[06:53] Tomás: Great. On the logging front, what fields do you emit for each request? We need user, role, request id, route, model version, token count, and outcome.
[07:03] Ethan: Our structured logs include request_id, user_id (or api_key id), role, model_id/version, tokens_in, tokens_out, latency_ms, and response_status. We also emit binary hashes for outputs if you need content fingerprinting.
[07:18] Priya: Fingerprinting — useful for data residency audits. Speaking of residency, we need to guarantee that inference input data and embeddings don't leave the EU region. Can Redwood pin to region?
[07:29] Maya: Yes — in Redwood Private you control where the compute runs; we can restrict deployment to specific regions and ensure no cross-region replication for cache or logs unless explicitly configured. We’ll capture that requirement in the pilot SOW.
[07:42] Tomás: For key rotation, do you support automatic re-encryption of stored artifacts? That’s a must for us.
[07:49] Ethan: We provide a rewrap operation: you can rotate the CMK and call a key rewrap process that re-encrypts envelope keys. We also support background rewrap jobs that are rate-limited to avoid IO storms.
[07:59] Lina: What about emergency key revocation — if a CMK is compromised, how fast can we invalidate data keys?
[08:06] Ethan: If a CMK is revoked the data keys that are encrypted with it become unreadable. We recommend multiple layers: have split- custody CMKs and keep the encrypted payloads immutable while you coordinate a full data restore plan. We can also destroy cache shards quickly to prevent further inferencing until keys are rotated.
[08:23] Priya: Does Redwood store any unencrypted plaintext anywhere (logs, metrics)?
[08:27] Maya: No plaintext request bodies are persisted. Transient memory contains inputs for inference while processing but not written to disk; logs and metrics are redacted by default and can be configured to exclude PII.
[08:39] Tomás: Is there a way to prove deletion of cached artifacts?
[08:43] Ethan: We have a deletion API that returns signed deletion receipts and we maintain immutable deletion manifests so you can prove a key/artefact was purged at a given timestamp.
[08:55] Lina: Can you walk through a typical egress rule set you'd propose for our setup?
[08:59] Ethan: Sure.
