Minimal example: Deny all outbound by default; allow outbound to Redwood control plane CIDR on ports 443/5222 (if using XMPP-style heartbeat), allow outbound to your private registry mirror, allow outbound to your SIEM endpoint, allow DNS to your internal resolvers. No direct public S3 access; object storage writes via VPC endpoint only.
[09:20] Priya: How do you handle DNS for private endpoints?
[09:23] Ethan: We can give you a private hosted zone delegation that resolves the private NLB FQDNs to internal IPs, or you can use conditional forwarding from your resolvers.
[09:33] Tomás: About pen tests — do you allow third-party assessments and how do you handle coordinate windows?
[09:38] Maya: Absolutely. We have a pen-test coordination process and a brief intake form. For Private we usually do it within your network, under your rules; Redwood provides a contact window and we can sign an NDA and red-team rules.
[09:50] Lina: Are there any known single points of failure in the private architecture?
[09:54] Ethan: Single points are generally in customer-managed infra (e.g., single NAT gateway). We recommend multiple AZ NLBs and redundant peering links. For storage we recommend cross-AZ replication within region but not cross-region unless you want that.
[10:08] Priya: On the console/ops side — we need token-level billing and tracing but in a way that doesn't leak request content — is that possible?
[10:16] Maya: Yes, we emit token-level cost metrics and per-route traces without embedding the prompt by default — trace records reference request_id and metadata only. Full recording is opt-in and encrypted.
[10:28] Tomás: Ok, can Redwood support a compliance log schema we can ingest into Splunk?
[10:34] Ethan: We have a Splunk HEC sink and a generic syslog/HTTPS JSON sink — we’ll provide the schema.
[10:40] Priya: A couple of low-level questions: do you require host OS versions or kernel modules for your runtime?
[10:44] Ethan: We publish a minimal host profile: Linux kernel >=5.10 with specific driver requirements for GPU hosts (NVIDIA driver + CUDA), and we provide an AMI-like image for cloud deployments. For on-prem we provide an installer that checks host prerequisites.
[10:58] Lina: Regarding encryption of ephemeral disks — do you support LUKS/dm-crypt or rely on cloud provider encryption?
[11:04] Ethan: We can support both. For cloud we rely on provider-managed encryption by default, but we also support dm-crypt/LUKS on instance boot for additional assurance.
[11:12] Tomás: How granular are the admin audit trails? We need to show who promoted a model and when, and any changes to key policy.
[11:18] Maya: Admin audit trails record user, role, action, target resource, old/new values where feasible, and request provenance. All of that is timestamped and included in the audit stream.
[11:29] Priya: About pipeline access — our CI system will need to push model artifacts. Can we use short-lived deploy tokens?
[11:35] Ethan: Yes, deploy tokens are short-lived and scoped. You can also do ephemeral sessions with your internal identity broker.
[11:41] Lina: Are there rate limits that might affect batch deployments?
[11:45] Ethan: For Private, you can configure throughput limits per cluster; for large batch imports we suggest a bootstrap window or backpressure-friendly ingestion.
[11:54] Tomás: We need a clear escalation path for security incidents. Who do we call?
[11:59] Maya: We'll provide a SOC contact and an on-call incident manager. For enterprise deals we'll agree on SLAs and an incident runbook.
[12:06] Priya: Can Redwood integrate with our ticketing system for incidents?
[12:09] Ethan: Yes, webhook or ticket creation via API integration is supported.
[12:13] Lina: For the pilot, we want to run a 6-week exercise with strict egress allow list, Splunk ingestion, and KMS CMK in our account. We also want to run a pen-test mid-pilot. Is that feasible?
[12:24] Maya: Absolutely — typical pilot is 4–8 weeks. We'll scope SOW to include those requirements.
[12:29] Tomás: Ok I have a question about immutable storage for audit logs. If logs are stored in S3, do you support object lock/legal hold?
[12:36] Ethan: Yes, we can write to an S3 bucket with Object Lock enabled and write-once read-many semantics; you control the retention mode and legal holds.
[12:46] Priya: One more on RBAC — do you offer separation of duties out-of-the-box? Eg, a user can't both approve deployments and rotate keys.
[12:54] Ethan: Yes, we have discrete roles and a policy engine that can block conflicting permissions. You can also add approval workflows for sensitive operations.
[13:03] Lina: Let's talk about network posture remediation. If we detect a node behaving oddly, can it be quarantined network-wise automatically?
[13:10] Ethan: Yes, we emit signals to your network controller and can automatically update security groups or route tables to isolate nodes; we support integration with NW automation tooling.
[13:20] Maya: Quick interjection — we should confirm the specific ports/protocols we'd expect from the pilot so your firewall team can pre-approve. Ethan will send a minimal rule set.
[13:30] Tomás: Good. Regarding the control plane key material — where is it stored?
[13:34] Ethan: Control plane secrets are stored in a KMS-backed secret store; for Private we can wire that to your KMS so you control the master key.
[13:42] Priya: Can you attest that control plane logs are separated per-customer?
[13:46] Maya: Yes, multi-tenant hosted control plane is logically separated; for Private you can run an isolated control plane entirely in your environment.
[13:55] Tomás: For governance, we need an evidence package for our auditors: architecture diagram, key rotation procedures, audit schema, and test logs. Can Redwood package that?
[14:03] Maya: Yes — we’ll assemble a compliance bundle tailored to the pilot, including architecture diagrams, procedure docs, and sample signed log bundles.
[14:11] Priya: Any limitations around SIEM throughput we should know about?
[14:15] Ethan: We’ve pushed to high-throughput SIEMs in other deployments; if you need sustained high-volume we recommend batching events and using compressed manifests to reduce cost.
[14:27] Tomás: Are there any known caveats with Splunk HEC tokens and retries? We need at-least-once semantics but dedupe on ingest.
[14:36] Ethan: We can emit idempotency keys per event so your SIEM can dedupe, and we do retries with backoff.
[14:45] Lina: Ok, let's recap next steps before we run out of time.
[14:49] Maya: Recap: Redwood will share SOC2/ISO mapping, firewall/IaC snippets, and a minimal IP/port list. Ethan will prepare sample firewall rules and the Splunk schema. Galileo will provide egress whitelist and retention requirements. We’ll aim to have the pilot SOW draft by next week.
[15:05] Tomás: I’ll add retention numbers — think 90–180 days for audit logs and 13 months for billing traces.
[15:13] Priya: We'll also enumerate the internal approvers for the pen-test.
[15:17] Maya: Great. Anything else?
[15:20] Lina: One last ask — can Redwood provide a runbook for key compromise scenarios?
[15:24] Maya: Yes, we'll include a key compromise runbook with recovery steps and timelines.
[15:29] Tomás: Thanks, that helps.
[15:32] Maya: Thanks everyone — we'll follow up with the artifacts.
[15:36] Priya: Appreciate it.
[15:38] Lina: Thanks all.

End of meeting (approx 65 min).

References shared during call:
- Redwood: private-deployment-whitepaper.pdf (link provided post-call)
- Redwood: SOC2-summary.pdf (link)
- Redwood: terraform-modules/example-nlb-security-groups.tf (link)

Notes about transcript quality: automated transcription with occasional speaker mislabels and minor transcription errors (networking acronyms sometimes garbled).

title: SpruceWare - Integration & Gateway Feasibility Call
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-11-20
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: 47m
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Lena Morales (CTO, SpruceWare), Ian Dupree (Platform Eng), Sara Kline (Security), Jai Nair (Product)
---
Summary:
SpruceWare is evaluating integration options (hosted vs dedicated vs private) for a customer-facing recommendations assistant with strict latency SLOs and data residency/security constraints. Focus areas: SDK support (Python/Node), LangChain/LlamaIndex compatibility, gateway/proxying patterns, rate limits/quotas and token billing, observability hooks (traces, token-level cost), and what changes are needed for a VPC/private deployment. Redwood AE + SE outlined architecture options, suggested a Dedicated POC for realistic load, and agreed to run benchmarks on SpruceWare sample prompts.
Topics: SDKs, LangChain & LlamaIndex adapters, gateway pattern (sidecar vs external proxy), rate limiting and quotas, observability and tracing hooks, KV/prefix cache behavior, VPC/Private control plane, security docs (SOC2 / KMS / SSO).
---
[00:00] Maya: Hey everyone, thanks for joining — you should all see my screen with the one-pager. Quick intros?
[00:14] Lena: Hi, Lena here, CTO at SpruceWare. We're building a recommendations assistant for product pages. Main constraints are 200ms p95 for short prompts and strict PCI-ish data residency, so interested in how Redwood can slot in.
[00:28] Ian: Ian, platform, I own infra and the gateway layer. Curious how we would route traffic and keep observability tied into our dashboards.
[00:35] Sara: Sara, security — I'll mostly focus on encryption at rest, KMS, and audit logging.
[00:42] Jai: Jai from product — mostly listening for integration friction.
[00:46] Maya: Great, I'm Maya AE and with me is Ethan Park, our SE. Ethan will dive into the nitty gritty.
[00:52] Ethan: Hey all — excited to walk through options. Quick clarifying question: you said 200ms p95 for short prompts, is that across all geos or US-east?
[01:05] Lena: Initially US-east, but we may expand. It's for the inline recommendation endpoint that sits in checkout flows. If we miss that, conversion drops.
[01:14] Ethan: Got it. That pushes toward lower-latency hosting — Dedicated or Private in-region. Hosted can sometimes hit that with small models and locality, but we should benchmark.
[01:26] Ian: Wanted to call out we have an existing API gateway (Kong) and we prefer not to rewrite routing logic. We usually put an external proxy in front of model endpoints. Can Redwood be put behind a proxy?
[01:40] Maya: Absolutely. Typical pattern: you can route through your Kong gateway to Redwood's hosted endpoint or to a dedicated/private endpoint in your VPC. Two common patterns: 1) external gateway forwards to Redwood; 2) sidecar per service that talks internally.
[01:54] Ian: Any latency hit with that proxy hop?
[01:58] Ethan: Minimal — round trip for HTTP is small relative to token generation, but adds microseconds. The bigger variable is KV cache hit-rate and batching. Want us to explain KV cache and prefix caching?
[02:12] Lena: Yes please, because we may use prefix caching for product descriptions that repeat a lot.
[02:18] Ethan: Sure. Redwood's runtime uses prefix/KV caches to avoid recomputing the initial context, so if you have a lot of similar prompts we save tokens and latency. Caveat: caching is most effective when prompts are near-identical; for personalized prompts we see less benefit. Also our cache keys are configurable but sometimes show up in transcribed docs as K-V cash (laughs).
[02:40] Ian: (laughs) I saw that in another transcript.
[02:44] [03:00] Maya: On SDKs — we have first-class Python and Node SDKs, plus a lightweight HTTP wrapper. For LangChain and LlamaIndex, we provide adapters and examples; Ethan, maybe run through the LangChain flow?
[03:16] Ethan: Yeah. With LangChain you point the LLM wrapper at our endpoint using the SDK. We have a RedwoodLLM class in the examples repo that implements generate/stream semantics and exposes token usage counters; LlamaIndex connectors are similar. Two notes: streaming behavior requires websocket or SSE, which our SDK handles. If you use an external gateway, ensure it preserves chunked responses.
[03:44] Ian: We do chunked responses currently; good. Does your adapter support tool-calling / function calling semantics?
[03:50] Ethan: Yes — function calling is supported via structured output. Our SDK serializes function call payloads and supports automatic fallback to non-function mode if schema validation fails. There are some quirks with LangChain's tool wrappers — we've got a sample repo for SpruceWare we'll share.
[04:06] Lena: On quotas — we charge per-token on Hosted; Dedicated is reserved capacity with predictable throughput. How do you enforce rate limits / quotas from our side? We have downstream services that mustn't be overwhelmed.
[04:20] Maya: Good question. You get two layers: Redwood enforces token and request quotas per API key; you can also configure per-route throttles. On Dedicated we can give you an allocation and you can set hard caps, plus Redwood Console exposes per-key usage breakdowns.
[04:36] Ian: Can we surface cost per route into our internal billing? We need to attribute cost to product teams.
[04:43] Ethan: Yes — Redwood emits token-level usage and cost attribution metadata that you can pull via our audit API or integrate into the Console's export. We also provide tracing headers to correlate with your request IDs.
[04:58] Sara: Encryption / KMS — in Private deployments where customer manages their own KMS, do keys live in their account? And do you keep any plaintext logs?
[05:08] Maya: For Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments, the customer manages the control plane and keys remain in the customer's cloud account; Redwood's control plane can be configured to never see plaintext. For Hosted and Dedicated we offer envelope encryption with customer-managed KMS options. Audit logs do not contain full prompt text unless enabled; we can redact or store hashes. We'll share the SOC2 and encryption notes.
[05:29] Sara: Good. We need to see the SOC2 report and details on retention and ability to purge.
[05:35] Maya: We'll attach that in the follow-up.
[05:40] [06:00] Ian: Back to integration patterns — if we put Redwood behind Kong, do we need to handle tokenization or do you accept raw text?
[06:09] Ethan: You send raw text. Tokenization and cost estimation happen server-side. If you need pre-flight cost estimation we provide an endpoint to estimate token usage for a prompt. That helps with quotas.
[06:21] Jai: Are there SDK features to do client-side batching? We have several callers that could batch small requests.
[06:29] Ethan: We recommend server-side batching in Redwood runtime — continuous batching at the runtime level is more efficient. Client-side batching is possible via our SDK but can increase tail latency. If you're trying to minimize token cost but keep latency, server batching + prefix cache is the sweet spot.
[06:46] Lena: For models — we have a constraint to avoid closed APIs sometimes due to vendor lock-in. What models do you host?
[06:55] Maya: We offer a curated catalog: open models and optimized variants. For strict latency you can bring quantized small models or pick one of our Redwood-verified low-latency models. Dedicated gives you license to pin versions and deploy locally.
[07:10] [07:30] Ethan: I want to call out an architecture we often recommend: put a lightweight gateway in your VPC that does auth, routing, and request shaping, then route to Redwood Dedicated in the same region. That minimizes cross-region hops and keeps control in your stack.
[07:44] Ian: Sounds reasonable. What about observability hooks — do you have tracing headers we can ingest into Datadog?
[07:52] Ethan: Yes — we propagate X-Request-Id and X-Trace-Id you can attach to your requests; Redwood Console can also export Spans and token-level metadata. We'll share an example of the trace-to-cost correlation we use. There can be minor naming differences in the SDK trace fields compared to OpenTelemetry, but we have a mapping guide.
[08:13] Sara: For audit logs, do you provide immutable event logs? We need to show access to prompts for compliance audits.
[08:22] Maya: Audit logs are immutable and exportable; for Hosted you can set retention and redaction policies. For Private deployments logs can live entirely in your VPC.
[08:32] [08:45] Lena: Can we talk about failover? If Dedicated capacity is saturated, how does Redwood fallback?
[08:51] Ethan: We support policy-based routing — automatic fallback to hosted or smaller model variants under load. You can set SLOs that prefer latency vs cost. For mission-critical flows, we recommend a small buffer of burst capacity.
[09:05] Ian: How are quotas billed during fallback? If we fallback to hosted and it costs more, does that come back to our Dedicated allocation or get billed separately?
[09:16] Maya: Billing is configurable — you can choose to have failover consumption drawn from the same commitment or billed as overage. We'll surface this in the pricing sheet.
[09:27] [09:45] Jai: Regarding LangChain — in some of our flows we use a retriever that calls into an embeddings pipeline. Does Redwood support managing embed pipelines or should we keep that internal?
[09:58] Ethan: You can do both. Hosted Redwood supports embeddings endpoints; we also provide connector examples for offloading vector stores to your infra (e.g., Weaviate, Pinecone). If latency is critical we often suggest co-locating the vector store in the same VPC as the model instance.
[10:15] Lena: We run Weaviate currently in our cluster. Is there any special networking required?
[10:21] Ethan: For Private/Dedicated in-VPC, networking can be set up so the model nodes access your Weaviate endpoint directly. For Hosted you can use secure peering or a proxy. We'll diagram options.
[10:34] [10:45] Ian: A lot of this looks good. On rate limiting — can we apply per-user rate limits (per customer id) at Redwood layer or do we need to manage that upstream?
[10:57] Ethan: You can configure token quotas per API key which you could map to a customer; for fine-grained per-user rate limits we usually recommend upstream enforcement combined with Redwood per-key quotas.
[11:10] Sara: Two security items — SSO for admin console and RBAC for team members. Can Redwood integrate with our SAML provider?
[11:18] Maya: Yes, SAML/SSO with SCIM provisioning is supported. We support role-based access in Console and can map groups to roles. For Private deployments you can use your identity provider directly.
[11:30] [11:45] Ethan: Quick demo snippet — I'll show a LangChain example with our SDK (Ethan shares code).
[11:50] Ethan: (screen) import redwood, client = redwood.Client(api_key=...); llm = redwood.LangChainWrapper(...). The key things: set stream=True for SSE, attach trace header, pass metadata:{"customer_id": ...} to help with cost-attribution.
[12:12] Ian: That metadata is picked up in Console?
[12:15] Ethan: Yes — we index free-form metadata fields and you can filter in the UI.
[12:20] [12:45] Lena: Any gotchas with LlamaIndex? We rely on a particular tokenizer pipeline.
[12:52] Ethan: LlamaIndex will work with our generate API, but watch out for token counting differences. We expose a token_count endpoint you can use to reconcile. Also when streaming with LlamaIndex you might need to buffer and re-emit chunks — sample code will show the pattern.
[13:10] Jai: If we decide to do a POC, what does the POC look like? Time boxed? What deliverables?
[13:19] Maya: Typically 2–3 weeks: baseline benchmark, integration using your gateway, and a performance run under representative traffic. SE and solutions eng will set up the test plan. We'll provide a POC checklist.
[13:31] [13:45] Ian: On the network side — for Private deployment, do we get a control plane in our VPC, or do you run the control plane and we host inference nodes?
[13:54] Ethan: We offer two patterns: 1) Private control plane — Redwood control plane runs but talk is via a management plane with your VPC; 2) Fully self-hosted on-prem package where you run both. For high compliance we recommend Private with customer-managed control plane and keys.
[14:10] Sara: For fully self-hosted, what is the upgrade cadence and who manages patching?
[14:16] Ethan: For self-hosted we give an ops bundle and recommend a managed upgrade window; Redwood can assist via playbooks. For Private with Redwood-managed control plane we handle control plane upgrades and you manage inference nodes.
[14:28] [14:45] Maya: Quick question for you all — what are your top success criteria for selecting a provider?
[14:52] Lena: Performance (p95 < 200ms), observable cost per route, and security posture (KMS, SOC2, SSO).
[15:02] Ian: Low integration lift — don't want to rewrite our gateway or retrievers.
[15:07] Sara: Auditable logs with redaction controls.
[15:11] Jai: Time to market — we want to launch an MVP within 6 weeks.
[15:16] Ethan: Understood. We usually recommend starting with Dedicated for that kind of SLO and time box a 2-week POC to validate latency and tracing.
[15:26] [15:45] Ian: Pricing question — Dedicated commit levels typically cover what throughput? For our flows, we expect ~250 requests per second peak with variable token lengths.
[15:39] Maya: We'll propose a few commit tiers. Dedicated can be configured for sustained RPS; we size by tokens/sec and concurrency. We'll include burst capacity options for spikes.
[15:50] Ethan: Also note quantization options; smaller quantized models cut GPU footprint which lowers cost and often improves latency for short sequences. There's a tradeoff for output quality.
[16:00] [16:30] Lena: Any references or case studies for retail workloads?
[16:36] Maya: We'll share a reference where a customer used Dedicated + local Weaviate and hit 180ms p95 for recommendations. We'll strip identifying details.
[16:48] Ian: On the security checklist — can you support VPC peering and private link?
[16:55] Ethan: Yes, both are supported depending on region and deployment. For Hosted we offer private link/peering options; Dedicated in-region is often simpler.
[17:05] Sara: About logs again — if we redact prompts, can we still do quality monitoring? We need to catch regressions in model behavior.
[17:15] Ethan: We have a dual approach: store hashed prompts for traceability and store a sampled plaintext corpus for quality evaluations with strict access controls. Redwood Optimize ties quality monitoring to prompt sets and evals; we can hook that into your workflow.
[17:31] [17:45] Jai: If we instrument everything, how much data egress should we expect? We have concerns about egress costs for frequent tracing exports.
[17:56] Ethan: Trace exports are compact; token payloads are the main egress. If you keep embeddings and model nodes co-located you reduce egress. We'll include estimates in the follow-up.
[18:10] [18:30] Maya: Quick recap, Ethan — what are the recommended next steps?
[18:36] Ethan: 1) SpruceWare sends sample prompts, API traces, and SLO definitions. 2) Redwood runs an initial bench against Hosted and a Dedicated config using SpruceWare corpus. 3) We deliver a POC runbook and pricing options. 4) Security artifacts delivered to Sara.
[18:54] Lena: Sounds good. For the benchmark, can you simulate Kong in front to reflect our routing?
[19:00] Ethan: Yes — we can script the gateway behavior to mimic request shaping and headers.
[19:08] [19:30] (short crosstalk)
[19:36] Ian: One small question — do you have baked-in token bucket rate-limiting with leaky-bucket behavior or is it more coarse?
[19:46] Ethan: We support token-bucket semantics with configurable refill rates and burst sizes. You can tune for per-route throttle.
[19:56] Sara: On authentication — do we need to rotate API keys frequently?
[20:02] Maya: We support short-lived API keys and key rotation via the Console and API. For Private deployments you can integrate with your KMS.
[20:12] [20:30] Ethan: I'll show a quick example of how a sidecar proxy + Redwood Dedicated flow looks on the network diagram (Ethan screens diagram).
[20:37] Ethan: (describing) Service -> Local sidecar (auth, short-circuit cache) -> Kong -> Redwood Dedicated endpoint in VPC -> model nodes. Sidecar can hit a local in-memory prefix cache to shave off 10–30ms for super common requests.
[20:57] Ian: That sidecar sounds useful. Is it open-source?
[21:03] Ethan: We have a reference sidecar repo under MIT that teams can adapt.
[21:08] [21:30] Jai: Any constraints around content filtering and moderation? We need to avoid PII leaks.
[21:37] Maya: Redwood has content filtering modules and optional pre/post-processing hooks where you can run custom scrubbing. For PII, we recommend a pre-flight scrub on your side and metadata-only logging on Redwood.
[21:51] Sara: For compliance, we'd like an attestation that prompts are not used to train public models.
[21:58] Maya: Redwood does not use customer prompts to train public models unless explicit opt-in. We'll include the policy text.
[22:07] [22:30] Ethan: Quick note on quotas vs throttles — quotas are long-term allotments (tokens/month) while throttles are runtime rate-limits. Both are available via Console and API.
[22:45] Ian: If we map a customer id to API key, and they do a bursty attack, is the key-level throttle sufficient?
[22:55] Ethan: Yes — combine upstream WAF + Kong rules + Redwood per-key throttle and you'll be covered. We'll collaborate on a recommended configuration.
[23:08] [23:30] (brief pause)
[23:40] Lena: One more question — for embeddings, we might want to run offline batch jobs as well as real-time. Does Redwood support batch embed jobs?
[23:50] Ethan: Yes — we have a batch embeddings API and a streaming embeddings endpoint. Batch jobs can be scheduled via our jobs API; results can be stored back to your vector DB.
[24:03] Jai: What's the SLA for Dedicated?
[24:10] Maya: We offer SLA-backed uptime and latency targets on Dedicated contracts. Specific numbers depend on commit level; we'll include those in the pricing doc.
[24:22] [24:45] Ian: For the POC, will Redwood provision temporary Dedicated nodes or use Hosted with pinned models?
[24:56] Ethan: Usually we spin up a small Dedicated pool (short-term) to emulate production and give isolated capacity. That gives more realistic latency than Hosted shared capacity.
[25:08] Sara: Regarding logging storage — we need to insist logs are only stored in our region. Can that be guaranteed?
[25:18] Maya: Yes — for Dedicated/Private region residency is configurable. Hosted has region-based retention options; for strict needs Private is safest.
[25:30] [25:45] Ethan: Wanted to address a practical integration note: if you use LangChain + Retriever pipeline, beware of double-tokenization when passing embeddings back into a prompt. We provide helper functions to reconcile token counts.
[26:00] Ian: Good to know.
[26:05] [26:30] Jai: Any constraints on the language support? We have multilingual prompts.
[26:38] Ethan: Models in our catalog support many languages; quality varies. For mission-critical multilingual flows, run targeted evals — Redwood Optimize can help recommend model variants per language.
[26:55] Lena: What's the expected time to get the POC started if we hand over traces today?
[27:02] Maya: If we get artifacts by Monday we can start benchmark scripts mid-next week and have initial results in ~7 business days.
[27:12] [27:30] Ian: A few operational questions — how does autoscaling behave with sudden spikes?
[27:40] Ethan: Dedicated autoscaling can be configured for target CPU/GPU utilization and can provision extra nodes, but there's a spin-up delay for cold GPUs — hence burst capacity is recommended. For sub-second ramps we use pre-warmed workers.
[27:55] Sara: Does Redwood provide node-level logs for forensic analysis or only aggregated logs?
[28:02] Ethan: Both. You get aggregated Console logs and can opt into node-level dumps for investigation with proper access controls.
[28:12] [28:30] Maya: We're close to time — any last-minute technical blockers?
[28:36] Ian: None that I can think of — mostly need to see perf numbers.
[28:42] Lena: And the SOC2 + retention policy.
[28:46] Sara: Yes.
[28:50] Maya: Great. We'll follow up with the POC plan and security bundle.
[28:55] [29:15] (wrap up)
[29:22] Lena: Thanks everyone — we look forward to the benchmark.
[29:26] Maya: Thanks — we'll share next steps & Doodle for POC kickoff.
[29:30] Ian: Thanks.
[29:33] (meeting ends)

Notes on transcription: occasional mis-hearings (e.g., "K-V cash" for KV cache), speaker label drift around 19:30 where an interruption was briefly attributed to "Speaker 3" in the auto transcript. Recording length aligns with stated duration 47 minutes though only first 30 minutes are transcribed in detail; additional follow-up chat existed in our side channel.

Attachments/references mentioned:
- Redwood Dedicated pricing sheet (to be attached)
- SOC2 + encryption/KMS summary (to be attached)
- Example LangChain/LlamaIndex adapters repo (link will be shared)
- POC runbook template (link will be shared)

End of transcript.
text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-11-20
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: 47m
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Lena Morales (CTO, SpruceWare), Ian Dupree (Platform Eng), Sara Kline (Security), Jai Nair (Product)
---
Summary:
SpruceWare is evaluating integration options (hosted vs dedicated vs private) for a customer-facing recommendations assistant with strict latency SLOs and data residency/security constraints. Focus areas: SDK support (Python/Node), LangChain/LlamaIndex compatibility, gateway/proxying patterns, rate limits/quotas and token billing, observability hooks (traces, token-level cost), and what changes are needed for a VPC/private deployment. Redwood AE + SE outlined architecture options, suggested a Dedicated POC for realistic load, and agreed to run benchmarks on SpruceWare sample prompts.
Topics: SDKs, LangChain & LlamaIndex adapters, gateway pattern (sidecar vs external proxy), rate limiting and quotas, observability and tracing hooks, KV/prefix cache behavior, VPC/Private control plane, security docs (SOC2 / KMS / SSO).
---
[00:00] Maya: Hey everyone, thanks for joining — you should all see my screen with the one-pager. Quick intros?
[00:14] Lena: Hi, Lena here, CTO at SpruceWare. We're building a recommendations assistant for product pages. Main constraints are 200ms p95 for short prompts and strict PCI-ish data residency, so interested in how Redwood can slot in.
[00:28] Ian: Ian, platform, I own infra and the gateway layer. Curious how we would route traffic and keep observability tied into our dashboards.
[00:35] Sara: Sara, security — I'll mostly focus on encryption at rest, KMS, and audit logging.
[00:42] Jai: Jai from product — mostly listening for integration friction.
[00:46] Maya: Great, I'm Maya AE and with me is Ethan Park, our SE. Ethan will dive into the nitty gritty.
[00:52] Ethan: Hey all — excited to walk through options. Quick clarifying question: you said 200ms p95 for short prompts, is that across all geos or US-east?
[01:05] Lena: Initially US-east, but we may expand. It's for the inline recommendation endpoint that sits in checkout flows. If we miss that, conversion drops.
[01:14] Ethan: Got it. That pushes toward lower-latency hosting — Dedicated or Private in-region. Hosted can sometimes hit that with small models and locality, but we should benchmark.
[01:26] Ian: Wanted to call out we have an existing API gateway (Kong) and we prefer not to rewrite routing logic. We usually put an external proxy in front of model endpoints. Can Redwood be put behind a proxy?
[01:40] Maya: Absolutely. Typical pattern: you can route through your Kong gateway to Redwood's hosted endpoint or to a dedicated/private endpoint in your VPC. Two common patterns: 1) external gateway forwards to Redwood; 2) sidecar per service that talks internally.
[01:54] Ian: Any latency hit with that proxy hop?
[01:58] Ethan: Minimal — round trip for HTTP is small relative to token generation, but adds microseconds. The bigger variable is KV cache hit-rate and batching. Want us to explain KV cache and prefix caching?
[02:12] Lena: Yes please, because we may use prefix caching for product descriptions that repeat a lot.
[02:18] Ethan: Sure. Redwood's runtime uses prefix/KV caches to avoid recomputing the initial context, so if you have a lot of similar prompts we save tokens and latency. Caveat: caching is most effective when prompts are near-identical; for personalized prompts we see less benefit. Also our cache keys are configurable but sometimes show up in transcribed docs as K-V cash (laughs).
[02:40] Ian: (laughs) I saw that in another transcript.
[02:44] [03:00] Maya: On SDKs — we have first-class Python and Node SDKs, plus a lightweight HTTP wrapper. For LangChain and LlamaIndex, we provide adapters and examples; Ethan, maybe run through the LangChain flow?
[03:16] Ethan: Yeah. With LangChain you point the LLM wrapper at our endpoint using the SDK. We have a RedwoodLLM class in the examples repo that implements generate/stream semantics and exposes token usage counters; LlamaIndex connectors are similar. Two notes: streaming behavior requires websocket or SSE, which our SDK handles. If you use an external gateway, ensure it preserves chunked responses.
[03:44] Ian: We do chunked responses currently; good. Does your adapter support tool-calling / function calling semantics?
[03:50] Ethan: Yes — function calling is supported via structured output. Our SDK serializes function call payloads and supports automatic fallback to non-function mode if schema validation fails. There are some quirks with LangChain's tool wrappers — we've got a sample repo for SpruceWare we'll share.
[04:06] Lena: On quotas — we charge per-token on Hosted; Dedicated is reserved capacity with predictable throughput. How do you enforce rate limits / quotas from our side? We have downstream services that mustn't be overwhelmed.
[04:20] Maya: Good question. You get two layers: Redwood enforces token and request quotas per API key; you can also configure per-route throttles. On Dedicated we can give you an allocation and you can set hard caps, plus Redwood Console exposes per-key usage breakdowns.
[04:36] Ian: Can we surface cost per route into our internal billing? We need to attribute cost to product teams.
[04:43] Ethan: Yes — Redwood emits token-level usage and cost attribution metadata that you can pull via our audit API or integrate into the Console's export. We also provide tracing headers to correlate with your request IDs.
[04:58] Sara: Encryption / KMS — in Private deployments where customer manages their own KMS, do keys live in their account? And do you keep any plaintext logs?
[05:08] Maya: For Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments, the customer manages the control plane and keys remain in the customer's cloud account; Redwood's control plane can be configured to never see plaintext. For Hosted and Dedicated we offer envelope encryption with customer-managed KMS options. Audit logs do not contain full prompt text unless enabled; we can redact or store hashes. We'll share the SOC2 and encryption notes.
[05:29] Sara: Good. We need to see the SOC2 report and details on retention and ability to purge.
[05:35] Maya: We'll attach that in the follow-up.
[05:40] [06:00] Ian: Back to integration patterns — if we put Redwood behind Kong, do we need to handle tokenization or do you accept raw text?
[06:09] Ethan: You send raw text. Tokenization and cost estimation happen server-side. If you need pre-flight cost estimation we provide an endpoint to estimate token usage for a prompt. That helps with quotas.
[06:21] Jai: Are there SDK features to do client-side batching? We have several callers that could batch small requests.
[06:29] Ethan: We recommend server-side batching in Redwood runtime — continuous batching at the runtime level is more efficient. Client-side batching is possible via our SDK but can increase tail latency. If you're trying to minimize token cost but keep latency, server batching + prefix cache is the sweet spot.
[06:46] Lena: For models — we have a constraint to avoid closed APIs sometimes due to vendor lock-in. What models do you host?
[06:55] Maya: We offer a curated catalog: open models and optimized variants. For strict latency you can bring quantized small models or pick one of our Redwood-verified low-latency models. Dedicated gives you license to pin versions and deploy locally.
[07:10] [07:30] Ethan: I want to call out an architecture we often recommend: put a lightweight gateway in your VPC that does auth, routing, and request shaping, then route to Redwood Dedicated in the same region. That minimizes cross-region hops and keeps control in your stack.
[07:44] Ian: Sounds reasonable. What about observability hooks — do you have tracing headers we can ingest into Datadog?
[07:52] Ethan: Yes — we propagate X-Request-Id and X-Trace-Id you can attach to your requests; Redwood Console can also export Spans and token-level metadata. We'll share an example of the trace-to-cost correlation we use. There can be minor naming differences in the SDK trace fields compared to OpenTelemetry, but we have a mapping guide.
[08:13] Sara: For audit logs, do you provide immutable event logs? We need to show access to prompts for compliance audits.
[08:22] Maya: Audit logs are immutable and exportable; for Hosted you can set retention and redaction policies. For Private deployments logs can live entirely in your VPC.
[08:32] [08:45] Lena: Can we talk about failover? If Dedicated capacity is saturated, how does Redwood fallback?
[08:51] Ethan: We support policy-based routing — automatic fallback to hosted or smaller model variants under load. You can set SLOs that prefer latency vs cost. For mission-critical flows, we recommend a small buffer of burst capacity.
[09:05] Ian: How are quotas billed during fallback? If we fallback to hosted and it costs more, does that come back to our Dedicated allocation or get billed separately?
[09:16] Maya: Billing is configurable — you can choose to have failover consumption drawn from the same commitment or billed as overage. We'll surface this in the pricing sheet.
[09:27] [09:45] Jai: Regarding LangChain — in some of our flows we use a retriever that calls into an embeddings pipeline. Does Redwood support managing embed pipelines or should we keep that internal?
[09:58] Ethan: You can do both. Hosted Redwood supports embeddings endpoints; we also provide connector examples for offloading vector stores to your infra (e.g., Weaviate, Pinecone). If latency is critical we often suggest co-locating the vector store in the same VPC as the model instance.
[10:15] Lena: We run Weaviate currently in our cluster. Is there any special networking required?
[10:21] Ethan: For Private/Dedicated in-VPC, networking can be set up so the model nodes access your Weaviate endpoint directly. For Hosted you can use secure peering or a proxy. We'll diagram options.
[10:34] [10:45] Ian: A lot of this looks good. On rate limiting — can we apply per-user rate limits (per customer id) at Redwood layer or do we need to manage that upstream?
[10:57] Ethan: You can configure token quotas per API key which you could map to a customer; for fine-grained per-user rate limits we usually recommend upstream enforcement combined with Redwood per-key quotas.
[11:10] Sara: Two security items — SSO for admin console and RBAC for team members. Can Redwood integrate with our SAML provider?
[11:18] Maya: Yes, SAML/SSO with SCIM provisioning is supported. We support role-based access in Console and can map groups to roles. For Private deployments you can use your identity provider directly.
[11:30] [11:45] Ethan: Quick demo snippet — I'll show a LangChain example with our SDK (Ethan shares code).
[11:50] Ethan: (screen) import redwood, client = redwood.Client(api_key=...); llm = redwood.LangChainWrapper(...). The key things: set stream=True for SSE, attach trace header, pass metadata:{"customer_id": ...} to help with cost-attribution.
[12:12] Ian: That metadata is picked up in Console?
[12:15] Ethan: Yes — we index free-form metadata fields and you can filter in the UI.
[12:20] [12:45] Lena: Any gotchas with LlamaIndex? We rely on a particular tokenizer pipeline.
[12:52] Ethan: LlamaIndex will work with our generate API, but watch out for token counting differences. We expose a token_count endpoint you can use to reconcile. Also when streaming with LlamaIndex you might need to buffer and re-emit chunks — sample code will show the pattern.
[13:10] Jai: If we decide to do a POC, what does the POC look like? Time boxed? What deliverables?
[13:19] Maya: Typically 2–3 weeks: baseline benchmark, integration using your gateway, and a performance run under representative traffic. SE and solutions eng will set up the test plan. We'll provide a POC checklist.
[13:31] [13:45] Ian: On the network side — for Private deployment, do we get a control plane in our VPC, or do you run the control plane and we host inference nodes?
[13:54] Ethan: We offer two patterns: 1) Private control plane — Redwood control plane runs but talk is via a management plane with your VPC; 2) Fully self-hosted on-prem package where you run both. For high compliance we recommend Private with customer-managed control plane and keys.
[14:10] Sara: For fully self-hosted, what is the upgrade cadence and who manages patching?
[14:16] Ethan: For self-hosted we give an ops bundle and recommend a managed upgrade window; Redwood can assist via playbooks. For Private with Redwood-managed control plane we handle control plane upgrades and you manage inference nodes.
[14:28] [14:45] Maya: Quick question for you all — what are your top success criteria for selecting a provider?
[14:52] Lena: Performance (p95 < 200ms), observable cost per route, and security posture (KMS, SOC2, SSO).
[15:02] Ian: Low integration lift — don't want to rewrite our gateway or retrievers.
[15:07] Sara: Auditable logs with redaction controls.

[15:16] Ethan: Understood. We usually recommend starting with Dedicated for that kind of SLO and time box a 2-week POC to validate latency and tracing.

[15:39] Maya: We'll propose a few commit tiers. Dedicated can be configured for sustained RPS; we size by tokens/sec and concurrency. We'll include burst capacity options for spikes.

[16:55] Ethan: Yes, both are supported depending on region and deployment. For Hosted we offer private link/peering options; Dedicated in-region is often simpler.
[17:05] Sara: About logs again — if we redact prompts, can we still do quality monitoring? We need to catch regressions in model behavior.
[17:15] Ethan: We have a dual approach: store hashed prompts for traceability and store a sampled plaintext corpus for quality evaluations with strict access controls. Redwood Optimize ties quality monitoring to prompt sets and evals; we can hook that into your workflow.
[17:31] [17:45] Jai: If we instrument everything, how much data egress should we expect? We have concerns about egress costs for frequent tracing exports.
[17:56] Ethan: Trace exports are compact; token payloads are the main egress. If you keep embeddings and model nodes co-located you reduce egress. We'll include estimates in the follow-up.

[18:36] Ethan: 1) SpruceWare sends sample prompts, API traces, and SLO definitions. 2) Redwood runs an initial bench against Hosted and a Dedicated config using SpruceWare corpus. 3) We deliver a POC runbook and pricing options. 4) Security artifacts delivered to Sara.

[22:45] Ian: If we map a customer id to API key, and they do a bursty attack, is the key-level throttle sufficient?

[23:50] Ethan: Yes — we have a batch embeddings API and a streaming embeddings endpoint. Batch jobs can be scheduled via our jobs API; results can be stored back to your vector DB.

[24:10] Maya: We offer SLA-backed uptime and latency targets on Dedicated contracts. Specific numbers depend on commit level; we'll include those in the pricing doc.
[24:22] [24:45] Ian: For the POC, will Redwood provision temporary Dedicated nodes or use Hosted with pinned models?
[24:56] Ethan: Usually we spin up a small Dedicated pool (short-term) to emulate production and give isolated capacity. That gives more realistic latency than Hosted shared capacity.
[25:08] Sara: Regarding logging storage — we need to insist logs are only stored in our region. Can that be guaranteed?
[25:18] Maya: Yes — for Dedicated/Private region residency is configurable. Hosted has region-based retention options; for strict needs Private is safest.
[25:30] [25:45] Ethan: Wanted to address a practical integration note: if you use LangChain + Retriever pipeline, beware of double-tokenization when passing embeddings back into a prompt. We provide helper functions to reconcile token counts.
[26:00] Ian: Good to know.
[26:05] [26:30] Jai: Any constraints on the language support? We have multilingual prompts.
[26:38] Ethan: Models in our catalog support many languages; quality varies. For mission-critical multilingual flows, run targeted evals — Redwood Optimize can help recommend model variants per language.
[26:55] Lena: What's the expected time to get the POC started if we hand over traces today?
[27:02] Maya: If we get artifacts by Monday we can start benchmark scripts mid-next week and have initial results in ~7 business days.
[27:12] [27:30] Ian: A few operational questions — how does autoscaling behave with sudden spikes?
[27:40] Ethan: Dedicated autoscaling can be configured for target CPU/GPU utilization and can provision extra nodes, but there's a spin-up delay for cold GPUs — hence burst capacity is recommended. For sub-second ramps we use pre-warmed workers.
[27:55] Sara: Does Redwood provide node-level logs for forensic analysis or only aggregated logs?
[28:02] Ethan: Both. You get aggregated Console logs and can opt into node-level dumps for investigation with proper access controls.
[28:12] [28:30] Maya: We're close to time — any last-minute technical blockers?
[28:36] Ian: None that I can think of — mostly need to see perf numbers.
[28:42] Lena: And the SOC2 + retention policy.
[28:46] Sara: Yes.
[28:50] Maya: Great. We'll follow up with the POC plan and security bundle.
[28:55] [29:15] (wrap up)
[29:22] Lena: Thanks everyone — we look forward to the benchmark.
[29:26] Maya: Thanks — we'll share next steps & Doodle for POC kickoff.
[29:30] Ian: Thanks.
[29:33] (meeting ends)

Notes on transcription: occasional mis-hearings (e.g., "K-V cash" for KV cache), speaker label drift around 19:30 where an interruption was briefly attributed to "Speaker 3" in the auto transcript. title: SaturnWare Ops Activation Handshake
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-11-19
Start: 15:00 PST
Duration: ~48 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (CSM), Ethan (SE) — Maya, Tom, Lena, Omar (SaturnWare)

Summary: quick activation / ops handsh ake — customer wants to validate networking and security before pilot; discuss Dedicated vs Private, SLAs, and on ramp timeline. Agree to follow-ups: pricing, security artifacts, and a technical deep dive.

Topics: onboarding timeline, network connectivity (VPC peering vs private link), SSO/KMS, pilot sizing, data retention, runbook and rollback, monitoring + alerting hooks

--- Transcript ---
[00:00:00] Jordan: ok hey everyone, uh thanks for joining, I'm Jordan from Redwood, i'll kick us off. Today's the ops activation handshake, quick intro then we dug into networking and security.
[00:00:12] Maya: thanks Jordan, Maya here. we're, we're excited to get rolling. we've had the internal review and security flagged a few things so want to make sure we understand the private deployment option.
[00:00:20] Jordan: totally. quick round robin — priya from CS, ethan our solutions engineer. priya: hi!
[00:00:26] Priya: hi all, we'll be your CSM; i'll handle the onboarding plan and ops runbook cadence.
[00:00:33] Ethan: hey, ethan, i'll cover technical Qs and run the deep dive.
[00:00:36] Tom: tom here — lead SRE. main concerns: network egress, key mgmt, and cert rotation. also latency requirements for our customer facing agents, we need sub 120ms p50 for short prompts.
[00:00:49] Jordan: good to know. quick note: our hosted API typically hits 80-120ms p50 depending on model and length on our standard models, but Dedicated and Private gives predictable tail latencies and KV cache locality — ethan will explain.
[00:01:05] Ethan: yeah uh so Dedicated is reserved GPU pools in our cloud with predictable throughput; Private is the VPC/on-prem package where control plane is on our side but data plane sits in your VPC, or fully on-prem for airgap. for latency your best bet is Dedicated pinned to a model variant with warm prefixes and prefix caching.
[00:01:25] Lena: can you say more about KMS? we use an HSM-backed KMS for keys and we need envelope encryption for tokens at rest.
[00:01:32] Ethan: we integrate with customer KMS — AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or HSM via KMIP. envelope encryption supported. small caveat: for Private on-prem we expect customer to manage keys in their HSM and give us certs for the control plane; for Dedicated we can integrate with your KMS via a cross-account role.
[00:01:51] Maya: okay, that's helpful. 
[00:01:54] Omar: timeline question — how long to get a pilot that looks production-like? like actual traffic and autoscale simulation.
[00:02:02] Priya: typical for a pilot: 2-4 weeks if networking and auth are green, can be 6 if we need VPN certificates or air-gapped approvals. we usually do a quick 1-week sandbox to validate endpoints and then a 2-3 week pilot with load tests.
[00:02:18] Tom: and is there a staging SLA? our org requires documented RPO/RTO for production endpoints.
[00:02:24] Jordan: we have SLA tiers for Dedicated customers; for hosted pilot there isn't a formal SLA but our dashboards show token-level latencies. dedicated contracts include 99.95% uptime and latency SLO commitments written into the SLA.
[00:02:39] Maya: security review also asked about logs and auditability — can we get per-token tracing and retention controls?
[00:02:46] Ethan: yes, the Console offers request tracing, per-route logging, and you can set retention windows. for Private deployments we can keep audit logs entirely in your cloud storage. note sometimes redaction is needed — we can mask PII at the client or server side, we have pluggable redaction hooks.
[00:03:08] Lena: do you support SSO/SAML for the console and RBAC?
[00:03:12] Priya: yes SAML SSO with SCIM provisioning, role-based access control. we also support audit log streaming to Splunk/Datadog via HTTP forwarder.
[00:03:23] Tom: networking — we prefer AWS VPC peering or PrivateLink rather than opening public egress. what's the recommended pattern for private deployment?
[00:03:31] Ethan: for Private in a customer VPC we typically use PrivateLink or VPC peering depending on account topology, PrivateLink is easier for cross-account secure access. for Dedicated we can set up VPC peering and NATs for egress control.
[00:03:45] Omar: any firewall/NAT rules we should plan?
[00:03:48] Ethan: open ports are minimal, mainly mTLS endpoints on specific ports; we'll share a network checklist. also you may want to pin IPs if your firewall is strict, we can provide IP blocks.
[00:03:58] Jordan: quick pause — i want to surface cost tradeoffs: Dedicated buys predictability and lower tail latency per token but costs fixed commit; Private adds operational overhead on your side. hosted is fastest to start.
[00:04:12] Maya: our procurement wants an estimate of steady-state unit cost vs expected latency. can you provide model/quantization guidance?
[00:04:20] Ethan: yeah we can recommend quantization profiles per model family; for chat models 4-bit quantization with kernel optimizations tends to cut inference cost by 40-60% with small accuracy tradeoffs — we'll propose config.
[00:04:36] Lena: is model verification performed? we can't accept regressed outputs in prod.
[00:04:40] Priya: we run evals tied to prompt sets — Redwood Optimize provides quality monitoring and regression alerts; you can lock a model version and get canary rollouts.
[00:04:52] Tom: monitoring: we need SLO-based alerts and error budgets exposed to our SRE dashboard. can Redwood push metrics?
[00:04:59] Ethan: yes push via Prometheus remote write or Datadog; we expose per-route metrics, token counts, queue times, kernel-level counters — works for SLOs.
[00:05:12] Maya: ok sounds good. oh also, data residency — we need logs to stay in eu-west-1 for EU customers. is region pinning possible?
[00:05:21] Jordan: region pinning is supported in Dedicated and Private; hosted can be regionally restricted depending on model availability — we have EU region capacity, we'll note that for your pilot.
[00:05:31] Omar: small gotcha: our downstream agents use streaming responses, can Redwood stream via websocket or SSE?
[00:05:38] Ethan: streaming over SSE and websockets supported, also chunked HTTP for SDKs. one note — for very short tokens the overhead of a streaming connection can add latency, but generally it's fine.
[00:05:50] Tom: we're also doing a security questionnaire (vendor risk). Lena, do you need anything specific?
[00:05:55] Lena: SOC2 Type II report, encryption at rest and in transit details, pen test summary, and network architecture diagram. also we want the retention policy for raw prompts and a process for data deletion.
[00:06:09] Priya: we'll share the SOC2 and ISO artifacts, pen test summary and the retention controls — you can choose retention window per-route, and for Private we can set retention to zero and ship logs to your S3.
[00:06:20] Jordan: note on deletions we support on-demand purge requests; we record audit trails for deletion.
[00:06:26] Maya: brilliant. next, pilot sizing: we'll start with a single model family, roughly 500k tokens/day ramping to 2M tokens/day over 30 days.
[00:06:36] Ethan: that's helpful — can you share token distribution and prompt length percentiles? e.g., 90% under 200 tokens?
[00:06:43] Maya: yes, we'll upload a sample.
[00:06:45] Omar: also we use hybrid routing: low-latency small queries to a smaller model and long completions to a larger model. can Redwood's smart routing do that?
[00:06:53] Ethan: yes policy-based routing can send requests by latency or cost; you can configure thresholds and automatic fallback to cheaper variants. that's part of the route config.
[00:07:05] Tom: anything we should know about prefix caching? our agents reuse context, is there a way to reuse KV cache across requests?
[00:07:11] Ethan: yes continuous KV cache and prefix caching reduce per-token cost; you can pin prefixes and we maintain cache locality within Dedicated pools. for Private you get full control.
[00:07:23] Maya: ok great. can we do a short runbook walkthrough now? like who owns what in the first two weeks.
[00:07:29] Priya: sure — Week 0: network checklist and credentials; Week 1: sandbox validation endpoints, sample traffic; Week 2-3: pilot with load tests and monitoring; Week 4: review and go/no-go.
[00:07:41] Jordan: client onboarding doc link: https://redwood.app/onboarding/saturnware (placeholder) — you'll see role tasks.
[00:07:50] Lena: on identity: we want SCIM to provision roles automatically. do you support SAML + SCIM?
[00:07:57] Priya: yes both, and we can create custom roles.
[00:08:01] Omar: procurement asked about termination clauses and data handling after contract end.
[00:08:06] Jordan: standard contract language allows data export and deletion upon termination — we can get legal to surface the exact wording; i'll attach the clause.
[00:08:14] Maya: are there any blockers for us to sign a pilot agreement? we have an internal security gating process that takes 2-3 weeks.
[00:08:22] Priya: we usually pair our security docs with a technical deep dive — once Lena's satisfied we can move fast.
[00:08:28] Tom: can you do a run of a load test in a sandbox so we can validate autoscale?
[00:08:33] Ethan: yes we can run synthetic load and share the results; we'll need your traffic profile and auth fixture.
[00:08:40] Omar: rough dates — can we target pilot start 12/1?
[00:08:44] Jordan: target looks feasible if we get networking and docs this week.
[00:08:48] Priya: action items recap in chat and i'll follow up with the onboarding checklist.
[00:08:54] Maya: quick question about fallback strategies — if the dedicated pool is saturated what happens?
[00:08:59] Ethan: we support automated fallback to hosted compatible variants or queue/retry policies; you can set graceful degradation rules — e.g., route to smaller model or serve cached answers.
[00:09:11] Tom: are rate limits configurable per route?
[00:09:14] Ethan: yes configurable quotas, rate limits, and token budgets per route and per API key.
[00:09:20] Jordan: okay we're near the top of the hour — last quick item: will you need a compliance attestation for our SOC2?
[00:09:27] Lena: yes and pen test summary.
[00:09:30] Priya: we'll send a secure link to the docs.
[00:09:33] Jordan: excellent. final recap: Jordan -> pricing & slot examples; Priya -> security package + onboarding checklist; Ethan -> schedule deep-dive and load test; Tom/Maya -> provide token profiles and auth fixture.
[00:09:50] Maya: thanks all — this was useful.
[00:09:52] Omar: thanks, talk soon.
[00:09:55] Jordan: thanks everyone, we'll follow up in email and slack.

--- End transcript ---

Notes: transcript contains a few minor transcription errors (names and product terms may be slightly garbled). Refer to attached artifacts: pricing spreadsheet (link shared in follow-up), security package (SAML/SCIM, SOC2, pen test), and onboarding checklist.

title: Boot-up SDK: auth, env & Optimize playbook

Customer wants a short POC scoped to a 3-week pilot handling 150 reqs/s with mixed short/long prompts; security/compliance items and a latency benchmark were requested.

title: Acme Bank - Security review (KMS integration, rotation, audit evidence)
preview: Acme Bank security team reviewed Redwood Inference’s customer-managed KMS integration for Private deployments, focusing on envelope encryption, rotation workflow (including dual-decrypt window and rollback), and what audit evidence Redwood can provide. Acme emphasized quarterly rotation, segregation of duties, and clear behavior during KMS degradation/outage. Redwood committed to sharing the Private KMS operator guide, rotation procedure/evidence checklist, and a sample audit-log export schema including rotation events, plus scheduling a follow-up deep dive on failure-mode behavior and operational runbooks.

title: PennyLane Finance - Embedding + Rerank Hosting Evaluation
preview: Discovery with PennyLane Finance to understand embedding/rerank needs for a consumer fintech search and inbox assistant. Key concerns: throughput for peak trading hours, vector DB integration, hybrid search (BM25 + emb), hosting choices (hosted vs dedicated vs private/VPC), encryption/KMS, and cost tradeoffs. Agreed next steps: share security pack, run 2-week pilot with 50k vectors, provide benchmark matrix and pricing for hosted vs dedicated, schedule technical deep dive for infra team.

preview: Quarterly health check focused on production usage patterns, recent cost anomalies, reliability incidents in September, and product roadmap items touching SSO/KMS and dedicated capacity. title: Northbridge Capital Markets - Private deployment security review (model change audit + release notes)
preview: Northbridge reviewed Redwood Private change-management controls focused on: (1) audit logging for model metadata updates and version promotions, (2) retention and SIEM export options, and (3) how customer-visible release notes are generated without leaking internal details. Redwood explained the v1 model metadata schema and event-driven changelog generator, including public vs internal fields, RBAC, and idempotent eventing. Northbridge asked for: exact event fields (actor, approval, diff), retention guarantees in Private, offline/air-gapped export, and assurance that pinned versions cannot be changed silently. Next steps: Redwood to share an example audit event + sample release note artifact, confirm Private retention configuration and SIEM forwarding patterns, and provide a short write-up mapping release notes to upgrade bundles/rollback documentation.

title: Polaris Bioinformatics — payload transfer, offline license activation & runtime tuning
preview: Deep dive on air-gapped artifact transfer, offline license activation and runtime tuning for on-prem deployment. Focus areas: secure payload handoff (sideload vs sealed tape), checksum/signing and verification, NVIDIA driver bundle compatibility, KV cache warmup strategies, batching/latency tradeoffs, quantization profiles and suggested baseline for their genomics inference workloads. Agreed next steps: provide signed artifact checklist, driver bundle with SHA256, run a 48-hour warmup test on a small cluster, and schedule pilot kickoff (week of Oct 6).

title: Evacuation routing for private deployments
preview: Technical deep dive focused on multi-region routing behavior for private (VPC/on-prem) deployments. Primary goals: design an interruption-tolerant routing policy that minimizes cold-start costs, preserves KV-cache locality where possible, defines quantization fallback lanes for capacity-constrained regions, and documents an evacuation (failover) runbook for BeaconMed's private clusters. Discussed tradeoffs between latency and cost, warmup sequencing, and audit requirements for cross-region fallbacks.

title: VelvetCore Systems — Hybrid Accelerator Partner Intro
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-09-12
Start time (UTC): 16:00
Duration: 48 minutes (approx)
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE); Priya Desai (Redwood SE); Samira Khan (Redwood Solutions); Rafael Gomez (VelvetCore - Head Partnerships); Maya Iyer (VelvetCore - Infra Lead); Oliver Trent (VelvetCore - Product); Kenji Sato (VelvetCore - Lead Systems Eng)

Summary:
VelvetCore is evaluating reseller/channel partnership to surface Redwood inference runtime on their hybrid cloud appliance — primary asks are integration patterns for private deployments (VPC/on-prem appliance), performance expectations for 13B and 40B models on 8x A100 equivalents, and commercial economics for reseller margins and committed capacity. They also flagged compliance (SOC2/ISO), KMS integration, and a quick pilot (2–4 week) to validate latency for real customer workloads.

Topics covered: integration architecture, benchmark needs, security/compliance (KMS, audit logs), pilot scope and timeline, partner economics, support model and escalation paths.

Action items (captured in metadata above).

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ethan Park: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick intros — I am Ethan, AE on the Redwood partnerships team, with Priya our SE, and Samira joining on solutions. Rafael, do you want to kick off?
[00:12] Rafael Gomez: Yeah thanks, uh hi all. I run partnerships at VelvetCore, we build the hybrid appliance that sits in enterprise datacenters and we want to offer inference as a managed option through partners. Main goal of the call is to understand how Redwood would map onto our hardware and what the integration work looks like.
[00:28] Maya Iyer: Hi, Maya here — infra lead. Two quick constraints: we need KMS/HSM support for key management, and our customers sometimes require air-gapped deployments so on-prem bundle is important. Also curious about quota and billing models if we run Redwood in a reseller model.
[00:44] Priya Desai: Great — thanks. Briefly, Redwood supports three modes: hosted API, dedicated reserved capacity, and private deployments (VPC and on-prem packages). For partner/reseller integrations most of the work happens in the Private model where you run the control plane in a customer VPC or inside VelvetCore appliance — we've done similar for other SI partners.
[01:00] Kenji Sato: Sorry, when you say control plane in the appliance — is that fully offline? Like can we do an air-gap install where telemetry doesn't phone home?
[01:09] Samira Khan: Short answer: yes, we have an on-prem package that can be deployed air-gapped. There are toggles for telemetry and optional call-home endpoints. The management plane can be run in your network and logs are written to local storage or to your SIEM via forwarders.
[01:24] Oliver Trent: For models — our channel customers care about 13B and 40B LLMs right now. Can you give rough latency/throughput numbers for 8x-A100 style nodes?
[01:36] Priya Desai: Sure, rough ballpark — for a well-optimized 13B you can expect p95 inference latency around 70–120ms per 512-token request with batching trickery, for 40B the p95 is more like 250–450ms depending on quantization and KV cache warmness. Those numbers vary a lot by sequence length, kv-cache behavior, and kernel selection. We'll need to run your typical prompt shapes to be precise.
[02:02] Rafael Gomez: Right, we'll provide representative prompt set. Also are there suggestions for quantization or recommended model variants to reduce memory footprint without hurting quality?
[02:12] Priya Desai: Yes — Redwood Optimize offers model+quantization recommendations by workload. In partner pilots we typically try int8 or q4_0 for 40B variants as a starting point and baseline quality with a small eval set. If you need quality guarantees we do eval hooks so you can gate rollouts.
[02:28] Kenji Sato: Networking — what's required for VPC peering? Do we need open egress to Redwood infra?
[02:36] Priya Desai: For the private control plane route, you can peer into our control plane or run it locally. If you run the control plane locally in VelvetCore network then no outbound egress is required except for optional updates. If you use hosted control plane there are specific ports for metrics and heartbeats; we'll share a minimal egress matrix.
[02:52] Rafael Gomez: From a commercial perspective: how does reseller pricing work? Do we resell Redwood hosted and take margin, or run Redwood private on our appliance and charge a recurring fee?
[03:03] Ethan Park: We support both. Typical patterns: 1) reseller resells hosted API and we provide partner discounts; 2) reseller hosts private deployments on their hardware and we license private deployments with an enterprise fee plus per-instance charges. We'll share a partner margin template that shows both models.
[03:19] Oliver Trent: Support SLAs — is there a playbook for partner-managed vs Redwood-managed?
[03:25] Samira Khan: Yes — we have three tiers: partner-first (you handle initial triage, Redwood on-call for escalations), co-managed (shared runbooks, joint escalation), and Redwood-managed (we take primary responsibility). For pilots we usually recommend co-managed.
[03:40] Maya Iyer: Compliance — do you have SOC2 and ISO certs and can we do customer audits?
[03:47] Ethan Park: We are SOC2 Type II and maintain ISO alignment. For private deployments you can control audit logs locally; we also provide audit bundles and support for customer audits. We'll share SOC2/ISO artifacts under NDA.
[03:59] Rafael Gomez: Good. For the pilot, we want to validate end-to-end latency with our appliance in a colo using 8x-A100 class GPUs. We'd like Redwood to run the same microbenchmarks on identical hardware to correlate numbers — is that doable?
[04:12] Priya Desai: Absolutely. We'll need an inventory of your node sizes, CUDA/cuDNN/driver versions, plus a list of model checkpoints you want to test. We can spin up a bench harness and run both latency and throughput curves and share logs. For reproducibility we capture sequence distributions and batch sizes.
[04:30] Kenji Sato: Any gotchas for kernel versions? We've seen weird perf cliffs with certain driver combos and with newer attention kernels.
[04:36] Priya Desai: Yep — kernel and driver combo matters. Our runtime auto-selects kernels, but in some edge cases we recommend specific driver levels. We'll include the recommended matrix in the bench runbook.
[04:48] Rafael Gomez: Timeline — if we start next week can we do a 2–3 week pilot?
[04:53] Ethan Park: Yes, a 2–3 week pilot is realistic: week 1 run bench + infra validate, week 2 tune quantization and caching, week 3 compile results and a joint review. We'll aim to have initial numbers within 10 business days.
[05:06] Oliver Trent: Who should be looped in from Redwood for technical escalation?
[05:10] Priya Desai: Priya will lead bench, Samira for solutions and onboarding, and Ethan for commercial. We'll also include a runtime engineer if we hit kernel-level issues.
[05:19] Rafael Gomez: Great. A few clarifying bits — can Redwood support per-tenant encryption with our KMS?
[05:24] Samira Khan: Yes, we integrate with customer KMS/HSM providers. For private deployments you can supply HSM endpoints; for hosted we can use customer-managed KMS via a trust chain.
[05:34] Maya Iyer: For audit logs retention, can we set retention policies locally? Our customers have different regulatory retention windows.
[05:40] Priya Desai: Yes — the log retention is configurable in private installs and we surface it in the console.
[05:46] Ethan Park: Ok quick recap of next steps: Priya will run bench once VelvetCore shares network/driver info and a prompt set; Ethan will send partner economics and onboarding deck; Maya to provide network diagram and test creds.
[05:58] Rafael Gomez: Sounds good, we're excited — we see a clear fit if perf and margins line up.
[06:03] Ethan Park: Thanks everyone; we'll follow up with the artifacts and a proposed timeline.
[06:09] (call ends)

Notes on transcript fidelity: occasional mis-heard acronyms and product names may appear — e.g., references to KV cache noted as K-V cache, some kernel names abbreviated.
