title: Slicing the curve: INT8 vs FP8 workshop
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-08-19
Start time: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~63 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Dr. Priya Rangan (LumenHealth ML Lead), Sam Torres (Infra), Benji Ortiz (Product)
Recording id: ff-20250819-7432

Auto-generated summary (quick):
Maya/Ethan walked LumenHealth through the Optimize console qprofile workflow, compared INT8 vs FP8 on a small demo model, discussed quality vs latency tradeoffs and a proposed 2-week sandbox bakeoff. Customer concerns: latency tail, hallucination risk, and KMS/data-residency. Next steps agreed: share sample prompts, run sensitivity sweep, provide pricing for Dedicated burst.

Topics: INT8/FP8 tradeoffs, qprofile setup, fallback/canary, security requirements, sandbox plan.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya: hey everyone thanks for joining, this is Maya from Redwood, i'll kick things off. Ethan from our SE team will do the deep dive on the optimize console and the qprofiles.
[00:14] Priya: thanks Maya, hi all. we have about an hour, so would like to see the console flows and then align on a sandbox plan. latency is the big one for us.
[00:27] Maya: great, quick check — what's your latency SLO again? like p95 and p99 numbers?
[00:33] Benji: p95 under 220ms, p99 under 500ms ideally. and cost target per 1k requests is... um, around $2.5? rough.
[00:44] Sam: also we need to keep everything in eu-west, and we use a managed KMS cluster, so KMS + audit logs are mandatory.
[00:56] Ethan: noted. i'll show the qprofile presets and then we can do a small run to show int8 vs fp8 on a demo workload. fair?

[00:58] -- demo starts, screen share --

[01:20] Ethan: so in the console here, Optimize has a qprofile tab, you can pick model family, target latency bucket, and quality constraints. we ship some defaults (conservative, balanced, aggressive). these map to INT8 or FP8 kernels depending on model and hardware. the console will suggest fallback rules automatically.
[01:48] Priya: when you say fallback, is that per-route? per-tenant? we have multiple product flows.
[01:55] Ethan: yes, you can scope at route level; each route can have a chain: INT8 primary, FP8 as higher-fidelity fallback, and a hosted full-precision endpoint as last resort. that way you get cost wins without risking a full outage.
[02:12] Benji: does that affect KV cache compatibility? we rely on prefix caching for patient context.
[02:20] Ethan: good question. K-V cache layout is preserved across compatible quant variants; the runtime manages a canonical serialized KV so when you fail over to FP8 we rehydrate, but cross-architecture fallbacks (like from a 16-bit FPGA variant) can force a cold rebuild. small glitch there, but for int8/fp8 variants it's smooth.

[02:45] [03:00] Ethan: pulling up the quick benchmark we ran earlier today — note this is a demo smaller model with 512 tokens, synthetic prompts. INT8 average latency 120ms, p95 220ms, p99 360ms. FP8 average 160ms, p95 310ms, p99 520ms. Quality delta measured by BLEU-style surrogate: INT8 dropped 3.2% vs FP32, FP8 dropped 1.0% — on this dataset.
[03:34] Priya: ok those numbers are helpful, p99 for FP8 exceeds our p99 goal at 520ms. but the quality hit on INT8 seems non-trivial for our clinical summarization use case. what do you recommend?
[03:48] Ethan: common pattern: use conservative qprofile in production for high-risk routes, balanced for low-risk, then use canary rollouts to validate. for summarization i would start FP8 with a progressive canary to see quality regen. maybe try INT8 on less sensitive flows.
[04:06] Maya: also worth noting the Optimize console can auto-run evals on a golden set. that helps detect regressions before rollout.

[04:20] [05:05] Sam: about data residency: can the sandbox run inside our VPC, or do we need to open ports? we prefer private deployment.
[05:14] Ethan: sandbox can be deployed in a customer VPC with private peering and temporary IAM role for the test. we support customer-managed KMS; logs are exported to your Cloud Audit Logs. small caveat: for dedicated on-prem there's a bit more ops to set up but we can assist.

[05:40] Benji: pricing: how do you price a 1-week burst of Dedicated for the bakeoff?
[05:45] Maya: we'll put together an estimate. usually it's a short reserved pool or a burst booking, we'll give you a pro forma. i'll include that in follow up.

[06:05] [06:30] Ethan: now i'll switch to a live qprofile creation — naming the profile, picking 'balanced' preset, uploading a small golden set of 50 prompts. console asks for target latency and maximum acceptable quality delta (we set delta <= 2%). choose hardware (Ampere A100) and run.
[06:58] Priya: is the hardware selection binding? meaning if i pick Ampere in console but our dedicated uses different cards, how's portability?
[07:08] Ethan: the qprofile is tied to model/hardware pair. console will recommend compatible variants for other hardware; some kernels are unavailable on older gen, in which case you'd see a compatibility warning.

[07:30] [08:00] Ethan: starting the job — this is a short sensitivity sweep. results will show latency distribution and per-prompt drift. watch the per-prompt failures column, that usually flags hallucinations or tokenization mismatches.
[08:22] [09:10] Maya: note the transcription on the demo might show 'opt e mize' sometimes in captions — ignore that — it's just auto-caption noise.

[09:30] [10:00] Priya: on quality metrics, BLEU is ok but our team uses ROUGE & a custom clinical-exactness metric. can the console ingest custom metrics?
[10:06] Ethan: yes you can plug in an eval hook or upload a CSV of golden references and a scorer. we currently support ROUGE, BERTScore, and custom callback via a lambda. there's a minor naming bug in the UI where 'BERTScore' was spelled 'BurtScore' in one build — that may show in captions — but backend is fine.

[10:45] [12:00] Sam: for the audit trail, will we see per-request token usage and who triggered model changes?
[12:10] Ethan: yes, console logs show token-level cost, model version, the qprofile used and actor (SSO user). plus you get a diff view for qprofile changes to review what changed during a rollout.

[12:40] [14:00] Ethan: now results are landing — quick look: the per-prompt drift heatmap shows 27 prompts with >2% quality delta on INT8, 6 prompts on FP8. lowest hanging mitigation is to mark those prompts as 'sensitive' and route them to FP8 via route-level override.
[14:32] Priya: that's useful. what's the friction to maintain two variants in production? do we double storage/cost?
[14:41] Ethan: not double storage for weights typically (we share artifacts), but you pay for instance time for each running variant if both are active. our cost estimator in the console simulates expected spend given traffic split.

[15:00] [16:00] Benji: are there automated rollback triggers? ex: if hallucination rate exceeds x% then switch back to FP32?
[16:12] Ethan: yes, you can set automated policies: e.g., if golden-set ROUGE drops >3% or user-reported errors spike, we can automatically rollback or shift traffic to a higher-fidelity variant. those policies are pluggable.

[16:40] [18:00] Priya: show us the canary rollout UI — we want to do progressive ramp: 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% over 3 days with automated checks.
[18:10] Ethan: clicking rollout, create canary, specify gates: success if p95 under target and custom metric within delta, duration per stage, and manual approval options. i'll create a template canary now and save it as 'lumen-summarize-canary'.

[18:48] Maya: quick question: how do you handle tokenization differences for clinical abbreviations? we've seen token splits change semantics.
[18:58] Ethan: tokenizer sensitivity is real. our eval pipeline flags tokenization mismatches and we recommend normalizing inputs (preprocessing step) or rerun a quick tokenization test as part of the qprofile sweep.

[19:20] [20:00] -- brief pause while console loads more details --

[20:30] Priya: ok on the metrics — what about hallucination signals beyond automated evals? do you have user complaint ingestion?
[20:42] Ethan: yes we ingest user feedback through an API endpoint. you can wire in your own production telemetry (e.g., false-positive flags) and create alert-based gates. the console's 'quality monitor' will surface drift and run periodic regression checks.

[21:10] Benji: is there an integration for our incidenting tool? we use PagerFlow (not PagerDuty) — any webhooks?
[21:18] Ethan: yes webhook endpoints are configurable; we've seen a few customers plug into nonstandard tools. just provide the endpoint and auth — minor mapping work.

[21:40] [23:00] Sam: from infra side, what's the network egress profile during a sand-box week? we need to open a minimal set of endpoints.
[23:08] Ethan: the sandbox nodes only require outbound to Redwood control plane for metrics. if in VPC peering mode it's all internal; we give you a list of CIDRs and ports. i'll include that in the follow up.

[23:40] [25:00] Priya: let's talk about model choice — we've been using an open LLM tuned for clinical summarization. would Redwood onboard that model in the console?
[25:10] Ethan: yes, we can onboard custom weights. we do a quick compatibility check and then produce quantized variants. note: some model architectures quantize more gracefully; smaller vocab or heavy layernorm patterns can change sensitivity. we document per-model recommended qprofiles.

[26:00] [28:00] Maya: quick aside — competitor question: if we wanted to run entirely on our infra, how does Redwood compare to the internal effort?
[28:12] Priya: yeah we have an internal team looking at building a similar runtime.
[28:20] Maya: common tradeoff — internal builds can be cheaper long term but take months to mature for production-grade routing, fallback, and observability. Redwood's value prop is the integrated rollouts, auto-evals and rocket-to-prod tooling.

[28:50] [30:00] Ethan: ok, i'm going to show a per-prompt example. prompt id 423: INT8 produced a truncated clinical term twice; FP8 returned the full phrase. console tags it as 'term-truncation' and suggests remap or route to FP8.
[30:25] Priya: can we tag prompts and keep a suppress list? some prompts are noisy.
[30:32] Ethan: yes tagging and suppression lists are supported. you can also setup a feedback loop to retrain or finetune later; the console will export problematic prompts for human review.

[31:00] [32:00] Benji: show cost sim for mixed rollout — 10% FP8 90% INT8.
[32:08] Ethan: here — cost delta ~ +18% vs pure INT8, but quality regressions drop from 27 prompts to 9. there's a projected monthly savings estimate if you go hybrid.

[32:40] [34:30] Priya: on the projected numbers, do they include tokenization overhead and kv-cache hit ratio assumptions?
[34:40] Ethan: yes we let you input expected avg sequence length and KV cache hit %, those drive effective per-request cost. default assumption was 40% cache hit in demo. change that and cost delta shifts.

[35:10] [36:00] Sam: we also have strict SOC2 + ISO expectations. do you have SOC 2 attestation and intel on audit retention windows?
[36:15] Maya: Redwood is SOC 2 Type II and we have ISO readiness docs. retention windows for logs are configurable; we can export to your log retention buckets. i'll attach the security packet in the follow up.

[36:50] [38:00] Priya: potential blocker: if FP8 p99 still exceeds our SLO, is there a way to optimize kernels further? maybe sequence-slicing or continuous batching?
[38:12] Ethan: yes, runtime levers: dynamic batching thresholds, prefix KV reuse, and sequence-slicing for very long contexts. some of those are available in managed mode and some require custom kernel tuning on Dedicated. we can try sequencing heuristics in the sandbox.

[39:00] [41:00] Ethan: running a deeper sensitivity run now — giving you a per-category breakdown: hallucination-prone prompts, long-context prompts, and short Q/A. INT8 handles short Q/A well, struggles on long-context summarization. FP8 handles long context better.

[41:30] Priya: ok that matches our intuition. if we need to choose, our first priority is correctness for discharge summaries, second is latency for triage flows.

[42:00] Maya: recommended approach: route discharge-summaries to FP8 with a conservative qprofile, use INT8 for triage after canary. deploy a canary template and run the 1-week sandbox to collect real traffic metrics.

[42:30] [44:00] Benji: what does the sandbox data collection look like? do you store the raw prompts? we need PHI handling policies.
[44:10] Ethan: we recommend pseudonymization. for PHI you can either run the sandbox on your VPC+private deployment (we do not see raw prompts), or mask on ingestion. audit logs still show token counts and route ids but not PII if masked.

[45:00] [46:30] Priya: ok we'll aim to run in our VPC. Sam will validate.

[46:50] Sam: quick question on KMS rotation — if we rotate customer KMS during the test, will that cause model artifact inaccessibility?
[46:58] Ethan: you must coordinate a maintenance window; the runtime caches keys for a short TTL but rotation without a handshake can cause auth failures. we support key-rotation workflows.

[47:30] [48:00] Maya: we've talked price and security, any other blockers?
[48:06] Priya: only internal procurement timelines, and we need the security packet.

[48:30] [50:00] Ethan: running the final report in the console — here's the heatmap, here are the flagged prompts, here's the canary template saved. i'll export this as a zip with logs and the qprofile json. note: the qprofile json uses shorthand names we sometimes mis-spell in captions (e.g., 'qprofie') but file is correct.

[50:40] Benji: please include the cost estimate with p95/p99 breakdown.
[50:45] Maya: will do.

[51:10] [52:00] Priya: one more question: for model drift detection, is there a continuous monitor we can run?
[52:06] Ethan: yes, quality monitor runs nightly and can trigger alerts if drift or golden-set regression is detected. you can also schedule heavier regression sweeps weekly.

[52:40] [55:00] -- end of demo, Q&A --

[55:30] Priya: thanks, that was helpful. next steps: we'll upload prompts, Sam to confirm VPC peering window, and Ethan to run full 1k sweep and share results.
[55:45] Maya: i'll follow up with pricing and the security packet.

[56:10] Benji: who should we reach out to if we hit a blocker during the sandbox?
[56:18] Maya: direct to me and Ethan, and cc the solutions engineering alias; we'll set up daily stand for first three days if you want.

[56:40] [58:00] Ethan: final note on fallback rules — we recommend a simplicity-first policy initially: FP8 fallback for flagged prompts, then full-precision as last resort. keep automated rollback thresholds tight for high-risk flows.

[58:30] Priya: agreed. thanks everyone.
[58:40] Maya: thanks all, i'll capture notes and action items. expect the follow up with recordings and docs in 24 hours.

[59:00] -- meeting wrap up and small crosstalk --

[60:20] End of recording.

References/Artifacts mentioned:
- shared sandbox folder: https://redwood.test/sandbox/lumen-2025-08 (placeholder link)
- demo qprofile export (attached in follow-up)
- perf + cost spreadsheet (will be shared)
- security packet (SOC2/ISO docs)

Meeting recap (auto):
- Agreed on a 2-week sandbox bakeoff focusing on INT8 vs FP8 with a 1k prompt sensitivity sweep.
- Customer to provide canonical prompts and SLOs; Redwood to run sweep and share detailed heatmaps and cost sims.
- Deliverables: qprofile export, canary template, audit/security packet, Dedicated burst pricing.
text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-08-19
Start time: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~63 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Dr. Priya Rangan (LumenHealth ML Lead), Sam Torres (Infra), Benji Ortiz (Product)
Recording id: ff-20250819-7432

Auto-generated summary (quick):
Maya/Ethan walked LumenHealth through the Optimize console qprofile workflow, compared INT8 vs FP8 on a small demo model, discussed quality vs latency tradeoffs and a proposed 2-week sandbox bakeoff. Customer concerns: latency tail, hallucination risk, and KMS/data-residency. Next steps agreed: share sample prompts, run sensitivity sweep, provide pricing for Dedicated burst.

Topics: INT8/FP8 tradeoffs, qprofile setup, fallback/canary, security requirements, sandbox plan.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya: hey everyone thanks for joining, this is Maya from Redwood, i'll kick things off. Ethan from our SE team will do the deep dive on the optimize console and the qprofiles.
[00:14] Priya: thanks Maya, hi all. we have about an hour, so would like to see the console flows and then align on a sandbox plan. latency is the big one for us.
[00:27] Maya: great, quick check — what's your latency SLO again? like p95 and p99 numbers?
[00:33] Benji: p95 under 220ms, p99 under 500ms ideally. and cost target per 1k requests is... um, around $2.5? rough.
[00:44] Sam: also we need to keep everything in eu-west, and we use a managed KMS cluster, so KMS + audit logs are mandatory.
[00:56] Ethan: noted. i'll show the qprofile presets and then we can do a small run to show int8 vs fp8 on a demo workload. fair?

[00:58] -- demo starts, screen share --

[01:20] Ethan: so in the console here, Optimize has a qprofile tab, you can pick model family, target latency bucket, and quality constraints. we ship some defaults (conservative, balanced, aggressive). these map to INT8 or FP8 kernels depending on model and hardware. the console will suggest fallback rules automatically.
[01:48] Priya: when you say fallback, is that per-route? per-tenant? we have multiple product flows.
[01:55] Ethan: yes, you can scope at route level; each route can have a chain: INT8 primary, FP8 as higher-fidelity fallback, and a hosted full-precision endpoint as last resort. that way you get cost wins without risking a full outage.
[02:12] Benji: does that affect KV cache compatibility? we rely on prefix caching for patient context.
[02:20] Ethan: good question. K-V cache layout is preserved across compatible quant variants; the runtime manages a canonical serialized KV so when you fail over to FP8 we rehydrate, but cross-architecture fallbacks (like from a 16-bit FPGA variant) can force a cold rebuild. small glitch there, but for int8/fp8 variants it's smooth.

[02:45] [03:00] Ethan: pulling up the quick benchmark we ran earlier today — note this is a demo smaller model with 512 tokens, synthetic prompts. INT8 average latency 120ms, p95 220ms, p99 360ms. FP8 average 160ms, p95 310ms, p99 520ms. Quality delta measured by BLEU-style surrogate: INT8 dropped 3.2% vs FP32, FP8 dropped 1.0% — on this dataset.
[03:34] Priya: ok those numbers are helpful, p99 for FP8 exceeds our p99 goal at 520ms. but the quality hit on INT8 seems non-trivial for our clinical summarization use case. what do you recommend?
[03:48] Ethan: common pattern: use conservative qprofile in production for high-risk routes, balanced for low-risk, then use canary rollouts to validate. for summarization i would start FP8 with a progressive canary to see quality regen. maybe try INT8 on less sensitive flows.
[04:06] Maya: also worth noting the Optimize console can auto-run evals on a golden set. that helps detect regressions before rollout.

[04:20] [05:05] Sam: about data residency: can the sandbox run inside our VPC, or do we need to open ports? we prefer private deployment.
[05:14] Ethan: sandbox can be deployed in a customer VPC with private peering and temporary IAM role for the test. we support customer-managed KMS; logs are exported to your Cloud Audit Logs. small caveat: for dedicated on-prem there's a bit more ops to set up but we can assist.

[05:40] Benji: pricing: how do you price a 1-week burst of Dedicated for the bakeoff?
[05:45] Maya: we'll put together an estimate. usually it's a short reserved pool or a burst booking, we'll give you a pro forma. i'll include that in follow up.

[06:05] [06:30] Ethan: now i'll switch to a live qprofile creation — naming the profile, picking 'balanced' preset, uploading a small golden set of 50 prompts. console asks for target latency and maximum acceptable quality delta (we set delta <= 2%). choose hardware (Ampere A100) and run.
[06:58] Priya: is the hardware selection binding? meaning if i pick Ampere in console but our dedicated uses different cards, how's portability?
[07:08] Ethan: the qprofile is tied to model/hardware pair. console will recommend compatible variants for other hardware; some kernels are unavailable on older gen, in which case you'd see a compatibility warning.

[07:30] [08:00] Ethan: starting the job — this is a short sensitivity sweep. results will show latency distribution and per-prompt drift. watch the per-prompt failures column, that usually flags hallucinations or tokenization mismatches.
[08:22] [09:10] Maya: note the transcription on the demo might show 'opt e mize' sometimes in captions — ignore that — it's just auto-caption noise.

[09:30] [10:00] Priya: on quality metrics, BLEU is ok but our team uses ROUGE & a custom clinical-exactness metric. can the console ingest custom metrics?
[10:06] Ethan: yes you can plug in an eval hook or upload a CSV of golden references and a scorer. we currently support ROUGE, BERTScore, and custom callback via a lambda. there's a minor naming bug in the UI where 'BERTScore' was spelled 'BurtScore' in one build — that may show in captions — but backend is fine.

[10:45] [12:00] Sam: for the audit trail, will we see per-request token usage and who triggered model changes?
[12:10] Ethan: yes, console logs show token-level cost, model version, the qprofile used and actor (SSO user). plus you get a diff view for qprofile changes to review what changed during a rollout.

[12:40] [14:00] Ethan: now results are landing — quick look: the per-prompt drift heatmap shows 27 prompts with >2% quality delta on INT8, 6 prompts on FP8. lowest hanging mitigation is to mark those prompts as 'sensitive' and route them to FP8 via route-level override.
[14:32] Priya: that's useful. what's the friction to maintain two variants in production? do we double storage/cost?
[14:41] Ethan: not double storage for weights typically (we share artifacts), but you pay for instance time for each running variant if both are active. our cost estimator in the console simulates expected spend given traffic split.

[15:00] [16:00] Benji: are there automated rollback triggers? ex: if hallucination rate exceeds x% then switch back to FP32?
[16:12] Ethan: yes, you can set automated policies: e.g., if golden-set ROUGE drops >3% or user-reported errors spike, we can automatically rollback or shift traffic to a higher-fidelity variant. those policies are pluggable.

[16:40] [18:00] Priya: show us the canary rollout UI — we want to do progressive ramp: 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% over 3 days with automated checks.
[18:10] Ethan: clicking rollout, create canary, specify gates: success if p95 under target and custom metric within delta, duration per stage, and manual approval options. i'll create a template canary now and save it as 'lumen-summarize-canary'.

[18:48] Maya: quick question: how do you handle tokenization differences for clinical abbreviations? we've seen token splits change semantics.
[18:58] Ethan: tokenizer sensitivity is real. our eval pipeline flags tokenization mismatches and we recommend normalizing inputs (preprocessing step) or rerun a quick tokenization test as part of the qprofile sweep.

[19:20] [20:00] -- brief pause while console loads more details --

[20:30] Priya: ok on the metrics — what about hallucination signals beyond automated evals? do you have user complaint ingestion?
[20:42] Ethan: yes we ingest user feedback through an API endpoint. you can wire in your own production telemetry (e.g., false-positive flags) and create alert-based gates. the console's 'quality monitor' will surface drift and run periodic regression checks.

[21:10] Benji: is there an integration for our incidenting tool? we use PagerFlow (not PagerDuty) — any webhooks?
[21:18] Ethan: yes webhook endpoints are configurable; we've seen a few customers plug into nonstandard tools. just provide the endpoint and auth — minor mapping work.

[21:40] [23:00] Sam: from infra side, what's the network egress profile during a sand-box week? we need to open a minimal set of endpoints.
[23:08] Ethan: the sandbox nodes only require outbound to Redwood control plane for metrics. if in VPC peering mode it's all internal; we give you a list of CIDRs and ports. i'll include that in the follow up.

[23:40] [25:00] Priya: let's talk about model choice — we've been using an open LLM tuned for clinical summarization. would Redwood onboard that model in the console?
[25:10] Ethan: yes, we can onboard custom weights. we do a quick compatibility check and then produce quantized variants. note: some model architectures quantize more gracefully; smaller vocab or heavy layernorm patterns can change sensitivity. we document per-model recommended qprofiles.

[26:00] [28:00] Maya: quick aside — competitor question: if we wanted to run entirely on our infra, how does Redwood compare to the internal effort?
[28:12] Priya: yeah we have an internal team looking at building a similar runtime.
[28:20] Maya: common tradeoff — internal builds can be cheaper long term but take months to mature for production-grade routing, fallback, and observability. Redwood's value prop is the integrated rollouts, auto-evals and rocket-to-prod tooling.

[28:50] [30:00] Ethan: ok, i'm going to show a per-prompt example. prompt id 423: INT8 produced a truncated clinical term twice; FP8 returned the full phrase. console tags it as 'term-truncation' and suggests remap or route to FP8.
[30:25] Priya: can we tag prompts and keep a suppress list? some prompts are noisy.
[30:32] Ethan: yes tagging and suppression lists are supported. you can also setup a feedback loop to retrain or finetune later; the console will export problematic prompts for human review.

[31:00] [32:00] Benji: show cost sim for mixed rollout — 10% FP8 90% INT8.
[32:08] Ethan: here — cost delta ~ +18% vs pure INT8, but quality regressions drop from 27 prompts to 9. there's a projected monthly savings estimate if you go hybrid.

[32:40] [34:30] Priya: on the projected numbers, do they include tokenization overhead and kv-cache hit ratio assumptions?
[34:40] Ethan: yes we let you input expected avg sequence length and KV cache hit %, those drive effective per-request cost. default assumption was 40% cache hit in demo. change that and cost delta shifts.

[35:10] [36:00] Sam: we also have strict SOC2 + ISO expectations. do you have SOC 2 attestation and intel on audit retention windows?
[36:15] Maya: Redwood is SOC 2 Type II and we have ISO readiness docs. retention windows for logs are configurable; we can export to your log retention buckets. i'll attach the security packet in the follow up.

[36:50] [38:00] Priya: potential blocker: if FP8 p99 still exceeds our SLO, is there a way to optimize kernels further? maybe sequence-slicing or continuous batching?
[38:12] Ethan: yes, runtime levers: dynamic batching thresholds, prefix KV reuse, and sequence-slicing for very long contexts. some of those are available in managed mode and some require custom kernel tuning on Dedicated. we can try sequencing heuristics in the sandbox.

[39:00] [41:00] Ethan: running a deeper sensitivity run now — giving you a per-category breakdown: hallucination-prone prompts, long-context prompts, and short Q/A. INT8 handles short Q/A well, struggles on long-context summarization. FP8 handles long context better.

[41:30] Priya: ok that matches our intuition. if we need to choose, our first priority is correctness for discharge summaries, second is latency for triage flows.

[42:00] Maya: recommended approach: route discharge-summaries to FP8 with a conservative qprofile, use INT8 for triage after canary. deploy a canary template and run the 1-week sandbox to collect real traffic metrics.

[42:30] [44:00] Benji: what does the sandbox data collection look like? do you store the raw prompts? we need PHI handling policies.
[44:10] Ethan: we recommend pseudonymization. for PHI you can either run the sandbox on your VPC+private deployment (we do not see raw prompts), or mask on ingestion. audit logs still show token counts and route ids but not PII if masked.

[45:00] [46:30] Priya: ok we'll aim to run in our VPC. Sam will validate.

[46:50] Sam: quick question on KMS rotation — if we rotate customer KMS during the test, will that cause model artifact inaccessibility?
[46:58] Ethan: you must coordinate a maintenance window; the runtime caches keys for a short TTL but rotation without a handshake can cause auth failures. we support key-rotation workflows.

[47:30] [48:00] Maya: we've talked price and security, any other blockers?
[48:06] Priya: only internal procurement timelines, and we need the security packet.

[48:30] [50:00] Ethan: running the final report in the console — here's the heatmap, here are the flagged prompts, here's the canary template saved. i'll export this as a zip with logs and the qprofile json. note: the qprofile json uses shorthand names we sometimes mis-spell in captions (e.g., 'qprofie') but file is correct.

[50:40] Benji: please include the cost estimate with p95/p99 breakdown.
[50:45] Maya: will do.

[51:10] [52:00] Priya: one more question: for model drift detection, is there a continuous monitor we can run?
[52:06] Ethan: yes, quality monitor runs nightly and can trigger alerts if drift or golden-set regression is detected. you can also schedule heavier regression sweeps weekly.

[52:40] [55:00] -- end of demo, Q&A --

[55:30] Priya: thanks, that was helpful. next steps: we'll upload prompts, Sam to confirm VPC peering window, and Ethan to run full 1k sweep and share results.
[55:45] Maya: i'll follow up with pricing and the security packet.

[56:10] Benji: who should we reach out to if we hit a blocker during the sandbox?
[56:18] Maya: direct to me and Ethan, and cc the solutions engineering alias; we'll set up daily stand for first three days if you want.

[56:40] [58:00] Ethan: final note on fallback rules — we recommend a simplicity-first policy initially: FP8 fallback for flagged prompts, then full-precision as last resort. keep automated rollback thresholds tight for high-risk flows.

[58:30] Priya: agreed. thanks everyone.
[58:40] Maya: thanks all, i'll capture notes and action items. expect the follow up with recordings and docs in 24 hours.

[59:00] -- meeting wrap up and small crosstalk --

[60:20] End of recording.

References/Artifacts mentioned:
- shared sandbox folder: https://redwood.test/sandbox/lumen-2025-08 (placeholder link)
- demo qprofile export (attached in follow-up)
- perf + cost spreadsheet (will be shared)
- security packet (SOC2/ISO docs)

Meeting recap (auto):
- Agreed on a 2-week sandbox bakeoff focusing on INT8 vs FP8 with a 1k prompt sensitivity sweep.
- Customer to provide canonical prompts and SLOs; Redwood to run sweep and share detailed heatmaps and cost sims.
- Deliverables: qprofile export, canary template, audit/security packet, Dedicated burst pricing.

title: Developer FastStart: SDK & Auth Walkthrough (Py/TS)
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-07-22
Start: 14:00 PDT
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Lena Ortiz (VantaWave), Samir Patel (VantaWave), Ruth Kim (VantaWave Sec), Diego (VantaWave intern)

Auto-summary: Quick hands-on walkthrough of Redwood SDKs (Python + TypeScript), auth patterns (API keys vs service accounts vs SSO/SAML), environment setup for sandbox and CI, and a short demo of the Console and Optimize recommendations. Agreed next steps: Redwood to send sandbox access + examples; customer to run quickstart and share numbers; security deep dive to be scheduled.

Topics: SDK quickstart, auth patterns, env vars and CI, sandbox provisioning, Console demo, Optimize tips (batching, quantization guidance), security questions (KMS, audit logs, data retention).

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya: hey everyone, thanks for joining, i'm maya from redwood inference — super excited to run through a quick dev faststart for the SDKs. ethan is gonna jump into the console and the optimize tips.
[00:12] Lena: thanks, maya. we're a bit crunched so if we can get the auth/ci parts clear that'll be great. we've got a small POC team — me for backend, samir for infra, ruth for security.
[00:20] Samir: yep, timeline's two weeks for an internal spike, then we'll propose a pilot.
[00:25] Maya: perfect, we'll aim for pragmatic steps. quick intro: redwood offers hosted API, dedicated capacity, and private deployments. for this call we'll focus on hosted sandbox + SDK integration.
[00:36] Ethan: cool — i'll demo python then typescript snippets, show how to wire API keys and a service-account style flow for CI. i also have a short console tour to show optimize suggestions.
[00:45] Ruth: security first — we need to know retention, where keys live, KMS integration, and SOC2 posture.
[00:52] Maya: noted, we'll cover at a high level and follow up with security docs.

[02:10] Ethan: ok starting with python quickstart: pip install redwood-sdk (note: pip package name in preview is redwood-inference but the docs link has latest). example: 
- set REDWOOD_API_KEY in your env 
- from redwood import Client 
- client = Client(api_key=os.getenv('REDWOOD_API_KEY')) 
- resp = client.text.generate(model='rw-13b-qual', input='summarize: ...')
(there may be slight token/field name changes depending on sdk version)

[04:05] Diego: little question — we plan to run inside k8s, we store secrets in vault, is that supported?
[04:10] Ethan: yes, treat the API key as any secret; we recommend service account tokens for CI where possible. vault -> k8s secret -> mount or env. for dedicated/private we also support KMS/HSM integrations for at-rest keys.

[06:20] Lena: what's the recommended pattern for CI (github actions) so we don't leak keys in logs?
[06:27] Maya: use repo secrets or org secrets, avoid printing the key, use a short-lived service account token for actions. we'll send a sample GH Actions workflow. ethan, do you have a sample?
[06:35] Ethan: yep, i'll add one to the repo we share. gist: use actions/checkout, set REDWOOD_API_KEY as a secret, call a script that runs smoke tests. also don't log verbose request payloads.

[08:15] Samir: on latency/cost: curious what we should expect for a 4k token context with rw-13b quantized on dedicated vs hosted.
[08:24] Ethan: quick ballpark — hosted quantized variants will be ~2-3x cheaper per token than full float; latency depends on concurrency and batch window. for predictable latency at scale dedicated pools with fixed GPU config are recommended. i can share a benchmark sheet.

[10:05] Maya: linking the docs here: https://docs.redwood.ai/quickstart and the sandbox invite will use service account creds for the POC.

[12:00] Ethan: now switching to TypeScript: npm i @redwood/inference (package alias might change); usage: 
- import { Redwood } from '@redwood/inference' 
- const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY }) 
- await client.text.generate({ model: 'rw-instruct-7b', input: '...' })
small differences: promises/streams vs python generator style. streaming is supported in both.

[14:30] Ruth: about streaming — is partial transcript stored? are there audit logs per token?
[14:38] Maya: audit logs capture request metadata and response hashes. streaming chunks aren't separately retained unless you enable verbose debugging. full details in security pack.

[16:50] Ethan: quick demo of the Console:
- I sign in to consola (note: transcript spelled console sometimes), go to Projects -> Sandbox-PoC -> Routes. you can see per-route token cost and latency heatmap. there is an Optimize tab that suggests batching and cache profiles.

[18:10] Ethan: here's an example: Optimize flagged that your typical short-chat workload benefits from prefix caching and a small batching window (30ms) — this reduced cost by ~18% in a recent customer trace. the console links to suggested model quantization profiles.

[19:45] Lena: can we pin a model version for the pilot? we need stable behavior.
[19:50] Ethan: yes, pinning is supported at route level, you can pin exact model build and rollout percent if you want gradual deploys.

[22:05] Samir: networking: if we use sandbox hosted, can we restrict egress or lock to our VPC?
[22:13] Maya: hosted sandbox doesn't natively join your VPC; for production we offer VPC peering and private deploy options. for the POC we can set IP allowlists and static egress ranges.

[24:30] Ruth: about compliance — are you SOC2/ISO? how do we do key rotation?
[24:37] Maya: redwood is SOC2 Type II (contact docket for latest cert). key rotation: you can rotate API keys in Console and issue short-lived service account tokens. for private deployments you can integrate your KMS. we'll attach the security bundle in follow-up.

[26:55] Ethan: some gotchas we've seen: 
- CI sometimes accidentally commits .env with keys — add .env to .gitignore and use secrets manager. 
- watch for tokenization mismatch: model families tokenize differently, so perf numbers can vary. 
- if you use continuous batching, set max latency SLO to avoid spikes.

[29:40] Diego: the team uses Terraform + Helm for infra. any infra-as-code templates?
[29:46] Maya: yes, we'll include a terraform snippet for route creation and the console has an api for programmatic route config. ethan will attach examples.

[32:10] Ethan: quick live run — i'm going to run a local python demo against the sandbox key (obfuscated in demo). watch the streaming: you'll see chunked responses and client-side assembly. please excuse audio lag while i paste.
[33:05] [Demo noises / short pause as screen share switched] 
[33:30] Ethan: result came back ~450ms for a 200 token response in this noisy env. again, sandbox isn't a hard SLA environment.

[35:10] Lena: good to see. can you share the code snippet for streaming in TS?
[35:18] Ethan: yes - basically create a stream handler, on('data') append to buffer, on('end') flush. i'll paste the example in the shared notes.

[37:40] Ruth: curious about data retention and customer content — are inputs used for training?
[37:46] Maya: by default redwood does not use customer data for model training for hosted enterprise customers; there are explicit flags and agreements for training data usage. audit logs and retention policies are configurable. we'll include the DPA in the follow-up.

[40:05] Samir: is there an ability to run inference on-prem later if pilot succeeds?
[40:12] Maya: yes, we offer private deployments and an on-prem package if you need air-gapped environment. the APIs are consistent across modes which makes migration easier.

[42:00] Ethan: Optimize tip: for retrieval-augmented workloads, enable prefix KV caching — store persistent KV across similar sessions. it dramatically cuts repeated attention compute. some transcription: KV cache sometimes came out as K V cash in older transcriptions.

[43:20] Lena: any recommended quantized model family for our use-case? we do multi-turn summarization, medium length.
[43:28] Ethan: try rw-instruct-7b-q8 for cost balance; if you need better long-context handling bump to 13b quant variants. we'll add model recommendation matrix to the doc.

[45:50] Diego: ok can we get a skeleton of the dev repo for the POC? folder layout, sample tests?
[45:57] Maya: yes — we'll share a starter repo with python and ts examples, dockerfile, and GH Actions workflow. it will include smoke tests and a sample metrics emitter for latency.

[47:10] Ruth: last security nit: do you support SAML SSO for Console access?
[47:17] Maya: yes, SAML and OIDC both supported for orgs, RBAC available. we can map roles to route-level permissions.

[49:00] Ethan: wrapping up — recap of action items: maya will send sandbox invite and quickstart doc; ethan will push github actions/terraform snippets; lena will run quickstart and share perf by next wednesday. also schedule security deep dive.
[49:30] Maya: perfect. any final questions?
[49:35] Samir: small one — do you have client libraries for internal telemetry/metrics?
[49:40] Ethan: we expose hooks and recommend open-telemetry integration; sample instrumentation included in the starter repo.
[50:10] Lena: thanks all, that was helpful.
[50:16] Maya: thanks everyone, i'll follow up with the bundle.

End of transcript.

Meeting recap (auto):
- Objective: on-board devs to Redwood SDKs and verify auth/CI patterns for POC.
- Outcome: agreement to provision sandbox and share starter repo + security docs.
- Risks: need to validate latency/cost in customer env; security deep dive required for SOC2/KMS specifics.

References & links shared in call:
- Quickstart: https://docs.redwood.ai/quickstart
- Starter repo (to be shared): https://github.com/redwood-inference/pocexamples (placeholder)
- Security bundle: https://docs.redwood.ai/security-bundle (will be attached)

Notes on transcript quality: medium — some proper nouns and acronyms may be mis-transcribed, occasional speaker labels inferred.

Explicit next steps (restated):
1) Redwood (Maya/Ethan) to send sandbox access, starter repo, GH Actions workflow, terraform snippet, and security bundle by 2025-07-24.
2) VantaWave (Lena) to run quickstart and post perf numbers to shared spreadsheet by 2025-07-30.
3) Schedule security deep dive (Ruth + Redwood security) for week of 2025-07-28.

Keywords: SDK, Python, TypeScript, API key, service account, CI, GitHub Actions, sandbox, console, optimize, batching, KV cache, quantization, SOC2, KMS, VPC, private deploy

(there may be slight token/field name changes depending on sdk version)

[04:05] Diego: little question — we plan to run inside k8s, we store secrets in vault, is that supported?
[04:10] Ethan: yes, treat the API key as any secret; we recommend service account tokens for CI where possible. vault -> k8s secret -> mount or env. for dedicated/private we also support KMS/HSM integrations for at-rest keys.

[06:20] Lena: what's the recommended pattern for CI (github actions) so we don't leak keys in logs?
[06:27] Maya: use repo secrets or org secrets, avoid printing the key, use a short-lived service account token for actions. we'll send a sample GH Actions workflow. ethan, do you have a sample?
[06:35] Ethan: yep, i'll add one to the repo we share. gist: use actions/checkout, set REDWOOD_API_KEY as a secret, call a script that runs smoke tests. also don't log verbose request payloads.

[08:15] Samir: on latency/cost: curious what we should expect for a 4k token context with rw-13b quantized on dedicated vs hosted.
[08:24] Ethan: quick ballpark — hosted quantized variants will be ~2-3x cheaper per token than full float; latency depends on concurrency and batch window. for predictable latency at scale dedicated pools with fixed GPU config are recommended. i can share a benchmark sheet.

[10:05] Maya: linking the docs here: https://docs.redwood.ai/quickstart and the sandbox invite will use service account creds for the POC.

[12:00] Ethan: now switching to TypeScript: npm i @redwood/inference (package alias might change); usage: 
- import { Redwood } from '@redwood/inference' 
- const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY }) 
- await client.text.generate({ model: 'rw-instruct-7b', input: '...' })
small differences: promises/streams vs python generator style. streaming is supported in both.

[14:30] Ruth: about streaming — is partial transcript stored? are there audit logs per token?
[14:38] Maya: audit logs capture request metadata and response hashes. streaming chunks aren't separately retained unless you enable verbose debugging. full details in security pack.

[16:50] Ethan: quick demo of the Console:
- I sign in to consola (note: transcript spelled console sometimes), go to Projects -> Sandbox-PoC -> Routes. you can see per-route token cost and latency heatmap. there is an Optimize tab that suggests batching and cache profiles.

[18:10] Ethan: here's an example: Optimize flagged that your typical short-chat workload benefits from prefix caching and a small batching window (30ms) — this reduced cost by ~18% in a recent customer trace. the console links to suggested model quantization profiles.

[19:45] Lena: can we pin a model version for the pilot? we need stable behavior.
[19:50] Ethan: yes, pinning is supported at route level, you can pin exact model build and rollout percent if you want gradual deploys.

[22:05] Samir: networking: if we use sandbox hosted, can we restrict egress or lock to our VPC?
[22:13] Maya: hosted sandbox doesn't natively join your VPC; for production we offer VPC peering and private deploy options. for the POC we can set IP allowlists and static egress ranges.

[24:30] Ruth: about compliance — are you SOC2/ISO? how do we do key rotation?
[24:37] Maya: redwood is SOC2 Type II (contact docket for latest cert). key rotation: you can rotate API keys in Console and issue short-lived service account tokens. for private deployments you can integrate your KMS. we'll attach the security bundle in follow-up.

[26:55] Ethan: some gotchas we've seen: 
- CI sometimes accidentally commits .env with keys — add .env to .gitignore and use secrets manager. 
- watch for tokenization mismatch: model families tokenize differently, so perf numbers can vary. 
- if you use continuous batching, set max latency SLO to avoid spikes.

[29:40] Diego: the team uses Terraform + Helm for infra. any infra-as-code templates?
[29:46] Maya: yes, we'll include a terraform snippet for route creation and the console has an api for programmatic route config. ethan will attach examples.

[32:10] Ethan: quick live run — i'm going to run a local python demo against the sandbox key (obfuscated in demo). watch the streaming: you'll see chunked responses and client-side assembly. please excuse audio lag while i paste.
[33:05] [Demo noises / short pause as screen share switched] 
[33:30] Ethan: result came back ~450ms for a 200 token response in this noisy env. again, sandbox isn't a hard SLA environment.

[35:10] Lena: good to see. can you share the code snippet for streaming in TS?
[35:18] Ethan: yes - basically create a stream handler, on('data') append to buffer, on('end') flush. i'll paste the example in the shared notes.

[37:40] Ruth: curious about data retention and customer content — are inputs used for training?
[37:46] Maya: by default redwood does not use customer data for model training for hosted enterprise customers; there are explicit flags and agreements for training data usage. audit logs and retention policies are configurable. we'll include the DPA in the follow-up.

[40:05] Samir: is there an ability to run inference on-prem later if pilot succeeds?
[40:12] Maya: yes, we offer private deployments and an on-prem package if you need air-gapped environment. the APIs are consistent across modes which makes migration easier.

[42:00] Ethan: Optimize tip: for retrieval-augmented workloads, enable prefix KV caching — store persistent KV across similar sessions. it dramatically cuts repeated attention compute. some transcription: KV cache sometimes came out as K V cash in older transcriptions.

[43:20] Lena: any recommended quantized model family for our use-case? we do multi-turn summarization, medium length.
[43:28] Ethan: try rw-instruct-7b-q8 for cost balance; if you need better long-context handling bump to 13b quant variants. we'll add model recommendation matrix to the doc.

[45:50] Diego: ok can we get a skeleton of the dev repo for the POC? folder layout, sample tests?
[45:57] Maya: yes — we'll share a starter repo with python and ts examples, dockerfile, and GH Actions workflow. it will include smoke tests and a sample metrics emitter for latency.

[47:10] Ruth: last security nit: do you support SAML SSO for Console access?
[47:17] Maya: yes, SAML and OIDC both supported for orgs, RBAC available. we can map roles to route-level permissions.

[49:00] Ethan: wrapping up — recap of action items: maya will send sandbox invite and quickstart doc; ethan will push github actions/terraform snippets; lena will run quickstart and share perf by next wednesday. also schedule security deep dive.
[49:30] Maya: perfect. any final questions?
[49:35] Samir: small one — do you have client libraries for internal telemetry/metrics?
[49:40] Ethan: we expose hooks and recommend open-telemetry integration; sample instrumentation included in the starter repo.
[50:10] Lena: thanks all, that was helpful.
[50:16] Maya: thanks everyone, i'll follow up with the bundle.

End of transcript.

Meeting recap (auto):
- Objective: on-board devs to Redwood SDKs and verify auth/CI patterns for POC.
- Outcome: agreement to provision sandbox and share starter repo + security docs.
- Risks: need to validate latency/cost in customer env; security deep dive required for SOC2/KMS specifics.

References & links shared in call:
- Quickstart: https://docs.redwood.ai/quickstart
- Starter repo (to be shared): https://github.com/redwood-inference/pocexamples (placeholder)
- Security bundle: https://docs.redwood.ai/security-bundle (will be attached)

Notes on transcript quality: medium — some proper nouns and acronyms may be mis-transcribed, occasional speaker labels inferred.

Explicit next steps (restated):
1) Redwood (Maya/Ethan) to send sandbox access, starter repo, GH Actions workflow, terraform snippet, and security bundle by 2025-07-24.
2) VantaWave (Lena) to run quickstart and post perf numbers to shared spreadsheet by 2025-07-30.
3) Schedule security deep dive (Ruth + Redwood security) for week of 2025-07-28.

Keywords: SDK, Python, TypeScript, API key, service account, CI, GitHub Actions, sandbox, console, optimize, batching, KV cache, quantization, SOC2, KMS, VPC, private deploy

title: Proto onramp: Py/TS auth & Console integration walkthrough
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-06-23
Start: 15:30 PT
Duration: ~52m
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Jules Moreno (Redwood SE), Nina Patel (Redwood DevAdv), Priya Shah (Veridian Lead Platform Eng), Liam O'Connor (Veridian Frontend Eng), Sara Kim (Veridian Security), Devon Reeves (Veridian ML)

Summary: quick hands-on walkthrough of Redwood Python + TypeScript client quickstarts, auth patterns (API key, org sandbox, service principal), Console tour (routes, models, tokens), and initial optimize suggestions (batching/caching). Goal: get Veridian running a first integration in their sandbox and agree POC next steps.

Topics: auth & env setup, repo quickstart run, streaming vs non-streaming, console route config, initial perf knobs (batching/kv cache/quantization), security questions (KMS, audit logs, SSO).

[00:00] Ethan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Can we do quick intros? I'll kick off, I'm Ethan, AE on the Redwood team, and Jules is our SE who will lead the demo.
[00:18] Priya: Hi, I'm Priya, lead platform, running the integrations for Veridian. Sorry for the background noise, we are switching teams.
[00:25] Liam: Liam here from frontend, we'll be using the TS client.
[00:31] Sara: Sara, security -- I'll focus on compliance and KMS questions.
[00:36] Devon: Devon, ML infra, I'll run the benchmark tests.
[00:41] Jules: Great, thanks. High-level plan: auth and env setup, run the Python quickstart to show token generation and a basic chat call, then TS quickstart for frontend wiring, then Console tour and optimize tips. Questions before we start?
[01:02] Priya: Timeline, we want to have a POC in two weeks. Also need to confirm VPC/on-prem options for PHI.
[01:10] Ethan: Noted. We'll cover private deployment notes and then follow up with a security package.
[01:18] Jules: Cool. I'll share my screen and open the quickstart repo — apologies if the transcript misnames things, but link will be in follow up: https://redwood.ai/quickstart-proto (demo repo).

--- Quickstart & Auth walkthrough ---

[02:05] Jules: First file is the Python quickstart. It's a tiny virtualenv, pip install redwood-client, set REDWOOD_API_KEY env var, and run sample_chat.py.
[02:22] Liam: Quick q: do you recommend API keys for dev or service principal for CI?
[02:29] Jules: For dev sandbox API key is fine, for CI we recommend service principals / short-lived tokens or a vault with KMS. For enterprise we support org-service tokens integrated with SSO.
[02:44] Priya: Can we rotate keys via console? Sara: that's critical for us.
[02:49] Nina: Yes, Console supports token management + rotation. You can scope tokens to routes and duration. We'll show that in the Console section.
[02:58] Jules: Running the sample now, you'll see the call — it's a standard REST under the hood but we stream the response in the client. Note the snippet: client = Redwood(api_key=os.environ['REDWOOD_API_KEY']); resp = client.chat.create(model='redwood-rt-3', messages=[...])
[03:24] Devon: redwood-rt-3 — is that one of your low-latency variants?
[03:28] Jules: Yes it's in the catalog, optimized for interactive latency. We can also route to cheaper quantized variants for background tasks.

[03:48] Ethan: A quick aside on costs — our Optimize tooling suggests batching at the request layer and KV prefix caching to reduce repeat tokens. We'll include a sample.

[04:10] Jules: Now switching to TS quickstart. NPM install @redwood/client, set process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY or use session token in browser. Important: in-browser keys should be short-lived or proxied via your backend.
[04:28] Liam: We plan to call from the frontend for UX reasons; what's the recommended pattern?
[04:34] Jules: Proxy via a minimal backend. Use a session token minted by your backend that is scoped and short-lived. We do provide a client helper for browser flows but it's not intended for long-lived secrets.

[05:05] Priya: About private deployment — can we run Redwood private behind our VPC and still use Console?
[05:12] Ethan: Yes, Private deployment supports a customer-managed control plane. Console can be deployed as a managed or self-hosted component; we'll share the architecture doc.
[05:22] Sara: KMS/HSM integration? We need HSM for key storage and customer-managed keys.
[05:28] Nina: We integrate with major KMS providers and can support bring-your-own-HSM for Private. We'll attach security slides.

--- Console tour ---

[06:10] Jules: Opening Console. Top-left is the org selector — you can provision a sandbox org. Under Models > Catalog you can pin model versions.
[06:28] Jules: Routes are where you wire model+prompts+promotions. You can attach rollout policies: canary percent, fallback models.
[06:40] Devon: Does the route config expose batching controls?
[06:44] Jules: Yes, you can set batching window (ms), max batch size, and per-route KV cache settings. There's also an Optimize tab that suggests knob values based on observed latency/cost.
[06:59] Priya: Audit logs and traceability?
[07:03] Jules: Under Observability you get per-token cost breakdown, traces, and audit logs that can be shipped to your SIEM.

[07:30] Ethan: Quick demo of generating a scoped token: create -> scope to Route 'veridian-test', TTL 1h, allowed ops: chat, embeddings. Token created and I copy to env.
[07:50] Liam: When proxied from backend, do we need CORS config?
[07:54] Jules: Yes typical CORS; if using session tokens from our auth helper you can avoid exposing the master key.

--- Live run / small hiccup ---

[08:30] Jules: Running the TS sample — hitting the route, streaming chunks.
[08:43] (audio: keyboard clack)
[08:47] Jules: Hmm, I'm hitting a 401 on the sandbox token — likely expired in this demo environment. Give me 30s to recreate.
[09:10] Ethan: This is a good capture of real-world: token TTLs and rotation come up in every integration; we'll include short scripts to mint/rotate keys in the follow-up.
[09:22] Jules: Recreated, re-run — ok streaming now, partial tokens arriving.
[09:41] Priya: The streaming latency looks good from the demo screen share. Can we measure from our infra?
[09:47] Devon: We'll run simple curl/bench locally and report median p50/p95.

[10:05] Jules: On the server side, you get SDK helpers: client.chat.stream(...) for chunked responses, and client.chat.create(...) for non-streaming. Also there are helpers for embeddings and reranking.
[10:23] Liam: Any special headers or correlation IDs we should pass?
[10:27] Jules: We recommend passing x-request-id and user_agent metadata. Console traces will link requests by these ids.

--- Security deep-dive snippets ---

[11:00] Sara: Can you show the audit log format quickly? We need to see fields captured.
[11:06] Jules: Sure, logs contain timestamp, request_id, route, model, user_id (if provided), token_scope but not raw prompt content unless you enable retention for evals. For PHI workloads you'd generally disable retention and encrypt logs at rest.
[11:28] Sara: Do you support field-level redaction?
[11:32] Nina: Not out-of-the-box; we recommend local preprocessing to scrub PHI before sending. For Private deployments we can pair with your DLP pipeline.

[12:10] Priya: Regarding data residency, we need EU-only hosting for some datasets.
[12:16] Ethan: We have region pins and can supply Private in EU regions. We'll note that in the security package.

--- Optimize & performance knobs ---

[13:00] Jules: From Optimize: batching window, max batch size, and KV cache TTL give the largest unit-cost wins. Example: for support-chat workload with 80% prefix reuse, enabling prefix KV cache reduced token spend by ~38% in our benchmark.
[13:28] Devon: Curious about batching impact on tail latency.
[13:31] Jules: We model that — you can set latency SLOs and Optimize will propose batch sizes that meet p95 targets. For interactive UI we often keep batching window <50ms with small batch sizes. For background generation increase window.

[14:10] Ethan: We'll include a starter config for your traffic profile — Jules will draft suggested knobs after you share sample traffic.

--- Customer questions and clarifications ---

[15:05] Priya: Can Redwood's client run inside our Lambda or ephemeral containers?
[15:10] Jules: Yes. The client is lightweight. For cold starts prefer pinned model variants or warmed pools; Dedicated capacity also helps.
[15:22] Liam: For frontend dev, is TypeScript typings fully supported?
[15:26] Nina: Yes, the client ships with typedefs and examples. We'll attach a small snippet for generics and streaming handlers.

[16:00] Sara: About compliance: do you provide SOC2 report and pen test summary?
[16:05] Ethan: Yes, we have SOC2 and ISO artefacts. We'll share the security packet via secure link.

[16:34] Jules: Small demo: I'll show a route rollout config — canary 10% to redwood-rt-3, fallback to redwood-q-2 if latency spike or error rate >2%.
[16:53] Devon: Can we run canary with different quantization levels?
[16:57] Jules: Yes, route variants can differ by model family and quantization profile; Console handles automatic fallback to compatible variants.

--- Troubleshooting common errors ---

[18:00] Jules: Common 401: wrong key or TTL. 429: hit concurrency or quota; Console shows rate usage and you can request burst capacity. 500: service side, check trace.
[18:20] Priya: For 429s, can we queue at the client?
[18:23] Jules: We recommend exponential backoff and local rate limiting; for guaranteed throughput Dedicated capacity is best.

[19:10] Ethan: I want to call out the dev DX: we provide a small Docker image for reproducible quickstarts — good for your CI. Link will be in follow up.

[20:00] Liam: Any gotchas for SSR frameworks and streaming?
[20:05] Jules: If you use SSR with streaming responses, ensure you flush chunks to the client and handle timeouts; the client exposes helpers for chunk parsing.

--- Wrap-up, next steps & POC scope draft ---

[21:15] Ethan: Quick recap: 1) we'll send quickstart repo and console screenshots, 2) you (Priya/Devon) will spin up sandbox org and run the two quickstarts, 3) we'll provide security packet, 4) run latency/cost runs and report back by 2025-06-30, 5) schedule POC kickoff for week of 2025-07-07.
[21:42] Priya: That works. For the POC scope, we want a covered feature set: chat in clinician portal, embeddings for search, and audit logging.
[21:53] Jules: Noted. We'll draft a one-page POC scope and responsibilities.

[22:10] Sara: Please include dataflow diagrams for Private deployment and KMS flows.
[22:15] Ethan: We'll include those.

[22:36] Liam: Any recommended initial model? Considering latency and cost, which would you pick?
[22:40] Jules: For clinician chat choose interactive low-latency family (redwood-rt-3) and enable KV prefix caching; for bulk summarization or batch tasks pick a quantized variant. We'll add a table of recommended pairs in follow-up.

[23:05] Ethan: I'll send the links and isolate the console steps to create a scoped token for your sandbox. Jules will include sample code snippets for minting session tokens.

[23:30] (small cross talk about calendar invites)

[24:00] Priya: One more question: do you have a policy for model change notifications and eval regression alerts?
[24:10] Nina: Yes, Console has eval hooks and alerts; we can wire your prompt sets and threshold-based regression alerts.

[25:00] Jules: Ok final note: we'll also attach a minimalbench script — curl-based — for p50/p95 measurement and token cost calc. Devon, that should help for initial perf numbers.

[25:22] Devon: Great, we'll run it.

[26:00] Ethan: Any competitor risks we should be aware of? Priya: We're also talking to Anthropic for a deeper safety set and OpenAI for managed endpoint simplicity.
[26:10] Ethan: Understood. We'll emphasize our unified runtime and private deployment story in the follow up.

[26:40] Jules: Thanks everyone — we'll email the repo and security packet. Do you want a 30m POC kickoff or a 60m deep dive for infra?
[26:50] Priya: 30m kickoff first, then a technical deep-dive with infra next week.

[27:05] Ethan: I'll create the invites. Anything else?
[27:10] Sara: No, thanks.
[27:14] Liam: Thanks all.
[27:18] (meeting ends)

Notes on transcript quality: there are a few micro-pauses and some mis-heard tokens (e.g., model names may be abbreviated). Shared artifacts: quickstart repo (https://redwood.ai/quickstart-proto), Console screenshots, security packet (sent via secure link), minimalbench script.

End of transcript.
