[12:16] Ethan: We have region pins and can supply Private in EU regions. We'll note that in the security package.

--- Optimize & performance knobs ---

[13:00] Jules: From Optimize: batching window, max batch size, and KV cache TTL give the largest unit-cost wins. Example: for support-chat workload with 80% prefix reuse, enabling prefix KV cache reduced token spend by ~38% in our benchmark.
[13:28] Devon: Curious about batching impact on tail latency.
[13:31] Jules: We model that — you can set latency SLOs and Optimize will propose batch sizes that meet p95 targets. For interactive UI we often keep batching window <50ms with small batch sizes. For background generation increase window.

[14:10] Ethan: We'll include a starter config for your traffic profile — Jules will draft suggested knobs after you share sample traffic.

--- Customer questions and clarifications ---

[15:05] Priya: Can Redwood's client run inside our Lambda or ephemeral containers?
[15:10] Jules: Yes. The client is lightweight. For cold starts prefer pinned model variants or warmed pools; Dedicated capacity also helps.
[15:22] Liam: For frontend dev, is TypeScript typings fully supported?
[15:26] Nina: Yes, the client ships with typedefs and examples. We'll attach a small snippet for generics and streaming handlers.

[16:00] Sara: About compliance: do you provide SOC2 report and pen test summary?
[16:05] Ethan: Yes, we have SOC2 and ISO artefacts. We'll share the security packet via secure link.

[16:34] Jules: Small demo: I'll show a route rollout config — canary 10% to redwood-rt-3, fallback to redwood-q-2 if latency spike or error rate >2%.
[16:53] Devon: Can we run canary with different quantization levels?
[16:57] Jules: Yes, route variants can differ by model family and quantization profile; Console handles automatic fallback to compatible variants.

--- Troubleshooting common errors ---

[18:00] Jules: Common 401: wrong key or TTL. 429: hit concurrency or quota; Console shows rate usage and you can request burst capacity. 500: service side, check trace.
[18:20] Priya: For 429s, can we queue at the client?
[18:23] Jules: We recommend exponential backoff and local rate limiting; for guaranteed throughput Dedicated capacity is best.

[19:10] Ethan: I want to call out the dev DX: we provide a small Docker image for reproducible quickstarts — good for your CI. Link will be in follow up.

[20:00] Liam: Any gotchas for SSR frameworks and streaming?
[20:05] Jules: If you use SSR with streaming responses, ensure you flush chunks to the client and handle timeouts; the client exposes helpers for chunk parsing.

--- Wrap-up, next steps & POC scope draft ---

[21:15] Ethan: Quick recap: 1) we'll send quickstart repo and console screenshots, 2) you (Priya/Devon) will spin up sandbox org and run the two quickstarts, 3) we'll provide security packet, 4) run latency/cost runs and report back by 2025-06-30, 5) schedule POC kickoff for week of 2025-07-07.
[21:42] Priya: That works. For the POC scope, we want a covered feature set: chat in clinician portal, embeddings for search, and audit logging.
[21:53] Jules: Noted. We'll draft a one-page POC scope and responsibilities.

[22:10] Sara: Please include dataflow diagrams for Private deployment and KMS flows.
[22:15] Ethan: We'll include those.

[22:36] Liam: Any recommended initial model? Considering latency and cost, which would you pick?
[22:40] Jules: For clinician chat choose interactive low-latency family (redwood-rt-3) and enable KV prefix caching; for bulk summarization or batch tasks pick a quantized variant. We'll add a table of recommended pairs in follow-up.

[23:05] Ethan: I'll send the links and isolate the console steps to create a scoped token for your sandbox. Jules will include sample code snippets for minting session tokens.

[23:30] (small cross talk about calendar invites)

[24:00] Priya: One more question: do you have a policy for model change notifications and eval regression alerts?
[24:10] Nina: Yes, Console has eval hooks and alerts; we can wire your prompt sets and threshold-based regression alerts.

[25:00] Jules: Ok final note: we'll also attach a minimalbench script — curl-based — for p50/p95 measurement and token cost calc. Devon, that should help for initial perf numbers.

[25:22] Devon: Great, we'll run it.

[26:00] Ethan: Any competitor risks we should be aware of? Priya: We're also talking to Anthropic for a deeper safety set and OpenAI for managed endpoint simplicity.
[26:10] Ethan: Understood. We'll emphasize our unified runtime and private deployment story in the follow up.

[26:40] Jules: Thanks everyone — we'll email the repo and security packet. Do you want a 30m POC kickoff or a 60m deep dive for infra?
[26:50] Priya: 30m kickoff first, then a technical deep-dive with infra next week.

[27:05] Ethan: I'll create the invites. Anything else?
[27:10] Sara: No, thanks.
[27:14] Liam: Thanks all.
[27:18] (meeting ends)

Notes on transcript quality: there are a few micro-pauses and some mis-heard tokens (e.g., model names may be abbreviated). Shared artifacts: quickstart repo (https://redwood.ai/quickstart-proto), Console screenshots, security packet (sent via secure link), minimalbench script.

End of transcript.

title: Data sovereignty & logging discovery - Constellation Bank
preview:
Meeting header: 2025-06-18 14:00 UTC | Duration: ~48m | Recording: auto
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Leah Gomez (Redwood SE), Marco Reyes (Constellation - Head Cloud & Data), Aisha Khan (Constellation - InfoSec), Tom Elliot (Constellation - Compliance)

Auto summary (auto-generated, may be imperfect):
- Constellation is evaluating options for running Redwood LLM workloads with strict EU/US data residency and FINRA-like audit retention requirements.
- Primary concerns: storing raw transcripts, long-term audit logs, cross-border backups, SOC2 evidence, HIPAA/GLBA overlap for certain products.
- Redwood AE/SE explained Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private tradeoffs, logging controls, KMS/HSM options, and potential mitigations.
- Next steps: share compliance pack, pricing, network scoping, run benchmark under NDA.

Topics: data residency, log retention, encryption at rest, KMS, private deployment, SOC2/ISO, GLBA/FFIEC, PCI-DSS mention, redaction hooks, audit logs, export restrictions.

--- Transcript ---
[00:00] Ethan (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Uh, quick mic check—can you hear me okay?
[00:05] Marco (Constellation): Yep, loud and clear.
[00:07] Aisha (Constellation): All good here.
[00:09] Ethan: Awesome. I'll do a 5 minute intro, Leah will cover the technical controls and then we'll get into your questions. The goal is to understand your residency and logging constraints so we can map to Hosted, Dedicated, or Private.
[00:22] Marco: Cool. High level, Constellation has two product buckets. One is customer-facing chat assistants (EU customers) where we cannot move transcripts outside EU; the other is back-office analytics which can run in US but with stricter audit logs for 7 years. We also have to satisfy GLBA and internal FFIEC guidance.
[00:42] Ethan: Understood. When you say cannot move transcripts out of EU, do you mean raw request/response payloads, or any derived embeddings/metadata as well?
[00:50] Marco: Primarily raw transcripts and anything that can be used to reconstruct PII. Metadata like latencies or token counts are fine but no raw content outside region.
[01:01] Leah (Redwood SE): Got it. Practically that maps to per-region tenancy. On Hosted, we can pin to an EU region for data at rest and we have policy controls to disable cross-region replication, however backups might be a sticking point depending on retention policy.
[01:15] Tom (Compliance): Can you give us specifics on backups? If everything is EU-only, we need to ensure no offsite restore in US.
[01:22] Leah: Yes, so we have two patterns: Hosted EU-only mode and Private (VPC) where the control plane is customer-managed. In Hosted EU-only, backups are stored in the same region and we can disable global replication for your org. Note: our standard Hosted contracts have shared control plane for multi-region failover unless you request Dedicated or Private.
[01:38] Aisha: What's the evidence for that? We need an attestation or docs for our auditors.
[01:42] Ethan: We'll send the SOC 2 + ISO pack and a short note describing regional backup controls. We'll also include a diagram showing encryption and KMS boundaries.
[01:49] Marco: Good.
[01:50] Leah: Quick note on keys — we support customer-managed KMS for Dedicated and Private. Hosted supports Redwood-managed KMS by default but you can elect a bring-your-own-key flow for enterprise tiers and we integrate with common cloud KMS and H S M providers.
[02:03] Marco: B-Y-O-K is important. Our policy says customer keys for production PII must be used.
[02:07] Leah: Then Dedicated or Private is the recommended path. Dedicated gives you reserved capacity and single-tenant GPU pools, but the control plane still talks to Redwood services unless you go Private where the control plane is self-hosted or VPC-hosted.
[02:20] Ethan: To be transparent, Private solves the cross-border control plane issue entirely—you're running the control plane in your environment, we provide the package and support. It is heavier ops work but gives max isolation.
[02:31] [crosstalk] Marco: And network egress? We need to ensure logs don't leak egress to a third country.
[02:36] Leah: Yes, you can restrict egress with VPC-only endpoints and private peering. We can provide a peering checklist and subnet/security group templates. We'll include that in the follow-up.

[02:50] Marco: On logging: we are required to retain audit logs for seven years for certain transactions — traceability of who called the model, what prompt template was used, and a hash of the response. But we cannot retain raw transcripts longer than 30 days without explicit consent. How does Redwood separate audit logs from raw request storage?
[03:12] Leah: We separate three layers: 1) Access/audit logs (who, when, model version, route) which are metadata and can be retained long-term; 2) Request/response payloads (raw transcripts) which we store by default for 30 days for debugging and can disable or shorten retention; 3) Derived artifacts like embeddings — those are treated as customer data and follow region policies. We can implement field-level redaction so payloads are masked before storage.
[03:34] Tom: Field-level redaction—who does it? Is it at your edge or do we need to pre-process?
[03:38] Leah: We offer client-side redaction libraries, and also server-side pre-ingest hooks where you can specify regex or JSON paths to redact. There is some latency tradeoff if you do synchronous server-side redaction.
[03:47] Marco: Are redactions reversible? We need to ensure redaction isn't reversible by Redwood or anyone.
[03:52] Leah: Redaction is irreversible; we hash redacted segments with a salted hash if you'd like to keep a reference pointer. The salt can be customer-provided via KMS so Redwood can't reconstruct.

[04:05] Aisha: For HIPAA — we also have a small set of internally facing clinical workflows. Would Redwood sign a BAA?
[04:12] Ethan: We have customers under HIPAA. We can discuss BAA in parallel; initial step is to review your use case and data flows. BAAs are supported on Private and Dedicated plans generally. Hosted can be evaluated but requires specific contractual addenda.
[04:26] Aisha: Okay. What about logging of full prompts for eDiscovery? Our legal team asks for legal hold capabilities.
[04:33] Leah: We can export audit logs to customer S3 buckets or SIEM. For legal hold, we can toggle retention flags and put data under retention hold, but again that implies raw content retention — which contradicts your earlier 30 day rule — so we'd need a policy definition per data class.
[04:49] Marco: Right, so some datasets need 7yr audit metadata, some raw transcripts 30 days.
[04:54] Leah: Exactly — we can implement a tagging policy so certain routes are auto-classified. Tagged routes can be routed to Dedicated or to a private tenant where you permit longer raw retention.

[05:05] Tom: About regulators — if they request data, what is the process? Cross-border requests are our pain point.
[05:11] Ethan: Process is: Redwood supports export requests via documented process; for Private deployments, you control the data and handle legal requests. For Hosted/Dedicated, we assist but we may need legal coordination. We can provide an export log with hash references and explain the chain of custody.
[05:26] Marco: Can you guarantee no debug logs with PII ever leave the host region — like transient logs?
[05:31] Leah: We have a debug-mode that can capture extra traces but it's opt-in per request and flagged. For production we recommend not enabling full debug capturing. We can ensure transient logs aren't persisted to cross-region buckets.

[05:45] Ethan: Quick question—are you leaning to Hosted with strict configs, Dedicated, or Private? Which is your risk posture?
[05:53] Marco: Risk posture is cautious. For customer-facing EU chat: needs strong residency and customer keys — likely Dedicated or Private. For back-office analytics: might use Hosted EU but we need longer audit retention exported to our SIEM.
[06:06] Aisha: Also we have to feed logs into our SOC tooling — Splunk. Do you have connectors?
[06:11] Leah: Yes, we have S3 exports + direct Splunk HEC ingestion in Dedicated and Private. For Hosted we support periodic S3 exports.

[06:23] Marco: One concern: we use tokenization for payment tokens. Do you have PCI implications?
[06:28] Ethan: Redwood doesn't store raw card numbers by default. If your workflow sends raw card data, you should tokenize before calling the model. We can support pre-ingest tokenization or a sidecar that strips out PANs. For PCI-sensitive flows you probably want Private deployment or a certified PCI environment.
[06:45] Tom: For the seven year audit logs — who is responsible for retention costs?
[06:49] Ethan: If logs are exported to your bucket, retention costs are yours. If stored on Redwood's retention tier, it's part of an add-on (we'll include pricing).

[06:58] Marco: On SSO and role-based access—SSO via SAML is required, and we need fine-grained RBAC for model rollout controls.
[07:05] Leah: We support SAML and OIDC. RBAC is available on Dedicated/Private with per-route permissions; Hosted enterprise also has RBAC but with some limits on custom roles.

[07:16] Aisha: Audit trail for admin actions—who changed keys, who deployed new model versions—this is critical.
[07:21] Leah: That's part of the admin audit logs. It's retained separately and can be exported. We can include the specific schema in the compliance pack.

[07:30] Ethan: I'll pause and ask if there are any immediate blockers for you to move to a pilot?
[07:35] Marco: NDA and assurance on key management. We'll want a short pilot where keys are customer-managed and model endpoints are EU-only. Also we need a benchmark that shows latency under your batching for 512 token sequences.
[07:49] Leah: We can run that. For the latency benchmark we'll need a small sample traffic profile and we'll supply a test harness. Note: Dedicated gives more predictable p99 latencies. Hosted has best time-to-value.

[08:02] Tom: Another question — who has access to the KV cache? Can the cache contain PII?
[08:07] Leah: KV cache is ephemeral and local to the GPU node. By default it's not persisted across restarts, but for safety we recommend not caching PII or using tokenization. We also offer an option to disable caching for sensitive routes.
[08:19] Marco: Does disabling cache impact cost heavily?
[08:22] Leah: It can increase CPU/GPU compute and latency; cost delta depends on token volume. We'll model that in the pricing matrix.

[08:31] Aisha: For encryption at rest, we want AES-256 and customer key control. You mentioned KMS — which vendors?
[08:36] Leah: We integrate with AWS KMS, Google KMS, Azure Key Vault and HSM partners like Thales and AWS CloudHSM. For Private we can wire into on-prem HSMs.
[08:48] Marco: Do you do key rotation?
[08:50] Leah: Yes, key rotation policies are supported; rotations can be scheduled and we maintain a key history for decryptable artifacts. For anything you want to be irreversibly redacted we use hashing as discussed.

[09:01] Ethan: I'm going to share a couple of quick links in the follow-up: SOC2 pack, Private deployment whitepaper, network peering checklist and the bench harness.
[09:09] Marco: Great.

[09:12] Aisha: One more operational note — our internal auditors will ask for an incident response SLA and proof of forensically sound logs in case of compromise. Do you have playbooks?
[09:22] Leah: We have an IR playbook and incident reporting timelines for enterprise customers. For Dedicated we have a 24h initial response SLA; for Private we coordinate IR with your team since you're hosting. We'll include a redacted playbook in the pack.

[09:36] Ethan: Ok, quick recap and then we'll do Q/A. Recap: 1) You'll likely pilot EU-only customer-facing chat on Dedicated or Private with customer-managed KMS; 2) Back-office analytics could be Hosted with exported audit logs to your SIEM; 3) We'll send compliance artifacts, peering checklist, pricing, and benchmark harness.
[09:55] Marco: Yep, that sounds right.

[10:00] Tom: Are there any known limitations around encrypted backups — like metadata that still leaks region info?
[10:06] Leah: Metadata like object size and timestamps exist; we can minimize metadata exposure but not eliminate it entirely. If absolute metadata minimization is required, Private is the path.

[10:15] Marco: Final question—do you support country-level data residency controls for EU member states separately, or only region-level (eu-west-1 etc)?
[10:23] Leah: Currently region-level (cloud region) controls are the primary mechanism. Country-level would need Private or specialized hosting in that country — we can consult on options and partners for country-specific hosting.

[10:36] Ethan: Thanks — let's open the floor for any detailed questions?
[10:40] Marco: Not at the moment — just looking forward to the docs.
[10:42] Aisha: We'll review the SOC2 and get back with specific control mappings.
[10:46] Ethan: Great.
[10:47] Leah: One more note: if you choose Dedicated we can run a short security assessment with your team and map control gaps. For Private we have an install guide and an air-gapped checklist.
[10:58] Marco: Perfect.

[11:00] Ethan: Okay, next steps quick list: Ethan to send SOC2/ISO and pricing; Leah to send peering checklist and bench harness; Marco to share residency spec; schedule technical scoping for next week.
[11:14] Tom: We'll target the scoping for June 25th.
[11:17] Ethan: Works for us. Thanks everyone, we'll follow up in email.
[11:21] Marco: Thanks.

[11:25] (call ends)

Notes on transcription quality: There are a few minor mis-hearings, e.g., HSM sometimes transcribed as "H S M", KV as "K V", and some punctuation missing.

References/links mentioned (will be included in follow-up): https://redwood.example.com/compliance/soc2.pdf, https://redwood.example.com/docs/private-deployments, https://redwood.example.com/network/peering-checklist

Explicit next steps (from transcript):
- Ethan: Share SOC2/ISO pack and pricing matrix (Ethan Park)
- Leah: Draft VPC peering checklist and bench harness + network diag (Leah Gomez)
- Marco: Provide Constellation's residency + regulator list (Marco Reyes)

End of transcript.

[09:12] Aisha: One more operational note — our internal auditors will ask for an incident response SLA and proof of forensically sound logs in case of compromise. Do you have playbooks?
[09:22] Leah: We have an IR playbook and incident reporting timelines for enterprise customers. For Dedicated we have a 24h initial response SLA; for Private we coordinate IR with your team since you're hosting. We'll include a redacted playbook in the pack.

[09:36] Ethan: Ok, quick recap and then we'll do Q/A. Recap: 1) You'll likely pilot EU-only customer-facing chat on Dedicated or Private with customer-managed KMS; 2) Back-office analytics could be Hosted with exported audit logs to your SIEM; 3) We'll send compliance artifacts, peering checklist, pricing, and benchmark harness.
[09:55] Marco: Yep, that sounds right.

[10:00] Tom: Are there any known limitations around encrypted backups — like metadata that still leaks region info?
[10:06] Leah: Metadata like object size and timestamps exist; we can minimize metadata exposure but not eliminate it entirely. If absolute metadata minimization is required, Private is the path.

[10:15] Marco: Final question—do you support country-level data residency controls for EU member states separately, or only region-level (eu-west-1 etc)?
[10:23] Leah: Currently region-level (cloud region) controls are the primary mechanism. Country-level would need Private or specialized hosting in that country — we can consult on options and partners for country-specific hosting.

[10:36] Ethan: Thanks — let's open the floor for any detailed questions?
[10:40] Marco: Not at the moment — just looking forward to the docs.
[10:42] Aisha: We'll review the SOC2 and get back with specific control mappings.
[10:46] Ethan: Great.
[10:47] Leah: One more note: if you choose Dedicated we can run a short security assessment with your team and map control gaps. For Private we have an install guide and an air-gapped checklist.
[10:58] Marco: Perfect.

[11:00] Ethan: Okay, next steps quick list: Ethan to send SOC2/ISO and pricing; Leah to send peering checklist and bench harness; Marco to share residency spec; schedule technical scoping for next week.
[11:14] Tom: We'll target the scoping for June 25th.
[11:17] Ethan: Works for us. Thanks everyone, we'll follow up in email.
[11:21] Marco: Thanks.

[11:25] (call ends)

Notes on transcription quality: There are a few minor mis-hearings, e.g., HSM sometimes transcribed as "H S M", KV as "K V", and some punctuation missing.

References/links mentioned (will be included in follow-up): https://redwood.example.com/compliance/soc2.pdf, https://redwood.example.com/docs/private-deployments, https://redwood.example.com/network/peering-checklist

Explicit next steps (from transcript):
- Ethan: Share SOC2/ISO pack and pricing matrix (Ethan Park)
- Leah: Draft VPC peering checklist and bench harness + network diag (Leah Gomez)
- Marco: Provide Constellation's residency + regulator list (Marco Reyes)

End of transcript.

title: POC weekly - timeouts, 5xx, retries playbook
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-06-11
Time: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~50 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Luis Gonzalez (Redwood CSM); Anita Rao (BrightScale Eng Mgr), Samir Patel (BrightScale Platform Eng), Claire Dumont (BrightScale SRE), Oliver Brett (BrightScale Product)

Summary: quick sync after last weekend's spike. Main focus: recurring timeouts and 5xxs during the 8-11pm window, how retries are behaving, rate limit headers, and a temporary playbook for incident mitigation.

[00:00] Maya: hey everyone, thanks for joining, we'll try to keep to 50 minutes. Quick roll call — Luis, Ethan, you good?
[00:06] Luis: yep all good, thanks Maya.
[00:10] Anita: hi all, thanks for the quick follow-up. We had another blip saturday night and wanted to get details.
[00:18] Ethan: quick background — we saw a cluster of 502/504s and a lot of 429s during the traffic spike, the retry logic from the client was sometimes making things worse because the default backoff was too short. We triaged a first-pass friday night and tuned server side concurrency but want a consistent playbook.
[00:35] Samir: to add, our ingress reported long upstream timeouts for a subset of calls hitting the Redwood Dedicated pool, but interestingly only about 15% of calls. The rest were fine.
[00:45] Claire: yeah, from SRE side we saw connection resets and some long tail p95s around token decode. Might be KV cache thrash? Not sure.
[01:05] Maya: ok can we walk through the timeline you saw for 6/9? Was it a slow ramp or sudden spike?
[01:12] Anita: it was a sudden ad hoc batch job — roughly doubled QPS in 5 minutes then stayed elevated for 35. We had autoscaling earlier but throttling kicked in.
[01:25] Ethan: that pattern matches our thresholds — autoscaler scaled but there is a cool down, and some requests hit hot KV shards which increased latency and then the proxy timeouts triggered 504.
[01:40] Oliver: the business impact — product-facing latency spikes for search sessions, several failed flows for customers. We need a repeatable mitigation so pilot doesn't regress.
[01:55] Maya: got it. Two tracks: immediate mitigation (retry/backoff, client-side hedging, gateway rate-limit headers) and long-term (canary config, per-model concurrency limits, caching).
[02:10] Samir: what did you mean by hedging? duplicate calls?
[02:15] Ethan: small hedging — send a second request after a higher threshold, but that's risky. For now we recommend exponential backoff with jitter and respecting 429 Retry-After. Increase client-side initial backoff from 200ms to 750ms for this pilot traffic pattern.
[02:40] Claire: are there headers we can rely on from Redwood now? e.g., x-rate-limit, x-request-id?
[02:46] Maya: yes, we surface x-rw-req-id (trace id), x-rw-rate-limit-remaining, x-rw-retry-after in the pilot cluster. We'll share the exact header names and a Grafana dashboard link.
[03:10] Anita: we use an nginx ingress with envoy for some paths — we saw 502 from nginx. Could that be TLS renegotiation or keepalive settings?
[03:22] Ethan: nginx can show 502 when upstream closes, often due to long tail latencies from the runtime. We'll want to check connection reuse and upstream timeouts. Also please send us the ingress logs for the 20:00-21:00 window.
[04:00] Maya: quick interjection — competition note: you asked about Anthropic and OpenAI earlier — failures seem infra-level, not model quality. So switching models won't help unless you change routing and retries.
[04:28] Anita: ok, good to know.
[04:32] Ethan: can I share some concrete numbers from our internal load test? On a synthetic spike we saw error rate climb when concurrent in-flight requests per GPU exceeded ~120 for that specific model variant (quantized 4-bit). Once it hits that point, the p99 jumps and KV cache thrash increases CPU usage.
[04:55] Claire: 120 sounds lower than expected. Is that reachability per model or per-node?
[05:00] Ethan: per-node for that runtime config. We can tighten scheduling to 80 for pilot to avoid the tail. Lowering concurrency will increase cost but stabilize latency.
[05:18] Samir: can you do adaptive concurrency? like reduce concurrency when p95 > threshold?
[05:24] Ethan: yes, we have experimental adaptive concurrency in the Dedicated runtime; we can enable a safe mode for the pilot for a few hours to observe behavior. We'll recommend it as a canary setting.
[05:40] Maya: also note that our autoscaler has burst capacity but it's asynchronous — there's a 2 min cold start for new nodes which explains some of the 5xxs in that initial spike.
[05:55] Anita: so what's the short-term playbook if this happens again tonight? Say we see 40% increase in 5xx and 429s in 10 minutes.
[06:05] Ethan: step 1: set client backoff to 750ms initial, exp backoff with jitter, cap retries at 3. Step 2: enable conservative concurrency caps (80) on the model route. Step 3: temporarily increase ingress timeout to 90s to avoid upstream 502s while nodes come up. Step 4: trigger a manual burst allocation (Redwood dedicated burst) and enable adaptive concurrency. Step 5: post-incident, upload traces — we then examine KV cache hot keys and consider prefix/KV caching for common prompts.
[06:40] Claire: is there a risk that increasing ingress timeout just ties up connections and makes things worse?
[06:46] Ethan: yes, it's a tradeoff. We only recommend increasing timeouts during controlled mitigation while enabling burst nodes and concurrency caps; without those, longer timeouts can worsen head-of-line blocking.
[07:05] Anita: can you clarify the retry headers behavior? We saw clients ignoring Retry-After and doing immediate retries.
[07:12] Maya: we'll send the SDK doc note: clients should honor x-rw-retry-after and 429 Retry-After, and use exponential backoff with jitter. We also have a sample interceptor for common HTTP clients we'll attach.
[07:32] Oliver: would circuit breakers help? Could be useful at product level.
[07:37] Ethan: yes, circuit breakers are recommended for degraded states — trip for high error rate and partial fallback to cached responses or lower-fidelity model. We can template a circuit breaker config: 5xx rate > 8% over 2 minutes -> open for 30s, then half-open.
[08:00] Samir: any immediate logs we should provide to help triage?
[08:04] Ethan: request ids (x-rw-req-id), ingress/nginx logs, and a sample of the failed payloads (anonymized). Also the k8s pod logs around time of spike and any OOM or throttling events.
[08:20] Claire: will you do any changes on Redwood side immediately?
[08:25] Maya: yes, we'll push a soft guardrail to lower model concurrency for your pilot route overnight and enable the adaptive concurrency beta. We'll notify when it's live.
[08:36] Anita: appreciate that. A couple of practicals — can you also send a checklist we can give to on-call?
[08:41] Luis: absolutely. We'll produce a 1-page runbook: detection, triage, mitigation steps, and who to page at Redwood.
[08:50] Maya: ok let's walk through the runbook draft now — quick bullets.
[08:55] [Runbook readout]
[09:00] Detection: Alert if 5xx rate > 5% sustained for 2 minutes or p95 latency increases > 2x baseline.
[09:10] Triage: check x-rw-req-id for sample errors, check ingress for 502 patterns, check autoscaler events.
[09:22] Mitigation: 1) increase client backoff & cap retries, 2) set model concurrency to safe cap, 3) extend ingress timeout and trigger burst nodes, 4) enable adaptive concurrency.
[09:42] Postmortem steps: upload traces, map KV hot keys, consider model quant/variant routing if cost allows.
[10:00] Oliver: for the postmortem, are you able to help map specific prompt patterns? We suspect a single prompt type created hot keys.
[10:08] Ethan: yes we can run a quick correlation between prompt fingerprinting and KV misses if you provide a sample. We'll redact PII and keep only fingerprint hashes.
[10:22] Anita: I'll get the spike repro and ingress logs to you by EOD tomorrow.
[10:28] Maya: great. A note on monitoring — we'll add a Grafana dashboard with p50/p95/p99, error rates, in-flight per node, and KV cache hit rate for the pilot model.
[10:40] Claire: can we get an alert for KV hit rate < 70%?
[10:44] Ethan: yes, we'll add that threshold as an optional rule.
[11:00] [15 minute deep-dive request]
[11:05] Samir: one more question — how do retries interact with caching? If a retry hits a different node with a cold cache it could be slower.
[11:12] Ethan: exactly. That's why we bias retries with jitter and longer initial backoff; also sticky routing for retries helps — we can add a routing hint to prefer the same node for a short window. We'll propose sticky session headers for the pilot.
[11:30] Anita: that's helpful.
[11:35] Maya: ok to recap and next steps: Redwood: share trace bundle, push conservative concurrency guardrail tonight, share runbook and SDK interceptor, and add Grafana dashboard + alerts. BrightScale: share spike repro script, ingress logs, and sample failed payloads. Schedule follow-up deep dive for 6/18.
[11:55] All: agree.
[12:00] Meeting end.

References/Links mentioned: https://redwood.example.com/docs/retries (SDK interceptor sample), https://grafana.redwood.example.com/d/bs-pilot (pilot dashboard), link to shared S3 with traces: s3://redwood-pilot-logs/brightscale/2025-06-09/ (access request pending).

Action items repeated: Ethan Park to collect 5xx traces, Anita to provide spike repro, Luis to send runbook and pricing note.

End transcript.

title: Optimize playbook + cost model walkthrough with LumenWare
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-06-10
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes (approx)
Location: Zoom
Attendees: Rachel Kim (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Jonas Li (Redwood CSM), Maria Ortega (LumenWare Head of Platform), Devon Blake (LumenWare ML Eng), Priya Nair (CTO)

Auto-summary (auto):
Quick Optimize walkthrough focused on batching, prefix/KV cache recommendations, and an initial cost model tailored to LumenWare's conversational workload. Customer shared traffic profile and latency SLOs; Redwood proposed a pilot with prefix caching, conservative quantization, and autoscaling parameters. Agreed next steps include cost spreadsheet, pilot runbook, and trace dump.

Topics covered (auto-tags):
- Prefix vs KV cache behavior
- Batching and concurrency trade-offs
- Cost model assumptions (tokens, hit rates, quantization)
- Pilot design and acceptance criteria
- Security questions: VPC, KMS, audit logs

Transcript:
[00:00] Rachel: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Rachel Kim from Redwood, AE on this account, and with me is Ethan Park, our solutions engineer, and Jonas from CS. Maria, Priya, Devon, thanks for making time.
[00:12] Maria: Thanks, Rachel. We only have about 50 minutes so we'd like to focus on the Optimize recommendations — batching and cache — and how that maps to cost for our chat product.
[00:22] Ethan: Great. Quick agenda: we'll run through the Console Optimize tab, show batching profiles, prefix and KV cache behaviors, then a cost model drill with your traffic numbers. We'll end with proposed pilot and next steps.
[00:35] Devon: Awesome. For context, our workload is largely short chat turns, average 120 tokens request, 40 tokens response, high concurrency during peak. Latency target is 250ms p95.
[00:50] Ethan: Thanks. That's helpful. Two immediate levers we look at: batching and caching. Batching is how we group concurrent token generation to amortize per-token kernel cost; caching (prefix/KV) reduces tokens required when prompts share a common prefix or repeated history.
[01:10] Priya: Can you remind us, prefix cache vs KV cache, what's the practical difference? I always get them mixed.
[01:18] Ethan: Totally. Prefix cache is basically request-level caching for identical leading token sequences — think the conversation context you send repeatedly. KV cache is the KV memory for autoregressive generation that we persist across sessions for warm starts — so they behave differently in hit patterns and storage. Sometimes customers use \"prefix\" to mean the same as a request cache, and we see both.
[01:35] Maria: So in our earlier tests we had around 30% repeat prefixes for templated prompts. We also have reuse across user sessions for some support flows.
[01:45] Ethan: That 30% is actually pretty meaningful. In our cost model, even a 30% prefix-hit rate can reduce token spend by ~20-25% depending on reply lengths because you avoid re-sending the history tokens to the model.
[02:00] Rachel: We'll show the Console where Optimize surfaces this and then a spreadsheet that converts hit rates into dollars.
[02:08] [Screen share start — Redwood Console]
[02:15] Ethan: Here is the Optimize tab — note the suggested batching window and target concurrency. For short chats we usually recommend small batch windows like 20-30ms plus a concurrency target that matches your frontend pool size to preserve latency.
[02:30] Devon: Wait, 20-30ms seems tiny — how do we get batching to kick in at that window without adding latency?
[02:36] Ethan: It's a tradeoff. We use continuous batching in the runtime: requests sit very briefly for the window or until batch-size threshold. Because the tokens are short, the added tail is usually a few tens of milliseconds — often within your p95. The savings come from token kernel amortization: fewer small kernel launches.
[02:55] Jonas: Also worth noting we can do adaptive windows during peak vs off-peak so you get the best of both worlds.
[03:05] Maria: And KV cache — how does that affect latency? If we warm KV, do we see faster responses?
[03:12] Ethan: Yes, warm KV removes the need to re-evaluate earlier tokens; the runtime uses the KV cache to skip recomputation. That often reduces both latency and cost per request for long contexts. But it requires memory and careful eviction policies.
[03:28] Priya: Memory footprint was a concern for us. We run in a dedicated cluster and want predictable RAM usage.
[03:34] Ethan: Totally. For Dedicated we expose KV cache sizing options + an eviction policy. For VPC/private deploys we can set limits and also recommend quantization to reduce memory.
[03:44] Maria: Can you show the cost model with numbers?
[03:48] Ethan: Sure. Switching to the cost spreadsheet — this is tailored to LumenWare's profile you sent. Assumptions: model cost 0.0008 USD per token in baseline host, average request tokens 160 (120+40), 100k requests/day, concurrency distribution peaking at 400. Here are scenarios:
[04:05] Ethan: Scenario A: No cache, minimal batching: baseline monthly token cost X (on screen we show $12,800/mo).
[04:15] Ethan: Scenario B: 30% prefix hit, batching at 20ms: token cost drops to $9,600/mo (~25% reduction).
[04:26] Ethan: Scenario C: 60% prefix + KV warm for hottest flows, plus moderate batching and 4-bit quantization on low-priority routes: token cost down to $6,400/mo (~50% reduction) but with tradeoffs on tail quality.
[04:40] Maria: Can you explain the quantization tradeoff? Our QA is sensitive to hallucinations.
[04:46] Ethan: Yes. Quantization (like 4-bit) reduces model memory and inference cost, sometimes with slight degradation on rare token choices. We recommend doing quantization on lower-risk routes — e.g., internal suggestions and non-critical summarization. Prod customer-facing completions we often keep fp16 unless bench shows safe. Part of the pilot is an A/B quality check.
[05:05] Devon: That makes sense. For the 60% prefix + KV scenario, what's the required hit rate to justify the memory and operational complexity?
[05:12] Ethan: In our experience, above ~40-45% hit rate on repeat prefixes it usually pays back in tokens saved relative to the KV memory cost. The model and response length distribution influence the exact breakpoint.
[05:24] Priya: What about latency SLOs? We need p95 < 250ms — how do we ensure the caching/batching doesn't violate that?
[05:30] Ethan: Two levers: aggressive batching can hurt tail latency, so we set very small windows for tail-sensitive endpoints and larger for throughput-bound. KV warm reduces CPU time per request hence helps p95. We also recommend autoscaling headroom: keep a concurrency cushion so backlog doesn't accumulate during spikes.
[05:50] Rachel: I want to walk through a quick pilot proposal: 2-week run, subset of traffic (10% of sessions), instrumented metrics: p50/p95 latency, token spend, prefix-hit rate, output-quality regression on sampled prompts.
[06:05] Maria: Would Redwood run the pilot or we run it in our Dedicated?
[06:10] Rachel: We can run it either: in Hosted with dedicated capacity or in your Dedicated cluster. For the initial bench we usually run hosted so we can iterate quickly, then port the config to Dedicated once behavior is stable.
[06:22] Devon: I want to dig into the runtime knobs — can you list concrete config names?
[06:26] Ethan: Sure: batching_window_ms, batch_max_items, concurrency_target, prefix_cache_ttl_sec, kv_cache_size_mb, quantization_profile (e.g., q4_0), fallback_model_priority. All configurable via the Console or infra-as-code.
[06:40] [10-minute marker skipped in transcript generation]
[06:42] Maria: Any security concerns around caching? We don't want customer PII persisted unencrypted.
[06:47] Ethan: Good call. Prefix cache and KV cache are always encrypted at rest when you use Redwood Private/Dedicated with KMS enabled. We also provide TTLs and scrub rules so sensitive tokens are never persisted beyond policy. For Hosted we have SOC 2 controls and per-tenant encryption options.
[07:05] Priya: SAML and RBAC integration — can we hook into our IdP for console access?
[07:10] Jonas: Yes, SSO via SAML + SCIM for provisioning, we also provide RBAC roles to lock down model and rollout settings.
[07:18] Devon: Back to batching — earlier you mentioned adaptive windows. How are those computed?
[07:23] Ethan: We sample recent latency & queue metrics and compute an objective function: minimize tokens-in-flight subject to latency SLO. The runtime can switch to more aggressive batching in sustained steady-state and relax during spikes. We expose the policy curve in Console for transparency.
[07:40] Maria: I'd like to understand one concrete math example — show us how a 30% prefix hit translates to token count.
[07:46] Ethan: Sure. Example: baseline request sends 120 context tokens + 40 response = 160 tokens processed. With 30% prefix hit, 30% of requests omit 80 tokens of repeated context on average, so effective average tokens per request = 0.7*160 + 0.3*(160-80) = 112 + 24 = 136 tokens. That's ~15% token reduction; combined with batching advantage you get to the ~20-25% total number we showed.
[08:14] Maria: The math helps.
[08:17] Priya: We also have heavy multimodel routing — some flows go to instruction- tuned, others to a larger model for complex answers. How do you recommend routing policies interact with cache?
[08:28] Ethan: Two approaches: route by model first then apply cache per-model, or unify caching across compatible model variants by using a normalized request hash. For model fallback we prefer to keep cache keys tied to semantics so a fallback to a smaller model can still benefit if outputs are compatible. That adds complexity but reduces duplicate work.
[08:48] Devon: Any gotchas with batch size on GPU memory? We're on A100s and occasionally hit OOM for longer sequences.
[08:54] Ethan: Longer sequences increase per-batch memory; the runtime has per-batch memory budget and will cap batch size for sequences above threshold. For Dedicated you can set a hard limit to avoid OOM. Also consider sequence bucketing: group similar length requests to improve packing.
[09:10] Maria: Could you show the console trace view for a warm KV hit vs cold?
[09:15] Ethan: Yup — here's a capture. Cold run shows token counts and KV build time; warm shows the missing recomputation time removed and smaller syscall counts. You see p95 drop by ~40ms in this synthetic capture.
[09:33] [Noise, partial transcription error: \"we saw a 40msec in the syn\" -> likely 40ms in the synthet]
[09:40] Priya: If we do the pilot in hosted, will logs and traces contain PII? We need to ensure redaction.
[09:46] Jonas: We recommend sending anonymized traces. Redwood supports field-level scrubbing and sample-level redaction policies. We'll include the configuration in the docs we share.
[09:58] Maria: Anything else on cost math? I want to understand the sensitivity to prefix hit %. If our templating increases from 30% to 45% via engineering changes, how much more do we save?
[10:09] Ethan: Roughly linear-ish up to a point. If you go from 30% to 45% prefix hits, you might drop token count another 7-8% in the same example, but combined with batching it compounds. That's why we recommend investing in prompt engineering for high-frequency flows.
[10:26] Devon: About evidence — do you have benchmark artifacts or previous customer examples we can see?
[10:31] Rachel: Yes — we'll share anonymized benchmarks from another conversational SaaS customer and a template cost model. We'll annotate where their traffic matched yours.
[10:40] Priya: Can we lock an SLO into the Dedicated contract if we see regression risk?
[10:44] Rachel: Yes, Dedicated comes with SLA-backed targets; we'll define p95 and uptime in the contract.
[10:49] Maria: Any competitor tradeoffs? We chatted with Replicate and Anthropic; Anthropic pushed fine-tuning in their stack which changes how caching works.
[10:59] Ethan: Right. Anthropic's approach tends to emphasize model-side efficiency; our differentiation is the runtime-level caching and transparent cost tooling that you control.
[11:08] [20-minute mark — mid-call summary by Rachel]
[11:10] Rachel: Quick recap: 1) prefix caching at your observed 30% gives immediate token reductions; 2) KV warm for heavy flows yields latency and token wins but needs memory planning; 3) batching windows tuned by endpoint preserve p95 while saving kernel overhead; 4) pilot proposal to validate with real traffic.
[11:28] Maria: Sounds good. A couple of nitty-gritty questions: what's the retention TTL on prefix cache? We may want only 24 hours for PII reasons.
[11:36] Ethan: We can set TTLs as low as a few seconds up to days. For your case 24h TTL and scrubbing rules are straightforward.
[11:44] Devon: For the pilot, what's the instrumentation we need to add on our side?
[11:48] Ethan: At minimum: request id propagation, timing headers, and a sample flag to capture raw prompt/response pairs for quality checks. We'll share the exact schema.
[11:58] Priya: Anything on cost predictability? We want to avoid surprise spikes.
[12:02] Rachel: Suggest commit tiers on Dedicated for stable capacity or use budget alerts in Console for Hosted. Additionally, Optimize gives per-route token forecasts based on moving windows.
[12:15] Maria: Let's talk about rollout: after pilot, what are typical next steps?
[12:19] Ethan: Usually: extend pilot to 50% traffic, iterate on quantization, configure Dedicated if desired, and then formalize commit levels.
[12:28] [Longer technical dive — skipped to later exchange]
[30:12] Devon: One more deep question — how do you handle cache invalidation when the system's prompt template changes?
[30:18] Ethan: We version cache keys with a prompt template hash. When the template changes we bump the hash and cold starts for that flow occur until cache warms. We also provide a manual invalidate API for ops.
[30:30] Maria: Great. Can you include that in the runbook?
[30:33] Ethan: Will do.
[30:35] Rachel: Ok I want to call out the pilot acceptance criteria: token reduction >=15% and no p95 regression > 10% and user-visible quality delta A/B within tolerance. If we hit those, we move to a larger rollout.
[30:50] Maria: That's acceptable.
[30:53] Priya: Last security doc ask: can you provide SC-2 or SOC 2 type attestation?
[30:58] Jonas: Yeah we'll share redacted SOC 2 mapping and the private deployment compliance pack.
[31:05] Maria: Awesome. Any blockers from our side?
[31:08] Devon: We'll need to export the 7-day production trace and coordinate a maintenance window for 10% traffic routing.
[31:15] Rachel: Great. We'll put proposed dates in writing.
[31:20] [Noise and brief interruption — \"hang on I have a calendar\"
31:25] Maria: One more thought on cost — can you show a sensitivity table with token price per 1k change? Our finance team wants stretch cases.
[31:32] Ethan: Yes, the spreadsheet includes sensitivity columns for model token price +/- 25% and prefix hit +/- 15%. We'll send that.
[31:40] Devon: Do you support per-route quantization policies? Eg apply q4 to non-critical route but keep fp16 for billing-critical routes.
[31:47] Ethan: Yes, per-route model profiles and quantization overrides exist.
[31:51] Rachel: Ok let's recap next steps and owners out loud. Rachel to send cost model and Console screenshots; Ethan to draft bench/ pilot runbook; Devon to share traces; Jonas to include SOC2/ security pack.
[32:05] Maria: Perfect. Anything else on the Optimize Console features?
[32:09] Ethan: One small note — we recently added a \"simulate savings\" toggle that runs your trace locally against profile configs to estimate hit rates without changing prod traffic. That might help you tune before pilot.
[32:23] Priya: That simulation would be very helpful.
[32:26] Rachel: We'll include steps on how to run that.
[32:30] [Silence, brief side chatter]
[32:40] Maria: Ok thanks everyone. We'll aim to review the cost model and pick a pilot start date next week.
[32:46] Rachel: Great. We'll follow up with the spreadsheet and a calendar invite for the pilot kickoff.
[32:52] Ethan: Quick note — for the pilot we recommend enabling detailed telemetry for the first 48 hours then sampling thereafter to control storage costs.
[33:02] Devon: Understood.
[33:05] [Closing housekeeping — 2-3 minute small talk and calendar coordination omitted]
[35:00] Maria: Thanks all.
[35:03] Rachel: Thanks — we'll send the deck and cost model.
[35:06] [Recording stops — end of captured transcript. Some timestamps omitted by auto-transcriber.]

Customer shared traffic profile and latency SLOs; Redwood proposed a pilot with prefix caching, conservative quantization, and autoscaling parameters. Agreed next steps include cost spreadsheet, pilot runbook, and trace dump.

Topics covered (auto-tags):
- Prefix vs KV cache behavior
- Batching and concurrency trade-offs
- Cost model assumptions (tokens, hit rates, quantization)
- Pilot design and acceptance criteria
- Security questions: VPC, KMS, audit logs

Transcript:

[00:12] Maria: Thanks, Rachel. We only have about 50 minutes so we'd like to focus on the Optimize recommendations — batching and cache — and how that maps to cost for our chat product.

[00:35] Devon: Awesome. For context, our workload is largely short chat turns, average 120 tokens request, 40 tokens response, high concurrency during peak. Latency target is 250ms p95.
[00:50] Ethan: Thanks. That's helpful. Two immediate levers we look at: batching and caching. Batching is how we group concurrent token generation to amortize per-token kernel cost; caching (prefix/KV) reduces tokens required when prompts share a common prefix or repeated history.
[01:10] Priya: Can you remind us, prefix cache vs KV cache, what's the practical difference? I always get them mixed.
[01:18] Ethan: Totally. Prefix cache is basically request-level caching for identical leading token sequences — think the conversation context you send repeatedly. KV cache is the KV memory for autoregressive generation that we persist across sessions for warm starts — so they behave differently in hit patterns and storage. Sometimes customers use \"prefix\" to mean the same as a request cache, and we see both.
[01:35] Maria: So in our earlier tests we had around 30% repeat prefixes for templated prompts. We also have reuse across user sessions for some support flows.
[01:45] Ethan: That 30% is actually pretty meaningful. In our cost model, even a 30% prefix-hit rate can reduce token spend by ~20-25% depending on reply lengths because you avoid re-sending the history tokens to the model.
[02:00] Rachel: We'll show the Console where Optimize surfaces this and then a spreadsheet that converts hit rates into dollars.
[02:08] [Screen share start — Redwood Console]
[02:15] Ethan: Here is the Optimize tab — note the suggested batching window and target concurrency. For short chats we usually recommend small batch windows like 20-30ms plus a concurrency target that matches your frontend pool size to preserve latency.

[02:36] Ethan: It's a tradeoff. We use continuous batching in the runtime: requests sit very briefly for the window or until batch-size threshold. Because the tokens are short, the added tail is usually a few tens of milliseconds — often within your p95. The savings come from token kernel amortization: fewer small kernel launches.
[02:55] Jonas: Also worth noting we can do adaptive windows during peak vs off-peak so you get the best of both worlds.

[03:12] Ethan: Yes, warm KV removes the need to re-evaluate earlier tokens; the runtime uses the KV cache to skip recomputation. That often reduces both latency and cost per request for long contexts. But it requires memory and careful eviction policies.

[04:46] Ethan: Yes. Quantization (like 4-bit) reduces model memory and inference cost, sometimes with slight degradation on rare token choices. We recommend doing quantization on lower-risk routes — e.g., internal suggestions and non-critical summarization. Prod customer-facing completions we often keep fp16 unless bench shows safe. Part of the pilot is an A/B quality check.

[05:12] Ethan: In our experience, above ~40-45% hit rate on repeat prefixes it usually pays back in tokens saved relative to the KV memory cost. The model and response length distribution influence the exact breakpoint.

[05:30] Ethan: Two levers: aggressive batching can hurt tail latency, so we set very small windows for tail-sensitive endpoints and larger for throughput-bound. [06:26] Ethan: Sure: batching_window_ms, batch_max_items, concurrency_target, prefix_cache_ttl_sec, kv_cache_size_mb, quantization_profile (e.g., q4_0), fallback_model_priority. All configurable via the Console or infra-as-code.

title: POC scoping: workload profile & QPS modeling - LuminaHealth
preview:
Meeting header
Date/Time: 2025-06-03 15:00 UTC
Duration: 55 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Raj Patel (LuminaHead ML), Aisha Mohammed (Cloud Architect), Tom Brooks (CTO), Nicole Li (Data Engineer)

Auto-summary (auto):
- Purpose: scope POC for LuminaHealth conversational assistant workload, focus on traffic modeling (QPS, concurrency), token distributions, and success criteria for benchmarks.
- High-level ask: validate performance and cost across Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private.

Topics: workload characterization, baseline QPS, peak concurrency, input/output token profiles, KV/prefix caching, warm-up, test harness, timeline and next steps.

Action items (recap at top): see action_items field.

Transcript:
[00:00] Maya Chen: hi everyone thanks for joining, um we'll give a quick agenda, then dig into the traffic profile you sent — Raj, thanks for that — and then talk about how we'd run the benchmarks and what success looks like.
[00:28] Raj Patel: yeah thanks, um quick context, our assistant is embedded in our patient intake flow and clinical notes summarization. We expect bursts during morning check-ins and then steady background traffic. Currently proxied through internal api gateway, roughly 400 rps avg, peak at 1.8k rps during windows.
[00:52] Ethan Park: ok helpful, and what's your typical concurrency — meaning how many simultaneous sessions holding KV cache per model?
[01:05] Raj Patel: we see about 120 concurrent active sessions at peak; though session-length varies, some long-running summarization jobs hold a larger context (2–3k input tokens).
[01:22] Aisha Mohammed: to add, most requests are short Q/A where input is ~60 tokens, output ~30 tokens, but the note-summarization pipeline can be 1500 input tokens and 400 output tokens. So token distribution is heavy-tailed.
[01:44] Ethan Park: great — that informs model selection and batching. For POC we typically model three classes: short queries (60/30), mid (350/150), long (1500/400). Can you confirm approximate % breakdown?
[02:05] Nicole Li: sure from logs last week it's roughly 73% short, 22% mid, 5% long.
[02:14] Maya Chen: perfect. For QPS modelling we translate that into effective token-per-second load and also simulate concurrency to measure 95th pct latency. What are your SLOs?
[02:30] Tom Brooks: we want p95 under 700ms for short queries and under 2s for mid; long jobs can be up to 10s but we'd like to keep cost predictable.
[02:45] Ethan Park: noted. For benchmarking we'll target both latency and cost per 1k output tokens. We'll run both steady-state (sustained 400 rps) and spike tests up to 2k rps in a traffic replay.
[03:01] Raj Patel: how do you handle KV cache/warm starts? Our sessions often reuse previous context.
[03:09] Ethan Park: good question — Redwood runtime supports prefix/KV cache — in Dedicated or Private we can pin long-lived KV state per session. In Hosted we emulate with a cache layer; for accurate POC we recommend replaying traces with realistic session affinity so cache hit rates match production.
[03:29] Aisha Mohammed: we have session IDs in logs, can redact then share sample traces — Nicole will take that.
[03:35] Nicole Li: yes I'll export and scrub PHI.
[03:40] Maya Chen: excellent. For the POC plan I'm thinking: week 1 collect traces + agree success criteria; week 2 run hosted benchmarks; week 3 run Dedicated sizing and on-prem trial if needed; week 4 analyze and present. Is that timeline workable?
[03:57] Tom Brooks: that's aggressive but doable, want to target a decision before end of Q2.
[04:04] Raj Patel: Q: how do you simulate user think-time and burstiness? we don't want synthetic smooth load.
[04:12] Ethan Park: our harness supports replay with original inter-arrival times and session stickiness, and we can inject burst windows to match your morning spikes. Also we can vary payload sizes per the distribution.
[04:27] Raj Patel: ok good. Also curious about autoscaling — if we go Dedicated what's the scaling latency?
[04:35] Ethan Park: Dedicated autoscale depends on policy; typical scale-up for additional GPUs takes a couple of minutes to provision and route; we use burst pools to handle immediate spikes while new capacity spins up. For strict p95 SLOs we recommend a hybrid policy: keep baseline capacity for 99th percentile and burst for extreme events.
[04:55] Aisha Mohammed: and network egress — we have strict whitelisting, do you need outbound egress to third-party services?
[05:03] Maya Chen: for Hosted there is outbound egress for model download or telemetry. For Private, everything stays in your VPC, and we can support strict egress rules. We'll share the network matrix.
[05:15] Raj Patel: another practical — tokenization differences across models cause token-count variance. Our logs used GPT tokenization. If you run bench on a Llama variant counts will differ.
[05:30] Ethan Park: agreed — we'll normalize by using the same tokenizer or report both model-token counts and GPT-equivalent tokens.
[05:40] Tom Brooks: what's the expected GPU sizing for a steady 400 rps with 73% short, 22% mid, 5% long on a 8k context model?
[05:52] Ethan Park: rough back-of-envelope: for an 8k context 7B quantized model on A100-ish equivalence, a single GPU might handle ~200–300 short-rps depending on batching. For mixed workloads at 400 rps steady you likely need a small pool 2–4 GPUs with batching and caching. We'll put exact numbers in the Dedicated sketch.
[06:12] Maya Chen: we'll include two scenarios: cost-first (more batching, higher latency tolerance) and latency-first (more reserved capacity, lower batch sizes).
[06:22] Raj Patel: benchmark success criteria — besides p95, we want functional parity on hallucination rate for critical prompts under latency constraints. Can you run quality checks?
[06:34] Ethan Park: yes we can run evals using your prompt set; we'll measure answer correctness and hallucination regression against baseline. Please provide canonical test prompts and acceptability thresholds.
[06:45] Nicole Li: we have a 300-prompt test suite for clinical summaries and acceptance is >=92% factuality.
[06:52] Maya Chen: great — include that as a must-pass quality gate for POC.
[06:58] Raj Patel: on the topic of shared artifacts, please send the benchmark template, and we'll fill in sample volumes and session ids.
[07:05] Maya Chen: will do — sending via email and also link in CRM.
[07:10] Tom Brooks: any known gotchas with long sessions and KV memory growth?
[07:16] Ethan Park: yes long-lived sessions with unbounded context can increase memory pressure; we recommend session TTLs or summarization checkpoints. In Dedicated we can configure KV eviction policies. We'll show memory profiles in the POC run.
[07:33] Aisha Mohammed: security — we need encryption at rest and KMS integration for Private. Is that supported?
[07:40] Maya Chen: yes, Private supports customer-managed KMS and HSM integrations; we'll share the compliance pack including SOC2 controls and audit log formats.
[07:52] Raj Patel: timeline again: if we can get logs to you by Friday, what's earliest we can start hosted replay?
[07:59] Ethan Park: with logs Friday, we can spin up hosted-run the following Tuesday-Wednesday; Dedicated sketch will come after we review initial results.
[08:10] Nicole Li: ok I'll prioritize scrubbing and sending traces.
[08:15] Maya Chen: back to cost modeling — we typically present cost per 1M tokens and per-1k inference requests under the tested load. Would you prefer cost modeled as monthly steady-state or per-spike?
[08:27] Tom Brooks: both, please. Execs want steady-state estimate and worst-case spike delta.
[08:33] Ethan Park: noted. We'll include both and show impact of batching/quantization on unit cost.
[08:40] Raj Patel: curious about fallbacks — if a Dedicated pool is saturated can you route to a cheaper hosted fallback model?
[08:48] Maya Chen: yes that's part of smart routing — policy-based fallback to compatible model variants or other regions; we'll include an example policy and cost/latency tradeoff.
[08:58] Raj Patel: ok great. On success criteria let's summarize: p95 latency targets (700ms short, 2s mid), quality gate >=92% factuality, cost under X (TBD), and ability to handle 2k rps spikes with acceptable degradation plan.
[09:17] Maya Chen: yea let's write that down — we'll propose concrete cost numbers after we run the hosted benchmark.
[09:25] Tom Brooks: about data retention: logs must be retained for 90 days and we need audit logs for all model calls. Is that possible?
[09:33] Ethan Park: yes, Dedicated and Private include audit logging and configurable retention. Hosted can provide similar logs but retention tiers are an add-on.
[09:44] Nicole Li: regarding data privacy, can we mask PHI deterministically before sending?
[09:49] Aisha Mohammed: we already redact at the gateway but still want to minimize sensitive tokens in traces.
[09:55] Maya Chen: we recommend deterministic redaction and sample traces that keep type distributions.
[10:00] Raj Patel: what about evaluation harness — do you support warm-up runs, cold cache runs, and measurement of cache hit improvement?
[10:08] Ethan Park: yes, our harness can run cold, warm-up, and steady-state; we'll produce cache hit-rate curves and latency breakdowns by token range.
[10:18] Tom Brooks: any sign-off path after POC? we expect a one-pager for execs and TCO comparison.
[10:24] Maya Chen: we'll deliver a POC report with recommendation: Hosted pilot vs Dedicated commit sizing and TCO projection.
[10:30] Raj Patel: sounds good. Quick question about model variants: if we change quantization, do you expect big latency improvements?
[10:38] Ethan Park: yes quantization plus kernel selection can give 20–40% token throughput improvement for some models; we'll test 8-bit and 4-bit variants and look at quality regression.
[10:49] Maya Chen: ok let's capture next steps and owners — Nicole will send scrubbed traces, Maya and Ethan will run hosted replay and return results, and we'll reconvene after initial run.
[11:01] Nicole Li: noted.
[11:05] Aisha Mohammed: one more item — we may require on-prem air-gapped test, is that something Redwood supports in POC?
[11:12] Maya Chen: yes we have an on-prem package for Private POCs; it requires some lead time (~2 weeks) for infra prep. We'll discuss if needed after the hosted run.
[11:25] Raj Patel: ok that works. Thanks everyone.
[11:28] Maya Chen: thanks, we'll follow up with the benchmark template and a proposed calendar for the next three weeks.
[11:34] Ethan Park: thanks all.

End of transcript.

Recap (auto): Collect traces, run hosted and Dedicated benchmarks, measure p95 latency and quality gate, provide Dedicated sizing and cost models.

References/Links shared: benchmark_template.xlsx (will be attached), SOC2-summary.pdf, POC-dedicated-sizing-sketch.docx

[02:05] Nicole Li: sure from logs last week it's roughly 73% short, 22% mid, 5% long.
[02:14] Maya Chen: perfect. For QPS modelling we translate that into effective token-per-second load and also simulate concurrency to measure 95th pct latency. What are your SLOs?
[02:30] Tom Brooks: we want p95 under 700ms for short queries and under 2s for mid; long jobs can be up to 10s but we'd like to keep cost predictable.

[03:01] Raj Patel: how do you handle KV cache/warm starts? Our sessions often reuse previous context.
[03:09] Ethan Park: good question — Redwood runtime supports prefix/KV cache — in Dedicated or Private we can pin long-lived KV state per session. In Hosted we emulate with a cache layer; for accurate POC we recommend replaying traces with realistic session affinity so cache hit rates match production.
[03:29] Aisha Mohammed: we have session IDs in logs, can redact then share sample traces — Nicole will take that.
[03:35] Nicole Li: yes I'll export and scrub PHI.
[03:40] Maya Chen: excellent. For the POC plan I'm thinking: week 1 collect traces + agree success criteria; week 2 run hosted benchmarks; week 3 run Dedicated sizing and on-prem trial if needed; week 4 analyze and present. Is that timeline workable?
[03:57] Tom Brooks: that's aggressive but doable, want to target a decision before end of Q2.
[04:04] Raj Patel: Q: how do you simulate user think-time and burstiness? we don't want synthetic smooth load.
[04:12] Ethan Park: our harness supports replay with original inter-arrival times and session stickiness, and we can inject burst windows to match your morning spikes. Also we can vary payload sizes per the distribution.
[04:27] Raj Patel: ok good. Also curious about autoscaling — if we go Dedicated what's the scaling latency?
[04:35] Ethan Park: Dedicated autoscale depends on policy; typical scale-up for additional GPUs takes a couple of minutes to provision and route; we use burst pools to handle immediate spikes while new capacity spins up. For strict p95 SLOs we recommend a hybrid policy: keep baseline capacity for 99th percentile and burst for extreme events.
[04:55] Aisha Mohammed: and network egress — we have strict whitelisting, do you need outbound egress to third-party services?
[05:03] Maya Chen: for Hosted there is outbound egress for model download or telemetry. For Private, everything stays in your VPC, and we can support strict egress rules. We'll share the network matrix.
[05:15] Raj Patel: another practical — tokenization differences across models cause token-count variance. Our logs used GPT tokenization. If you run bench on a Llama variant counts will differ.
[05:30] Ethan Park: agreed — we'll normalize by using the same tokenizer or report both model-token counts and GPT-equivalent tokens.

[08:10] Nicole Li: ok I'll prioritize scrubbing and sending traces.
[08:15] Maya Chen: back to cost modeling — we typically present cost per 1M tokens and per-1k inference requests under the tested load. Would you prefer cost modeled as monthly steady-state or per-spike?

title: Axion Retail - Embeddings & Reranker Hosted API Showcase
preview:
Meeting: Axion Retail - Embeddings & Reranker Hosted API Showcase
Date/Time: 2025-05-22 14:00 PDT
Duration: 48 minutes
Location: Zoom / recorded
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Sam Torres (Axion Head of Search), Priya Nair (Axion Eng Lead), Diego Ruiz (Axion DS)
Links shared: Deck - https://redwood.ai/decks/axion-embedding-walkthrough, Benchmarks - https://redwood.ai/benchmarks/axion, Pilot template - https://redwood.ai/pilot-templates

Summary: Quick hosted API walkthrough focused on embedding generation and a two-step retrieval + rerank pipeline. Demo covered API calls (embeddings endpoint + reranker endpoint), Redwood Console insights, and Optimize recommendations for batching/caching. Customer is evaluating hosted vs dedicated for a retail product search pilot; main concerns: latency, cost per query, and PII handling / SOC2 / SSO.

Topics: hosted embeddings model choices, dimensionality and similarity metric, reranker endpoint scoring function, prefix/KV cache, batching, fallback model variants, security (VPC vs hosted), audit logs, pilot next steps.

-- Transcript --
[00:00] Maya (Redwood): Hey everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Maya, account lead for Axion. With me is Ethan, our solutions engineer. We'll run through a hosted API flow for embeddings + reranking and show how you'd set up a quick pilot.
[00:12] Sam (Axion): Hey Maya, thanks. Curious mostly about out of the box quality — our catalog search has a lot of short product descriptions and we care about recall for long-tail items. Also, we need to make sure PII doesn't leak into logs.
[00:24] Maya: Totally, we'll get to retention and audit logs. Ethan, quick intro?
[00:27] Ethan (Redwood): Yep hi, I'm Ethan. I'll demo the calls and the Console. Quick note: we typically recommend starting with one of our medium-sized embed models for retail — 768 or 1024 dims depending on your vector DB and latency goals. For hosted we have redwood-embed-medium (approx 768) and redwood-embed-large (1024).
[00:45] Priya (Axion): Are those names the same in the Console? We saw something called embed-v1 in the docs.
[00:49] Ethan: Docs sometimes show generic names, the Console maps them; we'll send the exact model alias you can pin in the API.
[00:55] Diego (Axion): What about similarity metric — cosine vs dot? Our current infra uses dot-product in our ANN layer.
[01:00] Ethan: Both supported. If your ANN expects dot, you can use raw vectors from our embeddings endpoint and leave normalization to your index. If you want cosine, we can return normalized vectors server-side — small extra cost but saves you a step.
[01:15] Sam: Ok. Show the pipeline please.
[01:18] Ethan: Sure. We'll do a two-step flow: 1) candidate retrieval from your vector DB using shared embed vectors, 2) pass top K to Redwood reranker endpoint which scores candidates against the query — returns re-ranked IDs and rationale if you enable structured output.
[01:32] (screen share starts)
[01:36] Ethan: Here's a sample cURL: curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/embeddings -d '{"model":"redwood-embed-medium","input":"red short cotton t-shirt"}' ... and then we take the vector and hit your index. Then sample rerank call: POST /v1/rerank with query + candidate_docs. You can pass parameters like top_k, temperature, explain: true.
[01:59] Priya: That explain flag — is it deterministic? We need consistent scoring for audits.
[02:04] Ethan: The reranker uses a deterministic scoring path when explain is enabled; we also return a score delta breakdown in the JSON. Note: sometimes the transcriber shows 'explane' which is just background noise.
[02:15] Sam: How about latency numbers for this two-step?
[02:18] Ethan: For hosted median numbers we see embedding generation ~15-30ms for medium model, reranker depends on sequence length and top_k; typical retail rerank for top 20 is 60-120ms. So end-to-end top_k 20 rerank is commonly in the 80-160ms ballpark. If latency is super critical you can run the rerank on a smaller model or use caching + prefix caching to avoid full recompute.
[02:38] Diego: Does Redwood do KV cache for the reranker or that's only for autoregressive?
[02:42] Ethan: We have prefix and KV caching primarily for LM autoregressive flows, for reranking we cache embeddings and candidate encodings if you enable the cache key on your route. That can reduce costs for repeated queries.
[02:53] Sam: Security — we need SOC 2, SSO, KMS for keys, and a way to keep logs in our VPC.
[03:01] Maya: We're SOC 2 Type II certified; I'll attach the certificate. SSO via SAML/Okta is supported and you can toggle audit log export to your SIEM. For Private deployments we offer a VPC option and HSM/KMS integration. For hosted we offer customer-managed encryption keys for data-at-rest and redaction controls.
[03:20] Priya: Any retention defaults? We can't keep PII beyond 30 days.
[03:24] Maya: Hosted default is 90 days but you can request 30-day retention for pilot; also we have an option to disable retention for specific routes. I'll include retention settings in the pilot template.
[03:35] Diego: Cost — how does per-query math look? Especially with top_k 50 + rerank.
[03:40] Ethan: Embeddings are priced per vector call and rerank per token-equivalent. For retail we often recommend a hybrid: do coarse retrieval with small top_k (e.g., 50) and then rerank top 10 with a small reranker model to keep cost down. We can model your QPS and show cost tradeoffs — will include in the pilot pricing spreadsheet.
[04:00] Sam: Can we do an initial pilot with hosted? We don't want the overhead of dedicated infra yet.
[04:04] Maya: Yes — hosted pilot is common. We can provision a trial capacity with monitoring and we can set a fallback policy to a cheaper model if you see bursts.
[04:12] Priya: What about the Console — can product managers see latency/cost by route?
[04:16] Ethan: Yes, Console shows per-route token and vector costs, latencies histogram, and per-token breakdowns. Here's a screenshot — (console shown). Note: the console exports sometimes show up as 'Redwood Consoel' in auto transcripts.
[04:30] Sam: Are there benchmarks we can run?
[04:33] Ethan: We'll share a benchmark script that runs queries against your sample docs and measures recall@k and latency. It's in the shared drive — we'll upload as a notebook.
[04:42] (brief pause, screen navigation)
[04:45] Ethan: Now a quick live demo: I create an embedding for a sample product phrase... the API returns a 768-d vector. We push to a sample index and run retrieval, then rerank. You can see the reranker shifts a few long-tail SKUs up because it picks up attributes in the description.
[05:05] Priya: The quality looks promising, especially for those long-tail variants. How do we tune reranker prompts?
[05:09] Ethan: You can provide candidate-level context and an instruction template. Optimize (our recommendations module) can suggest prompt templates and quantization settings; for example, it may suggest 8-bit quantization for reranker models if your latency target is under 100ms.
[05:24] Sam: Any gotchas on tokenization or vendor tokens? We use some nonstandard chars in SKUs.
[05:29] Ethan: We recommend normalizing SKUs client-side; we also have tokenization configs you can set on the rerank route.
[05:35] Diego: Will Redwood's hosted endpoints log raw text? We need to be mindful of PII.
[05:39] Maya: We can enable no-retention on those routes and integrate with your KMS. For extra compliance you can use Private deployment with a customer-managed control plane.
[05:48] Sam: Competitors — we've evaluated Pinecone and Weaviate. How does Redwood differentiate?
[05:52] Maya: Primary differentiation is the unified serving runtime plus reranker endpoint that avoids stitching multiple systems — we provide managed embeddings + reranker + console Observability out of the box. Also smart routing and fallbacks to keep SLAs.
[06:04] Priya: Ok. Pilot timeline?
[06:06] Maya: We suggest a 2-week pilot: week 1 collection + embedding generation (we'll take 1000 docs), week 2 run benchmarks and tune reranker, then review. Plan to start first week of June.
[06:17] Sam: Works for us.
[06:19] (wrap up)
[06:20] Maya: Quick recap — we'll send: hosted pricing + pilot template, security pack (SOC 2, SAML configs, retention controls), and Ethan will drop benchmark script + notebook. Sam will provide the sample doc set. Next meeting: pilot kickoff 2025-06-02.
[06:34] Sam: Thanks everyone.
[06:36] (call ends)

-- Auto-generated Action Items (from transcript) --
1) Maya Chen: Attach SOC 2 cert, retention options, and hosted pricing to the CRM record. (due 2025-05-23)
2) Ethan Park: Upload benchmark notebook and sample scripts to shared folder. (due 2025-05-25)
3) Sam Torres: Provide 1,000 doc sample and query set for benchmarking. (due 2025-05-27)

Recap / Next Steps:
- Maya to send hosted pricing and pilot template (includes retention toggles and KMS options).
- Ethan to share benchmark scripts + sample notebook.
- Customer to provide data sample.
- Schedule pilot kickoff for 2025-06-02 (Maya to send invite).

Notes on transcription: occasional mis-parse of product names (e.g., 'redwood-embed' sometimes appears as 'red wood embed') and tech terms (KV cache -> KV cash) due to background noise.
