For Private deployments we offer a VPC option and HSM/KMS integration. For hosted we offer customer-managed encryption keys for data-at-rest and redaction controls.
[03:20] Priya: Any retention defaults? We can't keep PII beyond 30 days.
[03:24] Maya: Hosted default is 90 days but you can request 30-day retention for pilot; also we have an option to disable retention for specific routes. I'll include retention settings in the pilot template.
[03:35] Diego: Cost — how does per-query math look? Especially with top_k 50 + rerank.
[03:40] Ethan: Embeddings are priced per vector call and rerank per token-equivalent. For retail we often recommend a hybrid: do coarse retrieval with small top_k (e.g., 50) and then rerank top 10 with a small reranker model to keep cost down. We can model your QPS and show cost tradeoffs — will include in the pilot pricing spreadsheet.
[04:00] Sam: Can we do an initial pilot with hosted? We don't want the overhead of dedicated infra yet.
[04:04] Maya: Yes — hosted pilot is common. We can provision a trial capacity with monitoring and we can set a fallback policy to a cheaper model if you see bursts.
[04:12] Priya: What about the Console — can product managers see latency/cost by route?
[04:16] Ethan: Yes, Console shows per-route token and vector costs, latencies histogram, and per-token breakdowns. Here's a screenshot — (console shown). Note: the console exports sometimes show up as 'Redwood Consoel' in auto transcripts.
[04:30] Sam: Are there benchmarks we can run?
[04:33] Ethan: We'll share a benchmark script that runs queries against your sample docs and measures recall@k and latency. It's in the shared drive — we'll upload as a notebook.
[04:42] (brief pause, screen navigation)
[04:45] Ethan: Now a quick live demo: I create an embedding for a sample product phrase... the API returns a 768-d vector. We push to a sample index and run retrieval, then rerank. You can see the reranker shifts a few long-tail SKUs up because it picks up attributes in the description.
[05:05] Priya: The quality looks promising, especially for those long-tail variants. How do we tune reranker prompts?
[05:09] Ethan: You can provide candidate-level context and an instruction template. Optimize (our recommendations module) can suggest prompt templates and quantization settings; for example, it may suggest 8-bit quantization for reranker models if your latency target is under 100ms.
[05:24] Sam: Any gotchas on tokenization or vendor tokens? We use some nonstandard chars in SKUs.
[05:29] Ethan: We recommend normalizing SKUs client-side; we also have tokenization configs you can set on the rerank route.
[05:35] Diego: Will Redwood's hosted endpoints log raw text? We need to be mindful of PII.
[05:39] Maya: We can enable no-retention on those routes and integrate with your KMS. For extra compliance you can use Private deployment with a customer-managed control plane.
[05:48] Sam: Competitors — we've evaluated Pinecone and Weaviate. How does Redwood differentiate?
[05:52] Maya: Primary differentiation is the unified serving runtime plus reranker endpoint that avoids stitching multiple systems — we provide managed embeddings + reranker + console Observability out of the box. Also smart routing and fallbacks to keep SLAs.
[06:04] Priya: Ok. Pilot timeline?
[06:06] Maya: We suggest a 2-week pilot: week 1 collection + embedding generation (we'll take 1000 docs), week 2 run benchmarks and tune reranker, then review. Plan to start first week of June.
[06:17] Sam: Works for us.
[06:19] (wrap up)
[06:20] Maya: Quick recap — we'll send: hosted pricing + pilot template, security pack (SOC 2, SAML configs, retention controls), and Ethan will drop benchmark script + notebook. Sam will provide the sample doc set. Next meeting: pilot kickoff 2025-06-02.
[06:34] Sam: Thanks everyone.
[06:36] (call ends)

-- Auto-generated Action Items (from transcript) --
1) Maya Chen: Attach SOC 2 cert, retention options, and hosted pricing to the CRM record. (due 2025-05-23)
2) Ethan Park: Upload benchmark notebook and sample scripts to shared folder. (due 2025-05-25)
3) Sam Torres: Provide 1,000 doc sample and query set for benchmarking. (due 2025-05-27)

Recap / Next Steps:
- Maya to send hosted pricing and pilot template (includes retention toggles and KMS options).
- Ethan to share benchmark scripts + sample notebook.
- Customer to provide data sample.
- Schedule pilot kickoff for 2025-06-02 (Maya to send invite).

Notes on transcription: occasional mis-parse of product names (e.g., 'redwood-embed' sometimes appears as 'red wood embed') and tech terms (KV cache -> KV cash) due to background noise.

title: VelociData SLO & streaming throughput discovery
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-05-21
Start: 16:00 PT
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Aisha Rahman (VelociData CTO), Roberto Silva (VelociData SRE), Claire Ng (VelociData ML)

Summary:
Quick discovery focused on production SLOs for streaming token generation and throughput benchmarking. Customer is evaluating Hosted for quick path, Dedicated for predictable p95 under load, and Private/VPC if compliance/network constraints require. Key goals: define p95 SLO, concurrency targets, streaming behavior (per-token latency vs flush size), and an actionable benchmark plan.

Topics covered:
- Target p95 latency numbers (short completions / streaming tokens)
- Concurrency and aggregate tokens/sec targets for peak windows
- Differences between Hosted, Dedicated, Private for predictability and isolation
- Bench plan: synthetic and representative prompt runs, KV/prefix caching, batching impacts
- Security/compliance flags (data residency, KMS, SOC2)

Quick notes (auto-gen-ish):
- transcription had occasional misnames (Redwood Dedicated sometimes becomes "Redwood dedicate"), technical terms sometimes split (K V cache, K-V cash).

Transcript body (timestamps are approximate, some overlaps and filler):
[00:00] Maya Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick intros — we from Redwood are Maya, AE, and Ethan, our SE. VelociData — Aisha, Roberto, Claire, thanks for making time.
[00:12] Aisha Rahman: Yep, thanks. We'll keep this tight. High level: we're trying to decide whether to start on Hosted then move to Dedicated, or go straight to Dedicated/Private. Primary concern is meeting p95 SLOs during spikes and predictable cost.
[00:28] Ethan Park: Great, that's what we'd expect. Can you share target p95 and concurrency numbers so we can recommend configs? Even ballparks help.
[00:36] Claire Ng: Right now we want p95 of about 250-350ms for short streaming responses (we stream tokens to users in UI), and for larger completions up to 1k tokens we can tolerate higher, up to 1.2s p95. Concurrency: peak of ~180 simultaneous streams, sustained 80 during business hours. Aggregate tokens/sec target around 6k tokens/sec during peak bursts.
[01:05] Roberto Silva: Also network: many clients are enterprise customers behind corporate proxies; we may need VPC or private peering for Dedicated. And audit logs must be shipped to our Splunk instance.
[01:18] Maya Chen: Understood. A couple of clarifying Qs: when you say streaming, are you flushing per token or in small batches? Because streaming flush size can materially change per-token p95.
[01:30] Claire Ng: We're streaming every 3-4 tokens ideally but we can move to per-token if needed. Latency sensitive flows are chat-like — need tokens to arrive with sub-300ms p95.
[01:42] Ethan Park: Good detail. For that pattern, two levers matter: single-request per-user concurrency and batching across users. On Hosted, we have dynamic batching but less predictability under noisy neighbor; Dedicated gives you reserved GPU pools and predictable tail latency. Private gives you the same runtime but in your network.
[02:05] Aisha Rahman: Can you give a concrete example of configuration that meets 250ms p95 for streaming per-token? What GPUs and model family?
[02:15] Ethan Park: Quick example: for an Llama-like 13B quantized (int8/4) model on A100 H100-class instances, with optimized kernels, we typically see p95 single-token decode around 60–120ms on a dedicated slot with KV cache warm, but that depends on sequence length. For a streaming setup with 3-token flush, end-to-end p95 for first token to client — network included — lands in your 200–350ms window if you provision capacity for your concurrency peaks and disable aggressive batching that adds latency.
[02:48] Roberto Silva: Hmm, that approx sounds good. What's the unit economics tradeoff if we over-provision Dedicated vs relying on Hosted burst?
[02:58] Maya Chen: Great Q. Hosted gives you on-demand elasticity — cheaper to start — but you have to accept some variability in p95 during global peaks. Dedicated has higher fixed monthly cost (reserved GPUs), but gives you predictable p95 and throughputs; Redwood Optimize can recommend quantization and batching to reduce per-token cost. We'll send a Dedicated pricing matrix.
[03:15] Claire Ng: For benchmarking, what's your recommended test? We want synthetic token/sec but also real prompt distribution.
[03:23] Ethan Park: Two-phase plan: 1) Synthetic token/sec spike tests to validate aggregate throughput and autoscaling logic (e.g., ramp to 6k tokens/sec sustained for 5 minutes, then 10k tokens/sec short spike) and measure p95 and p99; 2) Run representative prompt corpus with streaming enabled and measure per-client p95, payload jitter, and end-to-end user perceived latency. Include KV cache warm/cold scenarios. We'll share the scripts.
[03:48] Aisha Rahman: That sounds good. Also need to validate fallback behavior: if Dedicated saturates, can requests go to Hosted or smaller models?
[03:56] Ethan Park: Yes, we support smart routing and automatic fallback policies. You can route by policy to cheaper model variants or to Hosted as overflow. Note: overflow to Hosted will incur egress and possible higher latencies, so it's a graceful degradation strategy.
[04:12] Maya Chen: One gotcha: when you fall back to a smaller model you might see behavior/regression in outputs; Redwood's eval hooks can detect regressions pre-rollout.
[04:21] Claire Ng: On the KV cache — how does the K V cache warm-up behave for streaming? We rely on user conversation history, so long context matters.
[04:29] Ethan Park: KV cache is kept on the GPU and reused across tokens; warm cache improves per-token times dramatically. For long histories, you see increased memory pressure; we recommend pinning sessions on a slot for affinity or using prefix cache to reduce recomputation. We'll provide specific mem/caching numbers based on your model size.
[04:48] Roberto Silva: Security — we need SOC2 Type II, SSO/SAML, KMS encryption for keys, and audit logs to our Splunk; is that already supported?
[04:57] Maya Chen: Yes. Hosted has SOC2 and we offer audit logs and SAML SSO. Dedicated/Private give you additional network controls and options for customer-managed KMS/HSM for Private. We'll share the security pack.
[05:10] Aisha Rahman: Back to latency numbers — you referenced 60–120ms for single-token decode. Is that on H100? A100? We need to give procurement some numbers.
[05:18] Ethan Park: The 60–120ms is representative on H100 with optimized kernels; on A100 you might be in 80–160ms range depending on compilation. Quantization helps — int8 or llama-int4-like quant yields faster runs but requires eval to ensure quality meets your thresholds. We'll include model-specific benchmarks.
[05:36] Claire Ng: Are there impacts from continuous batching? If you batch aggressively to save cost, will it blow p95?
[05:42] Ethan Park: Yes — batching reduces per-token cost but adds latency. Redwood's serving runtime supports dynamic batching bounded by latency SLOs; you can set a batching window and max batch size. For strict 250ms p95 we typically recommend small batching windows (5–10ms) or disabling across-user batching for critical streams.
[05:59] Maya Chen: Quick practical question for your team: do you have a preferred GPU vendor or are you open? Also, any constraints on region/residency?
[06:07] Aisha Rahman: We'd prefer cloud-agnostic but likely start on GCP in us-west; some customers in EMEA will need EU data residency down the road.
[06:16] Roberto Silva: For networking — we'll need to test TLS termination and make sure session affinity works for streaming websockets, do you support that?
[06:25] Ethan Park: Yes, we support HTTP/2 streaming and websocket proxies; session affinity is supported in Dedicated and Private. We'll include recommended load balancer configs.
[06:34] Claire Ng: For the benchmark script, can you show us how to simulate 180 concurrent streams with per-token flushing every 3 tokens?
[06:40] Ethan Park: We'll share a script that opens N parallel streams, sends prompt then simulates token-by-token consumption with configurable flush sizes and artificial client-library latencies. The script will instrument p50/p95/p99 and tokens/sec.
[06:52] Aisha Rahman: Would Redwood help run the first benchmark on Dedicated if we sign an initial pilot? We might not be able to provision Dedicated immediately.
[06:59] Maya Chen: Yes — we offer a short pilot on Dedicated with trial capacity for qualified pilots; otherwise we can run a hosted spike with controlled load tests. We'll propose pilot terms in the follow-up email.
[07:10] Roberto Silva: One more: what's the expected warm-up time if we do autoscale from zero to handle a sudden 10k tokens/sec spike?
[07:18] Ethan Park: Autoscale depends on how you've configured capacity and burst pool. Cold scale from zero to full can take a few minutes depending on GPU provisioning; we recommend keeping a small warm pool for ultra-low tail latency. Dedicated reserved capacity avoids that ramp entirely.
[07:33] Claire Ng: For model quantization, do you support per-route quantization policies? Some routes need higher quality.
[07:40] Maya Chen: Yes, per-route or per-model policies are supported — you can pin high-quality models for sensitive routes and cheaper quantized models for lower-sensitivity ones.
[07:47] Aisha Rahman: Anything we should watch out for when we collect prompt corpus for benchmarking?
[07:52] Ethan Park: Make sure the corpus contains a mix of short conversational exchanges and long completions, mark which flows are streaming vs batch, include tokens-per-second distribution, and note average sequence length per flow. Also include failure scenarios (partial timeouts) so we can measure graceful degradation.
[08:09] Maya Chen: Recap — next steps: Maya sends pricing & security pack; Ethan sends benchmark scripts and a proposed spike plan; VelociData provides prompt corpus and confirms whether Private is needed. We'll set follow-up for a spike session.
[08:21] Aisha Rahman: Sounds good. Appreciate the clarity.
[08:24] (cross-talk / small interruptions as folks exchange closing logistics)
[08:33] Maya Chen: Thanks all. We'll email the artifacts and propose times for the technical spike.

End of meeting.

References & shared artifacts mentioned:
- Dedicated pricing matrix (to be attached in follow-up)
- Benchmark script (Redwood SE synthetic token/sec script) link: (will share in email)
- Security pack (SOC2, KMS options, SSO/SAML)

Notes on transcript quality: medium — some terms like "KV cache" transcribed as "K V cache", and Redwood Dedicated occasionally truncated to "Redwood dedicate".

Bench plan (short version included in transcript):
- Synthetic stress test: ramp to 6k tokens/sec for 5 minutes, spike to 10k tokens/sec for 30s, measure p50/p95/p99 and error rates.
- Representative run: use VelociData prompt corpus, streaming mode (3-token flush), simulate 180 concurrent sessions, measure per-client p95 and tokens/sec, cold vs warm KV cache.
- Environment: recommend run on Dedicated 8x A100 (or H100 if available) with prefix cache enabled. If starting Hosted, note higher variance in p95.

Deliverables promised:
- Pricing + security docs (Maya)
- Benchmark scripts + expected hardware matrix (Ethan)
- Follow-up meeting invite for spike (Maya)

title: Alpha Systems POC: Security & Performance Benchmark Scoping
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-05-20 08:43 UTC
Duration: 46 minutes
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Maya Singh (Redwood SE), Luis Ortega (Redwood SE), Ruth Delgado (Alpha CTO), Samir Patel (Alpha Lead ML Eng), Jodie Wang (Alpha Security Architect), Nora Kim (Alpha PM)

Automated summary (auto-generated, may be partial):
Alpha Systems wants to run a 2-week pilot to validate throughput/latency and security posture for a production inference integration. Key constraints: SOC 2/ISO evidence, SSO (SAML) integration, RBAC for data separation, audit logs retained 1 year (GDPR considerations), pilot must show p95 latency < 350ms for typical short prompts, cost target ~ $0.20 per 1k tokens for inference. Redwood to share security pack and coordinate benchmarks.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ethan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. we'll do a quick intro then jump into security and the bench plan. Is timing still good, Ruth?
[00:06] Ruth: Yeah, totally. We have about 45 minutes, thanks.
[00:10] Samir: quick note from our side — we want to validate both hosted and a VPC-connected pilot because of data residency concerns, and also want to see dedicated perf numbers.
[00:20] Maya: that makes sense. we typically run a benchmark with a representative workload — so if you can give us 2 prompt sets (short and long) and a session length distribution that helps. also, we'll need to align on authorizations and logging.
[00:36] Jodie: on security: SOC 2 is must-have, ISO nice-to-have. we also need SSO via SAML to integrate to our IDP, and RBAC controls so our teams don't see each other's debug logs. retention of audit logs is key — minimum 1 year, prefer 3 for some teams.
[00:58] Luis: noted. on the infra side we can run either hosted endpoints or a private deployment in your VPC. For benchmarks we can do throughput, p95/p99 latency, token/sec, and also cold-cache vs warm-cache runs to show KV cache effects.
[01:18] Nora: timeline question — how long would the pilot take? we'd like to be live-ish in June.
[01:24] Ethan: we can do a 2-week pilot once we have samples. week 1: integration + security review + smoke tests. week 2: full benchmark runs and QA. so if samples land by Friday we can run in the week of May 26.
[01:40] Ruth: ok that aligns.
[01:42] Samir: a couple of success criteria from our side: 1) p95 latency under 350ms for 1-2 sentence prompts (short), 2) cost per 1k tokens below $0.25, 3) ability to enforce RBAC and SAML SSO within pilot, 4) audit logs export to our SIEM.
[02:02] Maya: can you clarify the 350ms number — is that cold or warm?
[02:06] Samir: warm ideally. cold first token can be higher.
[02:10] Maya: fine — we'll show cold vs warm. For warm we can benchmark with KV cache primed and continuous sessions to emulate chat.
[02:20] Jodie: on audit logs — we need line-item logs for every request: user id, token usage, model id, route, timestamp, and that logs are forwarded to our SIEM via syslog or S3 push with encryption. retention controls ideally in the console or via retention policy. also we need proof that logs are tamper-evident.
[02:44] Ethan: we have audit log exports to S3 and API integrations — logs can be routed to your SIEM; retention policy is configurable. tamper evidence is supported via append-only write and KMS key control, and we can include SHA hashes if needed for attestation.
[03:00] Jodie: okay please include the exact S3 encryption + KMS flow in the security pack.
[03:06] Luis: for the network we propose a private connectivity mode: either VPC peering or a private link. in the pilot we can spin a 'private control plane' option which keeps customer metadata in your VPC and runs inference in dedicated hosts.
[03:22] Ruth: data residency: some PII will stay in our environment — so we need guarantees that any logs with PII are only in our S3 and not in shared Redwood buckets.
[03:32] Ethan: agree, we can set up dedicated buckets under your account and ensure the control plane only stores minimal metadata. we'll document what metadata persists on Redwood side.
[03:40] Samir: on workload sizing — our forecast for the pilot: 200 concurrent sessions during peak, average request size 600 tokens, long tails up to 2k tokens. target sustained throughput 50 qps and burst to 200 qps.
[03:58] Maya: that's helpful. for sizing we'll map to model choices — smaller models for cost-sensitive routes, larger ones for quality. we'll also show quantized variants to reduce memory footprint.
[04:12] Samir: want to confirm the model catalog you plan to test — any Redwood-verified models?
[04:17] Ethan: we'll include 3 variants: open-6.7Q (quantized), open-13B float, and a tuned 13B variant. we'll deliver per-model perf profiles and token cost estimates.
[04:30] Nora: do we need a full SOC 2 report to start the pilot or is a summary ok?
[04:35] Jodie: summary first, but we'll need Type II before production. for pilot a SOC 2 summary + evidence mapping works.
[04:44] Ethan: we'll push the SOC2 Type II summary and an ISO 27001 controls mapping in the security pack.
[04:50] Jodie: SSO integration: our IDP is Okta, we need SAML 2.0 based SSO and SCIM for user provisioning ideally. can Redwood support SCIM?
[04:58] Maya: yes, we support SAML for auth and SCIM for provisioning. role mapping for RBAC can be done via groups.
[05:06] Jodie: RBAC detail: we need at least 3 roles: admin, dev, observer. dev should be denied access to raw user data logs but allowed to see aggregated telemetry.
[05:18] Luis: we can apply route-level RBAC and token-level policies. also separate token scopes for embeddings vs generation to limit attack surface.
[05:26] Samir: how about encryption of data at rest — KMS?
[05:29] Ethan: KMS-backed encryption for buckets, and optional HSM for higher assurance. we'll include KMS flow diagrams.
[05:36] Nora: can we get an estimate of per-token cost for the pilot? rough numbers okay.
[05:40] Luis: rough: open-6.7Q ~ $0.08 per 1k tokens, open-13B ~ $0.20 per 1k, tuned-13B ~ $0.28 per 1k. those vary based on batching and cache hit rates.
[05:56] Samir: our target was $0.20 per 1k so the middle model looks ok.
[06:00] Maya: we'll run cost/cpu efficiency matrix during the benchmark. we'll test batching configs: small-latency (batch size 1-4) and high-throughput (batch size 16-64) and measure p95 and cost.
[06:16] Ruth: any limitations for the private pilot? e.g., GPU types?
[06:20] Luis: depends on region; for private deploys we can match A100 or H100 nodes, but quantized runs can often use L4-like hardware. we'll note hardware options in the runbook.
[06:34] Jodie: compliance: in addition to SOC/ISO we need a list of subprocessors and a DPA.
[06:40] Ethan: yes we'll send the DPA template and current subprocessors list.
[06:44] Samir: benchmarks — we need cold-cache and warm-cache numbers, multi-tenant interference tests (if hosted), and sustained 2-hour soak at peak to check stability.
[06:58] Maya: agreed; soak testing is important. we'll simulate 2-hour sustained traffic with your distribution and also do a 30-minute spike test.
[07:08] Nora: what about metrics and observability? we need per-route token counts, latency histograms, errors, and request tracing id to join logs.
[07:18] Luis: Redwood Console exposes per-route dashboards, token-level cost breakdown, and trace IDs. we can also stream metrics to your Datadog or Prometheus endpoint.
[07:28] Jodie: one more — can we get a read-only 'security observer' role for our auditors to access logs during the pilot?
[07:34] Ethan: yes, we can provision a read-only auditor role with restricted access to logs and reports.
[07:40] Samir: excellent. next, timeline recap?
[07:42] Ethan: recap: Samir uploads sample prompts by 2025-05-23; Ethan to send SOC2 summary + ISO mapping 2025-05-21; Luis to prepare bench runbook 2025-05-25; run week of 2025-05-26, with security deep-dive on 2025-05-27.
[07:58] Ruth: sounds good.
[08:00] Jodie: one risk — if pilot shows data leakage issues we need the ability to pause traffic and cut network. can you support emergency isolation?
[08:08] Luis: yes, we have kill-switch controls and network isolation procedures; we'll document RTO/RPO expectations.
[08:18] Ethan: any other questions?
[08:20] Nora: none from product.
[08:22] Samir: not right now.
[08:24] Ethan: great, we'll follow up with the security pack and the benchmark plan. thanks everyone.
[08:30] Meeting ends.

title: Contract review — internal SLAs, pricing committee & pilot gating
preview: Kickoff to align on MSA/DPA redlines, SLAs for inference latency, pricing approval path and pilot gating criteria. Action items: Redwood to share redline + security pack; Verdant to circulate pricing committee date and provide KMS/SSO requirements. Technical pilot window tentatively 6/02-6/16.

Action items: Redwood to share redline + security pack; Verdant to circulate pricing committee date and provide KMS/SSO requirements. Technical pilot window tentatively 6/02-6/16.

title: Org vs Project Key Routing and FairShare Demo - Harbor Dataworks
preview: Redwood AE + SE walked Harbor Dataworks through org-level and project-level API keys, fair-share rate limiting, concurrent request caps, and quota enforcement patterns. Discussion focused on how to map Harbor's teams to org/project keys, billing per-project, burst handling, and policy rollout strategies. Action items: pricing worksheet, policy examples, sample logs for a POC.

Discussion focused on how to map Harbor's teams to org/project keys, billing per-project, burst handling, and policy rollout strategies. title: Request Insights & Live Debugging — Console Demo
preview: AE + SE ran a Console-focused demo showing request-level tracing, error and latency analysis, and debugging flows for a low-latency health-data inference workload. Team identified optimization opportunities (batching config and prefix/KV cache) and agreed to a short benchmark. Security questions about VPC/private control plane, SSO/SAML and audit logs were raised; AE to share compliance docs. SE will reproduce a slow tail request and surface policy-level fallback options.

title: Reliability & Failover Discovery - Nimbus Health
preview: Discovery call focused on reliability, incident tolerance, and multi-region fallback strategies for Nimbus Health. Key asks: active-active vs active-passive routing, SLA expectations for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private, testing approach for failover, data residency and KMS concerns. Agreed next steps: share security pack, run a short failover sim, and exchange benchmark template.

title: Stream-first chat handoff & fallback strategy lab
preview: Intro demo focused on stream-first chat flow, parameterization for latency vs cost, and retry/backoff patterns. Customer has strict PHI considerations and wants a short POC to validate latency targets in their VPC. Action items and follow-ups agreed.
